E4-3_15

## Page 191

Our projects to protect biodiversity and ecosystems mainly involve the following stakeholders:
/ Environment: we involve organisations that represent it in the design of our strategies and implementation of our actions,
especially those focused on having a positive impact on the environment and biodiversity.
/ Suppliers: given that our impacts on biodiversity and ecosystems concentrates in our value chain, the commitment of all
actors involved is key to attaining our goals. In this regard, we work with our suppliers to incorporate best practices.
/ Communities: in keeping with our goal of contributing to the development of a fair and resilient society and a healthy
environment underpinning it, our programmes are aimed at preserving and restoring nature while respecting the rights
and well-being of local communities.
i More information in the MDR-P. Policies section of this Report.
Furthermore, no biodiversity offsets have been used in relation to our actions.
Agreements and collaborations
With the aim of promoting the conservation of ecosystems and biodiversity, we work with a number of specialist
organisations and coalitions such as Business for Nature, Open Planet Business for Biodiversity (OP2B), LEAF Coalition and
The Fashion Pact, among others.
In this regard, it is worth underscoring our adherence to Deforestation-Free Call to Action for Leather, led by Textile Exchange
and the Leather Working Group (LWG), which promotes supply chains that only use deforestation-free leather, and the
Pack4Good initiative driven by Canopy to transform the procurement of packaging materials.
i More information in the document Partnerships on Inditex’s corporate website.
C. Metrics and targets
E4-4. Targets related to biodiversity and ecosystems
Our commitment to biodiversity and ecosystems is evidenced by our intention of acting to protect, restore, regenerate or
otherwise improve biodiversity across five million hectares throughout our value chain and beyond.
Other related commitments, such as GHG emission reduction and climate neutrality targets, are also worth highlighting,
given the impact of climate change on natural ecosystems. In addition, the responsible management of natural resources
allows us to reduce our environmental impact. Consequently, in keeping with our Fibres Plan, we foster the use of lower-
impact fibres and materials18.
We have also set ourselves the target of a 25% reduction in water consumption in our supply chain by 2025, in line with our
Water Policy, as we are mindful of how important water is for biodiversity and ecosystems.
i More information in sections E1-4. Targets related to climate change, E3-3/E3-4. Water consumption and related
target and E5-3. Targets related to resource use and circular economy of this Report.
5.  Environmental information / E4. Biodiversity and ecosystems
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
191
18 See the Methodological Annex for more information on the criteria that fibres and materials must fulfil to be classified as lower-impact.

E4-3_10

E4-3_02

E4.MDR-T_01-13

## Page 192

• Protect, restore or regenerate five million hectares
Our key commitment in connection with biodiversity and ecosystems is to protect, restore, regenerate, or take other
management approaches for the improvement of biodiversity in an area of five million hectares.
This target is linked to our Sustainability Policy and, more specifically, to our Ecosystems and Biodiversity Policy. They both
establish our commitment to the protection of ecosystems and biodiversity —in turn aligned with our pledge to contribute to
the development of a fair and resilient society and a clean, healthy and sustainable environment that sustains it-.
Target: protect, restore or regenerate 5 million hectares by 2030
Current year
Target year
2024
2030
Hectares
758,534
5 million
To achieve this target, we collaborate with expert organisations such as the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWF) or
Conservation International in the various initiatives to regenerate and restore ecosystems detailed in this standard.
This target, along with the actions for achieving it, addresses the protection and improvement of biodiversity and
ecosystems, both within and beyond our value chain, as part of the ‘Restore & Regenerate’ category of SBTN’s action
framework, adapting the mitigation hierarchy. We defined this target being aware of the impacts of our activity on the
environment and our will to positively contribute to its recovery.
Furthermore, it is in line with the goals of the European Union Biodiversity Strategy for 2030 and the Kunming-Montreal
Global Biodiversity Framework.
i More information on the scope, methodology and calculation changes in the year in the Methodological Annex of this
Report.
E4-5. Metrics related to biodiversity and ecosystems change
As previously mentioned, our materiality assessment identified certain of our own facilities located near biodiversity-
sensitive areas. Nevertheless, we have not found any material negative impacts in those areas.
i More information in section IRO-1/SBM-3. Material biodiversity and ecosystem-related impacts, risks and
opportunities of this chapter.
However, we have identified potential impacts related to our value chain. Consequently, at Inditex we are in the process of
compiling new information to supplement the measurement of this impact in our reporting for subsequent years, in
accordance with the transitional provision in the CSRD, in ESRS 1, section 10.2.

## Page 193

E5. Resource use and circular economy
A. Impact, risk and opportunity management
IRO-1. Material resource use and circular economy-related impacts,
risks and opportunities
Identification of Impacts, Risks and Opportunities
The analysis of our activity, coupled with the materiality assessment and the views of our stakeholders, has allowed us to
compile a list of material impacts, risks and opportunities related to resource use and circular economy.
In the process we have included the assessment of all our activities and those of our value chain, considering the various
resource inflows and outflows at each stage, with the aim of identifying all the potential avenues of impact.
To understand the results of the exercise, it is important to take into account two considerations:
/ In general, purchases of raw materials and fabrics is not part of Inditex’s own operations. However, we do take part in the
selection of the raw materials used to manufacture our articles, as well as in the specifications of their characteristics.
Accordingly, when it comes to the use of resources, the associated impacts, risks and opportunities are mainly
concentrated on our value chain.
/ Waste management and outflow is generally focused within the scope of waste generated in our own operations.
Consultations with stakeholders
Stakeholder dialogue is a key element of our materiality analysis, as detailed previously in this Report. Specifically, in 2024
we involved specialised organisations such as the University of Leeds in our double materiality assessment.
As well as in the materiality assessment, during the course of our activity, we also collaborate with specialised organisations
that give us valuable knowledge regarding the impacts our activity might have on resource use and circular economy.
Among these, we highlight our involvement with standard-bearing organisations in circularity, in terms of both lower-impact
fibres and materials (Textile Exchange and Canopy, as well as many others) and packaging (Ellen MacArthur Foundation).
This continuous engagement also allows us to develop new targets and actions linked to the proper management of our
impacts, risks and opportunities.
5.  Environmental information / E5. Resource use and circular economy

(1) The extended value chain comprises the environment and the communities in which we are present even if they are not directly linked

i More information on stakeholder engagement and our materiality analysis can in sections SBM-3. Material impacts,
risks and opportunities and IROs. Disclosures on the materiality assessment process of this Report.
E5-1. Policies related to resource use and circular economy
Resource management is a key element for us. Consequently, our policies are interrelated to guide the adoption of a
business model that focuses on the use of lower-impact raw materials as opposed to conventional ones19, as well as
recyclability, durability and repairability, and proper waste management, to reduce the impact on society and the
environment.
Policies related to resource use and circular economy
/ Sustainability Policy i
/ Ecosystems and Biodiversity Policy i
/ Sustainability Stakeholder Relations Policy i
i More information on our policies and their updates in section MDR-P. Policies in this Report.
Towards lower-impact sourcing
Our Sustainability Policy enshrines our unwavering commitment to circularity, from the design of the products marketed
by the Group to considerations relating to their end of life, and improving resource and waste management throughout the
value chain. To do this, we apply the waste hierarchy, we seek to ensure that waste is reconverted into a new resource and
we move towards the creation of secondary raw materials as the essence of the circular economy to avoid the use of virgin
raw materials.
Our Ecosystems and Biodiversity Policy further develops those principles, laying the groundwork to promote best practice
in the sourcing and use of natural materials, prioritising the use of materials, inputs and products with a lower environmental
impact.
This ambition is enshrined in our Fibres Plan, which envisages the use of lower-impact textile fibres, as compared to
conventional ones, by 2030. To achieve this target, recycled and next-generation fibres play a key role.
5.  Environmental information / E5. Resource use and circular economy
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
194
19 More information on the criteria that fibres and materials must fulfil to be classified as lower-impact in the Methodological Annex of this
Report.

E5.MDR-P_01-06

E5-1_01;
E5-1_02

## Page 195

We are therefore committed to the responsible design of our garments and the careful selection of raw materials, which
allows us to source materials and textile fibres through lower-impact production systems.
In addition, our Sustainability Policy also includes the obligation that animal products come from animals treated
ethically and responsibly, in accordance with the internationally accepted ‘Five Freedoms’ of animal welfare.
i More information in section E5-3. Targets related to resource use and circular economy of this Report.
E5-2. Actions related to resource use and circular economy
Our sustainability strategy covers our aim to progress towards a circular economy model that transforms waste into
resources and that promotes lengthening the life of our products. Not only is circularity a transformative aspect in our
Company and industry, but it represents an opportunity to improve our long-term resilience and efficiency. That is why we
endeavour to integrate it at every level of our organisation, from design and production processes to managing our stores,
logistics and offices.
To achieve this, we are committed to innovation in materials, production processes and the use and end of life of our products.
We base this innovation on collaboration with universities, suppliers, startups, companies from different sectors and social
organisations.
Indeed, implementing these innovative projects and providing the sector with fibres and processes that have a lower
impact than traditional ones is one of the drivers of our circularity strategy.
Initiatives to progress towards a circular economy model
/ Sustainability Innovation Hub
Related IROs: [E5.I1; E5.I2; E5.R1; E5.O2]
In 2024 we continued to work on our Sustainability Innovation Hub (SIH), a centre for innovation launched in 2020 with an
action plan through 2030. This hub was designed to support the adoption and scaling of innovative fibre, material,
production process and traceability technologies in the textile and footwear sector. The aim is to minimise the
environmental impact associated with raw materials and production processes in the textile industry.
To achieve this, we join forces with startups, suppliers, academic institutions and technological centres in proposals that are
assessed based on their impact on social aspects, circularity, biodiversity and animal welfare, as well as being subjected to
a life-cycle environmental analysis. This assessment covers 16 impacts in areas such as emissions, water and land use.
This action is consistent with our Sustainability Policy, which fosters the use of lower-impact raw materials and processes,
and with our Fibres Plan target making 25% of our fibre to be Next Gen by 2030. This is one of the milestone to ensure that
100% of our textile fibres are lower-impact20 by that date.
In 2024 this platform encompass more than 300 emerging companies working to incorporate new materials, improve
production processes and make headway in connection with traceability, packaging and use and end of life. The Hub also
conducted the environmental, technical and commercial analysis of more than 20 new startups and completed more than
40 life cycle analyses.
5.  Environmental information / E5. Resource use and circular economy

## Page 196

Featured initiatives
/ Investments in capital:
• INFINITED FIBER: startup specialising in the recycling of cellulose materials and creator of Infinna™, a
recycled fibre made from cotton-rich textile waste.
• GALY: startup that has developed an innovative and unique process to grow cotton in vitro using stem
cell multiplication. We also have a Joint Development Agreement to optimise and perfect this technology,
strengthening our commitment to innovation in the textile sector.
• EPOCH: startup that uses artificial intelligence to design enzymes that allow the recycling of plastic and
textile materials. We have also signed a Joint Development Agreement.
This comes alongside the capital investment we already made in Circ, with a technology to enable the
recycling of garments made of more than one material.
/ Catalyst for pilot projects and demonstrations:
In 2024, we carried out pilot projects for more than 30 innovations and we have launched a collection with
CIRC x Zara, as the first milestone in our ongoing collaboration. Another striking milestone was the purchase
of the first 2,000 tonnes of Circulose®, an innovative textile pulp produced from cotton waste recycled using
a chemical process.
Lastly, we also have the research programme The Materials by Inditex, designed to put forward disruptive solutions for
fibres and materials on an industrial scale, in partnership with the foremost industry leaders such as BASF, YKK, Velcro and
Freudenberg, among others. Among the most noteworthy applications of this programme are Loopamid x Zara, an initiative
aimed at proving the viability of the first circular jacket, and Haptex x Tempe, the first synthetic leather designed to be
circular.
The financial resources earmarked for these actions are associated with Investment and Opex, which on their own do not
imply a material amount. They are included in Financial Investments and Operating Expenses and  (see Note 18 and Note 6
of the Consolidated Annual Accounts).
/ Cotton Strategy
Related IROs: [E5.I1; E5.R1; E5.O2]
One of the goals of our Fibre Plan is to ensure that, by 2030, 25% of the fibres we use are fibres from organic or
regenerative agriculture.
To make headway in this goal, in 2024 we launched the Cotton Strategy to help establish a supply of cotton that ensures
the well-being and resilience of farmers, communities and related ecosystems (financial, ecological and social), by fostering
agricultural best practices in connection with regenerative organic cotton. These agricultural best practices affect air and
soil health, biodiversity, water and people.
The main outcomes by 2030 we are pursuing with this strategy are:
/ Increase the visibility and transparency of cotton
growing practices, through solid systems that
ensure the integrity and traceability of the
certifications.
/ Promote the reduction of environmental impacts,
including GHG emissions and adverse effects on
water resources and biodiversity, by improving
agricultural soil health.
/ Promote the financial resilience of farmers and
respect for decent work in the countryside and the
community.
/ Improve the training provided to cotton suppliers,
fostering more responsible cotton production.
/ Encourage the development of mechanically
recycled cotton and its adoption in the supply
chain.

This action was carried out primarily by our people. The financial resources linked to the implementation of these actions
are associated with staff costs along with other external expenses deriving from the operations, which separately do not
represent a material amount. Both are included in Operating Expenses in the Consolidated Income Statement (see Note 6
of the Consolidated Annual Accounts).
Use, end of life and waste management
/ Zara Pre-Owned
Related IROs: [E5.I2; E5.I3; E5.R1; E5.O1]
At Inditex we want our products to accompany our customers for a long time. This is why we seek to help them extend the
life of their garments and to make our products durable, thereby contributing to a circular economy in our value chain, in
keeping with the commitment in our Sustainability Policy. With this goal in mind, we are working on actions that offer
innovative options and alternatives to maximise the useful life of our products.
One such initiative is Zara Pre-Owned, a platform we launched in 2022 to provide repair services and facilitate customer-to-
customer sales and donations. At present, in line with our aim of introducing circularity services in all the key markets by
2025, Zara Pre-Owned is available in Spain, Germany, Austria, Belgium, Croatia, Slovakia, Slovenia, the United States,
Finland, France, Greece, Ireland, Italy, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, Portugal and the United Kingdom, offering the
following services:
/ Repair of any used Zara garment from any season. Our customers can request services such as button
and zip replacements or seam repairs either online or in the Zara store of their choice. Our aim is to reach
all stores in the markets where the service is available.
/ Customer-to-customer sales so as anyone can buy and sell Zara garments from any season. The space
is organised by product category and provides detailed information on each item, including Zara's original
information on the garment and current images from the seller.
/ Donation: customers can request the collection of clothes from their home to be donated to charitable
entities. All collected items are delivered to local organisations that sort them for reuse where possible or
to be recycled if they have reached the end of their life cycle, supporting the development of projects in
local communities, as explained in the next section.
We have also adhered to the Refashion bonus scheme in France. This is an initiative under the French Anti-Waste for a
Circular Economy law (Loi AGEC) launched through Refashion. Its mission is to encourage French consumers to repair their
clothes and shoes, to promote the traceability of brands and to financially support organisations specialising in textile reuse
and recycling.
The bonus allows our customers to reduce the repair costs of clothing, promoting sustainability and extending the lifespan
of garments. It is financed by an Extended Producer Responsibility fund, which covers between 6 and 25 euros in certified
workshops, and Zara is already certified.
Zara Pre-Owned is a service hosted in Zara’s own website, so the funds linked to its launch were not significant. The
financial resources linked to implementing the associated actions are related to staff costs together with other external
operational costs, which separately do not represent a material amount. Both are included in Operating Expenses in the
Consolidated Income Statement (see Note 6 of the Consolidated Annual Accounts).
5.  Environmental information / E5. Resource use and circular economy

## Page 198

/ Clothing collection programme
Related IROs: [E5.I2; E5.I3; E5.R1; E5.O2]
The clothing collection programme allows our customers to donate items they no longer use to more than 91 local
community organisations in the markets where we operate.
This donation is made physically through containers located in our stores and, in certain markets such as Spain and the
United Kingdom or cities such as New York and Paris, we have made Zara.com home collection services available to our
customers. In Spain we also work with Caritas to enable the donation of garments by means of containers located in the
streets of various Spanish cities.
These organisations receive the donated garments and footwear from our facilities. The purpose of this programme is to
classify these articles in accordance with the waste hierarchy principle, in line with the commitment set out in our
Sustainability Policy. The organisations that receive the donations sort the garments, and those that are in good condition
are donated to vulnerable people or resold to fund their social projects. Products that cannot be reused are sent for
recycling.
Therefore, the clothing collection programme aims to mitigate the environmental impact of textile waste, mainly by
promoting reuse and recycling.
In 2024, our clothing collection programme recovered a total of 19,484 tonnes of garments and footwear (20,259 tonnes in
2023) that were donated to our partners in their entirety. To foster transparency in connection with these donations, our
partners report to us regularly regarding how the articles received are used. In 2024, they informed us that:
/ 57% of all garments were reused via donations to people in vulnerable situations or by reselling to finance community
projects.
/ The remaining 43%, which could not be reused due to their characteristics or condition, were sent to recycling projects
(mainly downcycling), or, as a last resort, were sent to energy recovery.
The financial resources earmarked for these actions are associated with staff costs and other external operating expenses,
which separately do not represent a material amount. Both are included in Operating Expenses in the Consolidated Income
Statement (see Note 6 of the Consolidated Annual Accounts).
/ Supply Chain Environmental Transformation Plan 2024-2027
Related IROs: [E1.I1; E1.I2; E1.R1; E2.I1; E2.I2; E2.R1; E3.I1; E3.I2; E3.R1; E5.I1; E5.I2; E5.I3; E5.R1; E5.O1; E5.O2]
Our Supply Chain Environmental Transformation Plan 2024-2027 also envisages actions linked to the use of resources and
circular economy in our supply chain.
Due to its transversal nature, the detailed description of the Plan, as well as the Plan's energy consumption and emission
reduction requirements are provided in chapter E1. Climate Change, information on wastewater treatment and chemicals is
provided in chapter E2. Pollution and information on water consumption is provided in chapter E3. Water resources. This
chapter includes information concerning waste management.
i More information on other related actions in sections E1-3. Actions related to climate change, E2-2. Actions related to
pollution and E3-2. Actions related to water resources of this Report.
• Waste management
At Inditex we want to promote optimal waste management, reducing waste generation, and encouraging reuse and
recycling, minimising the amount of waste sent to landfill, and fostering the circularity of textile waste.
Factories in our supply chain generate different types of waste, both hazardous and non-hazardous, which must be
properly handled, labelled and stored. The proper handling, control and management of hazardous waste is especially
important, to avoid leaks that spread hazardous substances to the environment.
Our Green to Wear (GtW) standard includes requirements on proper waste management. This means that the waste must
be sorted, properly labelled and stored with adequate ventilation and leakage prevention, especially in the case of
hazardous waste.
5.  Environmental information / E5. Resource use and circular economy

In order to achieve the goal of proper hazardous waste management, the facility must have a contract in force with an
authorised waste manager, a properly covered and updated hazardous waste inventory, a record of deliveries to the
authorised waste manager, and a separate storage area where waste can be properly managed.
i More information on other related actions in sections E1-3. Actions related to climate change, E2-2. Actions related to
pollution and E3-2. Actions related to water resources of this Report.
Agreements and collaborations
At Inditex we want to make headway in the use of recycled materials, which are not dependent on the extraction of virgin
raw materials and help reduce waste generation. For this reason, we establish collaborations with organisations like Textile
Exchange, OCA, Global Fashion Agenda and Fashion for Good in initiatives like the availability of lower-impact raw
materials, design geared to recyclability, the assessment of infrastructure necessary to process waste or the development
of new forms of textile recycling.
We are part of ReHubs Europe, a non-profit association of relevant companies and organisation in the European textile and
footwear value chain. ReHubs works to expand the current capacities for collection, sorting, preparation for recycling and
final recycling of textile waste in Europe to an industrial scale. We are also cofounders of the Association for the
Management of Textile and Footwear Waste (AGRTC) and, in 2024, we worked, in line with the activities initiated in 2023, to
form the voluntary Collective System of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) for the textile and footwear waste stream,
Re-Viste, which was launched in October 2024. The AGRTC is awaiting a licence for Re-Viste to start operating as a EPR on
a voluntary basis during 2025.
i More information in the document Partnerships on Inditex’s corporate website.
C. Metrics and targets
E5-3. Targets related to resource use and circular economy
So as to move towards a more circular business model that encompasses every phase of our activity, we have set
ourselves various targets, ranging from the sourcing of raw materials (promoting the use of lower-impact raw materials, in
line with our Fibre Plan) right through the use and end of life of our products, by providing circularity services.
These targets are closely related to our Sustainability Policy, in which we are committed to circularity, a core aspect in our
progress towards the decarbonisation of our value chain.
• Fibres Plan: use of lower-environmental-impact fibres
One of the main commitments in terms of resource use is our Fibres Plan, which is aimed at 100% of the textile fibres used
in our products by 2030 having a lower environmental impact as compared with conventional fibres.
This target is related to the commitment to prioritising sourcing raw materials, supplies and products that avoid and reduce
negative impacts on ecosystems throughout their life cycle, as set out in our Sustainability Policy and Ecosystems and
Biodiversity Policy.
5.  Environmental information / E5. Resource use and circular economy

We aim to encourage the design of products that last over time and can be recycled at the end of their useful life. With this
in mind, we train our designers and sales teams in sustainability criteria and foster those materials with a lower-impact on

## Page 201

• Provide circularity services such as Zara Pre-Owned in key markets
At Inditex, we want to help our customers extend the useful life of their garments, so our target is to provide circularity
services such as Zara Pre-Owned in key markets in 2025. With the addition of the United States in 2024, the programme is
now under development in 17 markets.
This target is linked to the commitment enshrined in our Sustainability Policy to resource efficiency, in both the products we
sell and our headquarters, stores and logistics centres.
i More information on the scope and calculation methodology in the Methodological  Annex of this Report.
By providing circularity services, we foster the reuse of products, extending their useful life through repair, resale between
third parties or donation to community organisations. These organisations sort the garments by their condition and quality,
allocating them to the best possible purpose, such as donation to people at risk of exclusion or sale in second-hand shops.
These organisations also support the implementation of social projects in their markets.
In cases where a product is not reusable, it is recycled, aiming to limit the amount of waste generated and repurposing the
raw materials obtained to make new garments. Considering the tiers of the waste hierarchy —Prevent, Reduce, Reuse,
Recycle, Recover, Dispose— this target corresponds primarily to ‘Reuse’ and ‘Recycle’.
At present, manufacturing new garments from recycled used clothing is one of the challenges facing the sector. That is why
we endeavour to foster textile innovation and the development of used clothing recovery and management systems.
E5-4. Resource inflows
The design of our products and selection of raw materials are factors that shape the impact of our products. Accordingly, a
fundamental aspect of our sustainability strategy is the commitment to use lower-impact fibres, enshrined in our Fibres
Plan.
i More information in section E5-3. Targets related to resource use and circular economy of this Report.
In 2024, we consumed 678,596 tonnes of raw materials used in our products (645,623 tonnes in 2023), which, by type, were
broken down between 88% fibres and 12% non-fibres. With regard to the category of fibres, 53% were natural fibres21, 37%
synthetic22 and 10% artificial23.

## Page 202

With regard to the use of recycled materials, in 2024, 33% of all fibres and other raw materials used in our products are
recycled, coming from both conventional and next-gen recycling of post-consumer waste (18% recycled materials in 2023).
These materials are designed to ensure the technical and durability functions required in certain products, thereby
contributing to sustainability and reducing the environmental impact in our supply chain.
In addition to the raw materials used directly to make our articles, we also use different materials in the packaging of our
products. In 2024 we estimated that 521,448 tonnes materials were used for this purpose (84% organic packaging and 16%
technical packaging). Furthermore, we continue to improve our corporate systems to report the recycled content of our
packaging with the required detail.
Moreover, manufacturing processes also require other resources, such as chemicals and water, at certain facilities along
the supply chain. At Inditex we have a series of policies and standards that determine our action principles on this topic, and
that provide guidelines to prevent the emission of harmful substances in the various stages of the value chain and to
promote a more efficient use of water.

i More information on water resources used in sections E2-1. Policies related to pollution and E3-3/E3-4. Water
consumption and related target of this Report.
The Methodological Annex of this Report contains a detailed description of the methodology employed to calculate the raw
materials used in the manufacture and packaging of our products.
i More information in the Methodological Annex of this Report.
E5-5. Resource outflows
At Inditex, our aim is to offer our customers an affordable, inspiring, high-quality and responsibly produced fashion
proposal.
We also intend to advance towards a circular economy model that permeates every level of our business, from the design
and production processes to managing our stores, logistics and offices.
As discussed throughout this chapter, we have numerous initiatives in place to promote the use of lower-impact raw
materials and to encourage mechanical and next-generation recycling. In section E5-4. Resource inflows, we report the
information concerning the raw materials used in our products, as well as the content of recycled materials used in 2024.
i More information in sections E5-2. Actions related to resource use and circular economy, E5-4. Resource inflows and
in the Methodological Annex of this Report.
With regard to the percentage of our products that are recyclable, at present there is no standard methodology in the
industry to calculate this information on a comparable way. Consequently, we are working with other important players to
be able to report this information in the future.
We are also working to address the lack of standardisation of potential methodological frameworks for measuring and
defining the durability and repairability of products. We are currently involved, as members of the Technical Secretariat, with
other major industry players, in developing the European Commission’s Product Environmental Footprint (PEF) , a new,
Europe-wide framework to gauge the environmental impact and provide comparable and harmonised information.
In turn, our commitment to reducing our products impact also includes those items that accompany our items, such as
packaging. Accordingly, at Inditex we have chosen to eliminate single-use plastics that reach customers in the packaging
containing our products. Our commitment in this sphere implies a continuous effort in innovation to avoid using single-use
plastics in new product lines, at our suppliers and in other Group operations.
5.  Environmental information / E5. Resource use and circular economy

## Page 203

We also have the #BRINGYOUROWNBAG (#TRAETUBOLSA) initiative, aimed at raising our customers’ awareness
regarding the importance of choosing reusable alternatives, reducing waste and minimising the consumption of raw
materials, water and energy. This initiative, currently present in 79 markets, has reduced the number of bags and envelopes
handed out in our stores by 48%. The proceeds from charging for bags and envelopes made from recycled paper in our
stores and earmarked for environmental projects since 2021 amount to 120.5 million euros. At the end of the financial year,
we allocated 67 million euros from those proceeds24 to projects in 31 countries.
i More information on the projects to which the proceeds of  #BRINGYOUROWNBAG have been donated in section
E4-3. Actions related to biodiversity and ecosystems of this Report.
In addition, we promote the collection of packaging materials after use for reuse or recycling. With the aim of going one
step further, we are rolling out a service allowing our Zara online customers to choose whether they wish to receive their
orders in reused boxes. The service is active in various geographic areas in Spain and we are working to expand its scope
into new markets forthwith. In this regard, we are working on improving our systems to report greater detail on these
aspects, such as the proportion of recyclable items, in future years.
Waste management
We work on the properly management and separation of the waste generated by our activity. We see this waste as useful
resources that can be repurposed or recycled, maximising its value and improving our impact.
This approach is not confined to properly sorting materials for processing and recycling, but includes rethinking our
processes to reduce waste generation at every stage: design, logistics, store and end of life.
In this regard, we have 15 TRUE certifications, processed by Green Business Certification Inc. for our headquarters, logistics
centres and own factories —nine Platinum and six Gold certifications—. TRUE certification identifies ‘environmentally
responsible spaces that have achieved 90% or greater overall diversion from landfill or incineration’.

Thus, in 2024, 9% of the waste generated at our headquarters, logistics centres and own factories will have been diverted
for operations other than recycling, representing 5,600 tonnes.
We continue to make every effort to ensure the traceability of waste in the most challenging cases, such as in our stores
located in shopping centres that combine the management of all waste.
In this connection, we continue to work to integrate in our systems the available information on the volume of waste
generated at our stores. Based on the data available at the reporting date, we have estimated that in 2024 around 116,965
tonnes of waste was generated through the Group’s own stores.

## Page 205

Disclosure pursuant to article 8 of
Regulation (EU) 2020/852 (Taxonomy
Regulation)
Regulatory context
The Inditex Group is subject to Regulation (EU) 2020/852 on EU Taxonomy (hereinafter ‘Taxonomy’), which stipulates a
series of obligations with regard to the disclosure of information relating to ‘environmentally sustainable’ activities. Since
2021, we have been reporting information concerning the Taxonomy in accordance with the applicable regulations, the
scope of which has increased since that date with the publication of Delegated Regulation (EU) 2021/2139 (the Climate
Delegated Act), Delegated Regulation (EU) 2021/2178 (the Disclosures Delegated Act) and Delegated Regulation (EU)
2023/2486 (the Environmental Delegated Act).
Companies remain exposed to the need to adapt to new requirements and periodically review their internal activities and
management systems, due to the continuous development of new legislation and guidelines in the context of the
Taxonomy, in order to ensure that the information provided is comprehensive and adequately compliant with the regulation.
In this context, it is confirmed that for now the Group’s main activity ― the distribution and sale of fashion and household
products―is not currently included in the applicable regulatory framework. Nevertheless, there are other activities linked to
the Group’s business model that are included, as outlined below.
Scope and assessment of Inditex’s activities
The scope of the information on Taxonomy includes all the companies over which the Inditex Group has control or joint
control (listed in Annex I of the Consolidated Annual Accounts). Within this scope, the Group has conducted an analysis of
its accounting information in order to determine which activities are associated with those described in the Taxonomy in
relation to each of the six targets.
Methodology and results
To assess the contribution to each of the objectives established by the Taxonomy, we applied the following methodology:
1)
Eligibility analysis by type of activity: analysing the accounting items linked to the three key indicators
and how they match the descriptions of the Taxonomy activities included in Annexes I and II of the
Climate Delegated Act and I to IV of the Environmental Delegated Act.
2)
Analysis of alignment by activity type: we assessed, for all the activities considered eligible, compliance
with the established criteria:
▪Technical screening criteria for substantial contribution.
▪The criteria of 'Do No Significant Harm' (DNSH) to the rest of environmental objectives.
▪Compliance with minimum social guarantees.
3)
Calculating the indicators and results: considering the eligible activities aligned with the Taxonomy in
accordance with the conclusions drawn from points 1 and 2.
5. Environmental information / Disclosure pursuant to article 8 of Regulation (EU) 2020/852 (Taxonomy Regulation)
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
205

## Page 206

1. Eligibility analysis of the activities carried out by Inditex
As a result of the assessment of its accounting information, eligible activities were identified within the framework of the
climate change mitigation (CCM), climate change adaptation (CCA) and circular economy (CE) objectives.
These activities may be encompassed in four main groups according to the activity type considered by Inditex: store
refurbishments and openings, construction and renovation of corporate headquarters, the Group’s logistics centres
and other own facilities, and generation of electricity from wind and photovoltaic energy.
The main conclusions of this analysis in connection with the three key indicators (Turnover, CapEx and OpEx) are presented
below.
Turnover: Inditex is mainly dedicated to the distribution and sale of fashion items, and this activity is its main source of
revenue. Since this activity is not currently included in the Taxonomy, no turnover items have been identified that could be
considered eligible.
OpEx: in keeping with the definition of this indicator provided in Taxonomy, the percentage of eligible OpEx of the Inditex
Group for 2024 has been considered to be approximately 0%.
CapEx: a core portion of the Group’s CapEx corresponds to investments undertaken in relation with store refurbishments
and openings. However, in most cases these actions are linked to the design and refurbishment of retail spaces, mainly
leased premises, which only exceptionally involve the building’s envelope or insulation (which is what has the capacity to
influence its energy efficiency). This means that only in these exceptional cases are the Group’s activities considered to be
eligible in accordance with the description of the ‘renovation of existing buildings’ activity corresponding to the activity
codes 7.2. CCM/CCA, for the climate change mitigation and adaptation objective, and 3.2. CE, for the circular economy
objective. In this regard, it is worth noting that both activities describe the activity of renovation of existing buildings
identically.
Another significant part of CapEx refers to the construction and refurbishment of the Group’s corporate headquarters,
logistics centres and other own facilities. In this case, with regard to the eligibility of the ‘acquisition and ownership of
buildings’ activity corresponding to activity code 7.7. CCM/CCA, for the climate change mitigation and adaptation
objectives. Activities with the capacity to influence the energy efficiency of the buildings analysed have been considered to
be eligible.
The Company also carries out investments to foster self-consumption through the generation of electricity using
photovoltaic and wind power at its own facilities. These investments are considered to be eligible in the framework of
activity ‘electricity generation from wind power’ 4.3. CCM/CCA, in connection with both climate change mitigation and
adaptation and the ‘installation, maintenance and repair of renewable energy technologies’, 7.6. CCM/CCA, in connection
with mitigation and adaptation.
Furthermore, the Inditex Group undertakes other eligible investments. They are related, among others, with the installation,
maintenance and repair of energy efficiency equipment in stores, headquarters and logistics centres, software
development and data processing, and the implementation and operation of systems that supervise and optimise energy
consumption to boost efficiency, which could be considered to be eligible in the framework of activities 7.3. CCM/CCA
(installation, maintenance and repair of energy efficiency equipment), 7.5. CCM/CCA (installation, maintenance and repair of
instruments and devices used to measure, regulate and control energy efficiency in buildings) and 8.1. CCM/CCA (data
processing, hosting and related activities), respectively. However, these activities, which represent a inmaterial proportion of
approximately 0% of the Group’s CapEx in 2024, would not meet the technical screening criteria and are not therefore
Taxonomy-aligned.
Other taxonomic activities in which the Group is investing were also identified. These notably include: Activity 5.3. CE
(preparation for re-use of end-of-life products and product components), activity 5.4. CE (sale of second-hand goods) for
the circular economy objective. However, these activities, which also represent a inmaterial proportion of approximately 0%
of the Group’s CapEx in 2024, would not meet the technical screening criteria and are therefore not Taxonomy-aligned.
5. Environmental information / Disclosure pursuant to article 8 of Regulation (EU) 2020/852 (Taxonomy Regulation)

2. Analysis of the alignment of activities by type of activity
For each of the eligible activities previously described, an assessment was carried out of the technical criteria in the climate
change mitigation and adaptation objectives, specifically for CapEx. As a result, it was observed that the Group’s activities
contribute solely to the mitigation objective. This is because the actions identified in relation to the taxonomic activities
mentioned are not carried out with the purpose of being ‘adaptation solutions’ or ‘transition towards a circular economy’ in
keeping with the requirements for these activities pursuant to the criterion of substantial contribution to these objectives.
Accordingly, below is a detailed breakdown of the analysis of compliance with the technical screening criteria for the
specific activities relating to the climate change mitigation objective (CCM):
/ Renovation of existing buildings (activity 7.2. CCM), which is reported as CapEx in accordance with point
1.1.2.2.a) of the Disclosures Delegated Act, explains the assessment of compliance with the technical criteria
relating to substantial contribution and DNSH specific to this activity;
/ Acquisition and ownership of buildings (activity 7.7. CCM), which is reported as CapEx and investment plan
in accordance with points 1.1.2.2.a) and 1.1.2.2. b) of the Disclosures Delegated Act, respectively, as well as
explaining the assessment of compliance with the technical criteria and DNSH applicable in the first case,
and, for the second case, explaining the criteria considered in the investment plan;
/ Electricity generation from wind power (activity 4.3. CCM), reported via the CapEx Plan, in line with the
applicable criteria.
/ Installation, maintenance and repair of renewable energy technologies (activity 7.6. CCM), which is
reported in point 1.1.2.2.a) of the Disclosures Delegated Act, explains the assessment of compliance with the
technical criteria relating to substantial contribution and DNSH specific to this activity;
/ Analysis of compliance with DNSH criteria for the climate change adaptation objective and for the
minimum social safeguards, assessed jointly for the various groups of activities.
/ Renovation of existing buildings (Activity 7.2. CCM)
In 2024, several actions related to the comprehensive refurbishment of three of the Group's stores were identified as being
aligned with the Taxonomy under activity 7.2. CCM, as explained below:
5. Environmental information / Disclosure pursuant to article 8 of Regulation (EU) 2020/852 (Taxonomy Regulation)
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
207

## Page 208

i. Compliance with the technical screening criteria for substantial contribution
With respect to the substantial contribution to the climate change mitigation objective, through the review of the works
technical projects, it was observed that the action identified in this context implies the intervention on the building’s facade
and, accordingly, on at least 25% of its envelope, in keeping with the Taxonomy’s requirements.
ii. Compliance with the technical screening criteria of ‘Do No Significant Harm’ (DNSH) specific to the activity
• Climate change adaptation: no material physical climate-related risks were identified that might affect the performance
of the reported activity pursuant to the provisions of Annex A of the Climate Delegated Act. The detailed breakdown of
compliance with this technical criterion is provided in the transversal section ‘Compliance with the technical screening
criterion of Do No Significant Harm (DNSH) to the climate change adaptation objective’.
• Sustainable use and protection of water and marine resources: the buildings analysed have been refurbished entirely,
including sanitary fittings such as taps, basins and toilets that meet the minimum requirements as regards maximum
water flow and maximum flush volume established by the Taxonomy. It is compliant with the technical guidelines followed
by the Inditex Group in connection with plumbing and sanitation in the works it undertakes, which include caps on water
flows in the plumbing systems of sanitary facilities in line with the Taxonomy’s requirements, promoting a more conscious
use of water resources.
• Transition towards a circular economy: the actions identified are compliant with the requirements of the Taxonomy,
including that at least 70% of non-hazardous construction and demolition waste generated in the work should be
recovered. This compliance is reflected in the store's Construction and Demolition Waste Management Plan, which
includes building practices that support circularity; in addition to the construction waste management certificates, which
confirm that 100% of the waste generated is recovered.
• Prevention and control of pollution: the construction components and materials used are compliant with the
Taxonomy’s requirements as per the record of the relevant affidavits and emission test. This includes the requirements
linked to substances in Appendix C of the Climate Delegated Act, formaldehyde and volatile organic components (VOCs).
In addition, the building complies with the Group's technical guidelines on indoor air quality and noise, which meet the
Taxonomy requirements.
/ Acquisition and ownership of buildings (Activity 7.7. CCM)
(a) Investments in corporate headquarters
With regard to investments in headquarters, we continued to invest in the construction of a new corporate building, which
will be a part of the complex of buildings that make up the Group's Arteixo headquarters. This action is presented by means
of an investment plan, approved by Inditex Management, which details all the measures in place to ensure that these
investments match the Taxonomy requirements within the framework of the climate change mitigation objective through
activity 7.7.
Compliance with the technical screening criteria by means of the investment plan
With regard to the new corporate building, in accordance with the provisions of the Disclosures Delegated Act, it is possible
to present aligned activities by means of the investment plan that evidences that the investment is earmarked for an activity
that will be Taxonomy-aligned within a maximum period of five years. Such a plan must meet a series of requirements as
per the provisions of the Disclosures Delegated Act and the guidelines in the FAQ documents published in 2022 by the
European Commission26.
The plan concerning the corporate building includes a detailed set of measures —with their related expense, status of
progress of implementation dates— that must be carried out to ensure that the action complies with the technical screening
criteria of activity 7.7. CCM. More specifically, the document details the measures to be adopted for compliance with the
requirements concerning energy efficiency27, Life Cycle Analysis28 and the Study of Air Tightness and Thermal Integrity of
the Envelope29 applicable to large non-residential buildings built from 2020 onwards. Meanwhile, the detailed breakdown of
compliance with DNSH is provided in the section ‘Compliance with the technical screening criterion of Do No Significant
Harm (DNSH) to the climate change adaptation objective’.
5. Environmental information / Disclosure pursuant to article 8 of Regulation (EU) 2020/852 (Taxonomy Regulation)

Construction of this project is scheduled for completion in 2025. Once the execution has been completed, the Group
undertakes to periodically assess compliance with the relevant technical criteria in this context and to conduct the related
adjustments to its calculations and disclosures where necessary.
(b) Investments in logistics centres, Group-owned adjacent buildings and other Group-owned facilities
This year investments were undertaken at logistics centres, adjacent buildings and other Group-owned facilities
(corresponding to a new Group-owned factory), that are considered to be aligned with the Taxonomy under activity 7.7.
CCM, as explained below:
i. Compliance with the technical screening criteria for substantial contribution
The aforementioned buildings, built after 2020, present non-renewable primary energy consumption of at least 10% below
the energy consumption thresholds established for non-residential buildings in accordance with the Basic Document HE,
Energy Savings (‘DB HE Ahorro de energía’).
The centres cover a surface area of more than 5,000m2 and during their construction were subject to robust and traceable
quality control processes. They are efficiently operated through the continuous oversight and assessment of their energy
performance as provided in the Taxonomy for this purpose. Moreover, the Life Cycle Analysis report of these buildings
states that the Global Warming Potential (GWP) resulting from their construction was calculated at each stage of their life
cycle in accordance with the relevant standards. Inditex formally undertakes to disclose this GWP to investors and/or
customers who so request.
ii. Compliance with the technical screening criteria of ‘Do No Significant Harm’ (DNSH) specific to the activity
• Climate change adaptation: no material physical climate-related risks were identified that might affect the performance
of the reported activity pursuant to the provisions of Annex A of the Climate Delegated Act. The detailed breakdown of
compliance with this technical criterion is provided in the transversal section ‘Compliance with the technical screening
criterion of Do No Significant Harm (DNSH) to the climate change adaptation objective’.
/ Electricity generation from wind power (Activity 4.3. CCM)
With regard to activity 4.3. CCM, one wind-generated electricity project was identified: the Punta Langosteira Outer Port Wind
Facility. This project is aimed at achieving self-consumption between the Inditex Arteixo headquarters and the A Coruña Port
Authority, and it will enter into operation in early 2027, so it is reported by means of an investment plan.
Compliance with the technical screening criteria by means of the investment plan
As previously explained in connection with the requirements pertaining to the investment plan for the Punta Langosteira
Outer Port Wind Facility, measures are envisaged to ensure that the infrastructure is compliant with the technical screening
criteria applicable to activity 4.3. CCM. Specifically, the purpose of electricity generation from wind power is included in the
Technical Construction Report, which shows that the purpose of this facility is to generate wind power for self-consumption.
Furthermore, an Environmental Impact Study is planned as is the compilation of a document outlining the availability of
highly durable, recyclable and easily dismantable equipment and parts for the installation, so as to comply with the
applicable biodiversity and circular economy DNSHs, respectively. In turn, the detailed breakdown of compliance with
DNSH is provided in the section ‘Compliance with the technical screening criterion of Do No Significant Harm (DNSH) to the
climate change adaptation objective’
Construction of this project is scheduled for completion in 2027. Once the execution has been completed, the Group
undertakes to periodically assess compliance with the relevant technical criteria in this context and to conduct the related
adjustments to its calculations and disclosures where necessary.
/ Installation, maintenance and repair of renewable energy technologies (Activity 7.6. CCM)
With regard to activity 7.6. CCM, three photovoltaic projects have been identified at our Laracha logistics centres (expansion of
existing facility) and Zaragoza. These projects are intended for self-consumption and are expected to come on stream in 2025.
i. Compliance with the technical screening criteria for substantial contribution
With regard to the substantial contribution to the objective of climate change mitigation, by means of review of the facility’s
technical projects, it was found that the activity consists of the installation, maintenance and repair of solar photovoltaic
systems and auxiliary technical equipment on site as technical installations pertaining to these logistics centres.
ii. Compliance with the technical screening criteria of ‘Do No Significant Harm’ (DNSH) specific to the activity
• Climate change adaptation: no material physical climate-related risks were identified that might affect the performance
of the reported activity pursuant to the provisions of Annex A of the Climate Delegated Act. The detailed breakdown of
compliance with this technical criterion is provided in the transversal section ‘Compliance with the technical screening
criterion of Do No Significant Harm (DNSH) to the climate change adaptation objective’.
5. Environmental information / Disclosure pursuant to article 8 of Regulation (EU) 2020/852 (Taxonomy Regulation)
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
209

## Page 210

/ Analysis of compliance with the climate change adaptation DNSH and minimum social safeguards
Compliance with the technical screening criteria of ‘Do No Significant Harm’ (DNSH) to the climate change adaptation
objective
The Inditex Group has analysed the potential impacts in connection with climate change risks, both physical and transition
risks, using five emissions pathways aligned with the socioeconomic forecasts of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate
Change (IPCC's Shared Socioeconomic Pathways - SSPs), and considering short (0-5 years), medium (5-10 years) and long
(more than 10 years) time frames. For physical risks, the Group has considered seven sources of risk that are relevant to
Inditex's business. After identifying and assessing the potential impacts deriving from the sources of climate-related risks
(including the most significant ones associated with a disruption of operations at key facilities, damages to physical assets
or disruptions to the supply of raw materials), no material risks were detected that affect the reported Taxonomy activities.
Furthermore, the Group has defined adaptation measures aimed at reducing its exposure to risks, thereby adapting to the
consequences of climate change.
i More information on the assessment of climate-related risks in section SBM-3. Material impacts, risks and
opportunities of this Report.
Compliance with the criteria for minimum social safeguards
For any economic activity to be considered to be aligned, it must be carried out in accordance with the OECD Guidelines
for Multinational Enterprises and the United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights.
The Group applies to its operations and business decisions an approach based on the development of due diligence
processes, which enables the Group to identify, prevent, mitigate and remedy, if applicable, the impacts on the economy,
the environment and people (including those related to human rights). In this regard, the Group's Human Rights Strategy is
consistent with the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights and based on three pillars for integrating the
promotion of and respect for human rights throughout the value chain: the Group’s Human Rights Policy, due diligence and
grievance mechanisms. There are also policies and procedures in connection with competition, anti-corruption, responsible
business, conflicts of interest and fiscal responsibility. It is worth highlighting the Code of Conduct, aimed at ensuring a
professional, ethical and responsible commitment from the Group and everyone belonging to it in the course of their
activities, as a basic element of the business culture.
i More information in sections G1. Business conduct and GOV-4. Statement on due diligence  of this Report.
3. Calculation of indicators and results
Having assessed the eligibility and alignment of the activities identified, the corresponding indicators (Turnover, CapEx, and
OpEx) were calculated for each of them. The process of calculating these key performance indicators involves analysing
the Group’s consolidated information, excluding intercompany transactions, bearing in mind the adjustments made in the
consolidation process, using a single source of information to avoid considering the same item twice, and checking the
traceability and accuracy of the information.
These indicators represent the proportion of the Group’s turnover, investments in assets and operating expenditure, that
adheres to, whether in the form of eligibility or alignment, the requirements outlined in the Taxonomy. To construct the
numerators of the three key performance indicators, the corresponding items identified as eligible or aligned as per the
Taxonomy are considered. With regard to the denominators:
• Turnover: the denominator includes the ordinary revenue in the year. Revenue from Group companies, grants or gifts,
among others, are not taken into account. Specifically, the figure is taken from the information on sales turnover reported
in Inditex’s Consolidated Annual Accounts (Note 4).
• CapEx: the denominator includes additions to assets, including those derived from business combinations and rights of
use. Therefore, the Group considers all additions to property, plant and equipment, intangible assets and rights of use in
accordance with IFRS 16. Specifically, the figure is taken from the information on additions reported in Inditex’s
Consolidated Annual Accounts (Notes 14, 15 and 16).
• OpEx: the denominator includes the expenses associated with research and development, building renovation measures,
short-term leases and maintenance or repairs that ensure the proper functioning of the assets. Specifically, the figure is
taken from the information on operational expenses reported in Inditex’s Consolidated Annual Accounts (Note 6).
5. Environmental information / Disclosure pursuant to article 8 of Regulation (EU) 2020/852 (Taxonomy Regulation)
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
210

## Page 211

The results obtained for each of these key performance indicators are shown below, alongside the respective conclusions.
Turnover
In keeping with the previous information and as in previous years, no items of turnover have been identified that could be
considered eligible as per the current Taxonomy framework. This means that the eligible turnover results in 2024 are
approximately 0%.
CapEx
The Inditex Group considers eligible items30 linked to the following investment types within the framework of the mitigation
and adaptation objectives:
• Renovation of existing buildings (Activity 7.2. CCM)
• Acquisition and ownership of buildings (Activity 7.7. CCM)
• Electricity generation from wind power (Activity 4.3. CCM)
• Installation, maintenance and repair of renewable energy technologies (Activity 7.6. CCM)
The percentage investment in environmentally sustainable activities in 2024 (7.8%) increased with respect to that reported in
2023 (3.4%). The difference between the two results lies mainly in the inclusion of the items linked to the construction of
logistics centres.
There are no Taxonomy-eligible activities that are not environmentally sustainable thanks to the Group’s efforts to put in
place measures to ensure current and future alignment of actions related to the acquisition and ownership of buildings
(activity 7.7. CCM) with the Taxonomy’s technical criteria.
OpEx
Consistent with the foregoing, the criterion for calculating OpEx is unchanged with respect to the previous year.
Consequently, eligible OpEx is observed to be approximately 0% of the Inditex Group’s total OpEx in 2024.
5. Environmental information / Disclosure pursuant to article 8 of Regulation (EU) 2020/852 (Taxonomy Regulation)
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
211
30 As explained, some of the items linked to activities CCM 7.7. and CCM 4.3. through the investment plan are included in the numerator.

## Page 219

S1. Own workforce
A. Strategy
SBM-3. Material impacts, risks and opportunities
Our people’s talent and contribution are key to Inditex’s success. Consequently, we strive to prevent, mitigate and
remediate potential negative impacts and their associated risks for our workforce, as well as to drive positive impacts and
opportunities.
S1.I1
Quality employment

/ Own operations
Transversal policies:
/ Code of Conduct
/ Human Rights Policy
/ Sustainability Policy

/ Diversity and Inclusion Policy

/ Own operations
Material Impacts, Risks and Opportunities related to our people
TYPE
(I/R/O)
PHASE OF THE VALUE
CHAIN
RELATED POLICIES
i More information on stakeholder engagement and our materiality assessment in section SBM-3. Material impacts,
risks and opportunities and IROs. Disclosures on the materiality assessment process of this Report.
Scope
We have analysed the possible positive and negative impacts for our own workforce. In the context of this Report, and
pursuant to the ESRS definitions, ‘our own workforce’ includes both employees and non-employees, hereinafter: ‘our
teams’.
Employees: personnel who have a direct employment relationship with the Company. Hereinafter referred to as ‘our
people’, ‘workforce’ or ‘employees’. This category includes the following groups, according to their job classification:
/ Management:  employees in management positions with responsibility for interdisciplinary working groups related to the
areas of design, manufacturing, distribution, logistics, stores, technology, sustainability and other general services. It also
includes store management.
6. Social Information / S1. Own workforce
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
219

S1.SBM-3_01;
S1.SBM-3_02

## Page 220

/ Supervisor: employees who are part of interdepartmental and transversal working groups for design, logistics and stores,
as well as sustainability, technology and other general services.
/ Specialist: employees with an impact through individual contribution related to one of the Group's activities in the areas of
design, manufacturing, distribution, logistics, stores, sustainability, technology and other general services.
Non-employees: other professionals who are not our own employees but that carry out activities for the Company (either
by providing labour or people provided by employment companies). Hereinafter, ‘other professionals’ or ‘non-employees’
Within this scope, we identify the groups at most risk of vulnerability at the workplace and we establish measures for their
protection and support. These actions are materialised in policies and best practices to prevent discrimination or
harassment of any kind.
Accordingly, we work to integrate people in our teams, comprised of people of different sexes, gender identities and
expressions, sexual orientations, races, ethnicities, ages, education, socio-economic status, disabilities or religions.
i More information in sections S1-4. Actions related to own workforce and S1-9. Diversity metrics of this Report.
Occurrence and types of impacts
The potential negative impacts related to our people tend to be systemic at the global and industry level. There are also
individual impacts, such as those linked to occupational accidents, cases of harassment, or protection of personal data.
To prevent both kinds of impact and mitigate the possible negative effects deriving from them, we introduce measures like
internal diversity and inclusion audits, occupational accident prevention programmes and training initiatives. We also have
an active role in social dialogue thanks to our collaboration with UNI Global Union (UNI), with whom we have a global
agreement that promotes decent work and protects the labour rights of all our workforce.
We also aim to generate positive impacts on our people. To achieve this, we implement measures to improve their quality
of life and well-being, by establishing favourable working conditions, work-life balance measures or adequate wages.
We also develop initiatives focused on quality employment through social dialogue, such as the creation of our Well-being
Committees; on promoting equality and inclusion in our people, as in programmes such as Salta and the INCLUYE project;
and on ensuring health, safety and well-being in our workplaces.
Lastly, we are concerned about the professional development of our people. We therefore provide practical training, which
is mostly given by in-house employees or developed in collaboration with top-level external entities.
i More information on actions related to our people in section S1-4. Actions related to own workforce  of this Report.
In addition, we have not identified negative impacts linked to the transition plans, nor operations or geographical areas with
a significant risk of incidents of forced, compulsory or child labour among our people.

i More information in the ‘Just Transition’ section of our Climate Transition Plan, available on Inditex’s corporate website.
Risks and opportunities
Based on the analysis of impacts and dependencies, in particular those linked to the dependence on qualified personnel,
we also identify the risks and opportunities associated with our people.
This allows us to work on the prevention and mitigation of risks and their associated negative effects. Part of this effort
consists of ensuring favourable working conditions and robust compliance with regulations to minimise the chance of
labour disputes. We also establish recruitment and retention strategies to avoid losing talented professionals; we implement
communication channels to resolve disputes early on; and we carry out initiatives for the dissemination, prevention and
promotion of occupational health, safety and well-being.
6. Social Information / S1. Own workforce
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
220

S1.SBM-3_11

S1.SBM-3_03

S1.SBM-3_04

S1.SBM-3_06

S1.SBM-3_07;
S1.SBM-3_08;
S1.SBM-3_09;
S1.SBM-3_10

S1.SBM-3_05

## Page 221

Moreover, we see attracting and retaining talent as a key opportunity for our business. Hence, we implement specific talent
programmes such as Zara Business Graduates and Zara Boost, as well as training initiatives such as LEAP.
i More information on our actions related to our people in section S1-4. Actions related to own workforce of this Report.
These risks and opportunities apply across the board to all our operations and do not correspond more to a specific group
of people.
B. Impact, risk and opportunity management
S1-1. Policies related to own workforce
We undertake to safeguard the rights of the people who make up Inditex and to foster continuous dialogue so as to take
into account their interests. This commitment is structured around different corporate policies, based on respect for human
rights; diversity, inclusion and equal opportunities; equal treatment and opportunities with regard to recruitment and
promotion; health, safety and well-being; as well as information security and data protection.
Policies related to our people
/ Code of Conduct i
/ Human Rights Policy i
/ Diversity and Inclusion Policy i
/ Global Anti-Harassment Policy i
/ Occupational Health and Safety Policy i
/ Compliance Policy regarding Personal Data Protection and Privacy i
/ Information Security Policy i
/ Ethics Line Procedure i
/ Global Policy on Internal Reporting Channels i
/ Sustainability Policy i
i More information on our policies and their updates in section MDR-P. Policies in this Report.
Our policies are the cornerstone that allow us to:
/ Respect human and labour rights, rejecting, among other things, any practice of child labour, forced or compulsory
labour and modern slavery.
/ Implement practices to prevent any form of discrimination and to promote equal opportunities in access to employment
and career advancement. This involves, for example, initiatives for inclusion and accessibility to decent work for people
with disabilities, as well as actions to promote the equality of people, regardless of their gender, ethnicity, sexual
orientation, or any other characteristic.
/ Work to improve satisfaction by providing quality employment, promoting job stability, with guarantees of fair working
hours, work-life balance and flexible working, as well as the payment of an adequate wage.
/ Look after the well-being of our teams with health and safety measures.
/ Establish effective mechanisms for social dialogue, encouraging collective bargaining and freedom of association.
/ Develop tools to ensure the protection and safeguarding of information on personal data and the privacy of our teams.
6. Social Information / S1. Own workforce
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
221

S1.SBM-3_12

S1.MDR-P_01-06;
S1-1_01;
S1-1_02

## Page 222

Respect for and promotion of the human, labour and non-labour rights of our teams is included in our Sustainability Policy
and further developed in the Human Rights Policy. Hence, the Human Rights Policy, which is binding on our employees and
on all those who have a relationship with the Group, enshrines Inditex’s commitment to the values that these frameworks
represent. For prioritisation purposes, the Policy also identifies the rights most directly connected with our main activities
and the stakeholders we interact with.
The Code of Conduct, along with other internal regulations, also reflects this projection, as we strive to ensure it is upheld
and fostered among our teams, with an emphasis on collaboration and respectful communication among people, both
internally and externally, in line with our Diversity and Inclusion Policy.
In addition, we complement our commitment to the Global Framework Agreement with UNI Global Union for the respect
and promotion of decent work and labour rights, which encourages best practices in labour relations.
Our Human Rights Policy, in line with all other corporate policies, including our Sustainability Policy, expressly prohibits
forced and child labour. Furthermore, we do not tolerate any form of modern slavery or human trafficking in our
organisation or in our supply chain.
This rejection of any form of forced or compulsory labour is also reflected in our Code of Conduct, which details the
minimum age of employment, in line with the prohibition of child labour. In this way, we ensure that we do not employ
anyone under the age of 16, unless not doing so would be in breach of applicable local regulations.
i More information on the Human Rights Policy in section MDR-P. Policies of this Report.
The commitment to respect and promote human rights in both the Group’s activities and in the communities in which we
operate is aligned with international benchmark principles, such as the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human
Rights and the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises on Responsible Business Conduct.
To ensure that our policies are known and effective, we promote continuous dialogue with our teams, using tools such as
the Agreement with UNI Global Union, communication with the trade unions, application of our Code of Conduct,
implementation of training measures, and the provision of guaranteed communication and consultation channels such as
the Ethics Line. This allows us to advance in our commitment to respect and promote decent work and the labour rights of
our people.
Diversity inclusion and equal opportunities
As part of our commitment to respect human rights, our regulations also include policies related to the promotion of
diversity and inclusion, and zero tolerance of any kind of discrimination or harassment. These policies also embody our
principles regarding training, in order to guarantee equal opportunities and professional growth within the Company.
Diversity and Inclusion Policy
Approved by the Board of Directors on 12 December 2017 and partially amended on 14 December 2020
Key contents
/ Promotion of diverse teams and a culture of equality, inclusion and respect, based on multiculturalism and gender
diversity, gender identity and expression, race, ethnicity, age, education, socio-economic class, disability, religion,
sexual orientation, etc.
Scope
/ The entire Group (companies and individuals)
/ We promote its application to all related natural and legal person (workers in the supply chain, customers, suppliers,
contractors and those who participate in selection processes)
Accountable for
the
implementation
/ People department and its chief officer
Third-party
standards
/ GEEIS (Gender Equality European & International Standard) and GEEIS Diversity certifications
/ UN Standards of Conduct for Business against LGBTQI+ discrimination at a global level
Consideration
