## Page 223

Global Anti-Harassment Policy
Approved by the Board of Directors on 15 March 2022 and updated on 7 November 2023
Key contents
/ Commitment to the prevention of harassment of any type or form
/ Prohibition of any kind of physical, sexual, psychological or verbal harassment or abuse to any member of our
workforce, as well as any conduct that might create an intimidating, offensive or hostile work
Scope
/ The entire Group (companies and individuals), both in their relationship with other members of the Group and with any
other stakeholder
/ People who provide services or collaborate with the Group (including the Board of Directors)
Accountable for
the
implementation
/ People department and its chief officer
Third-party
standards
/ Convention No. 190 and Recommendation No. 206 of the ILO on violence and harassment
Consideration
given to
stakeholders
/ We involve workers’ representatives in the advancements made in both the Policy and the Code of Conduct
/ Involvement through social dialogue, in the implementation of protocols against sexual harassment in the Equality
Plans with trade union representatives in Spain
Zero tolerance approach to discrimination
We are committed to a zero-tolerance policy towards any kind of discrimination, and we undertake to drive diversity
through a culture of inclusion, equality and respect.
This commitment is established on the basis of our Human Rights Policy, and is made effective through the application of
the Anti-Harassment Policy and the Diversity and Inclusion Policy, as well as the Code of Conduct, which must be
complied with by all our people.
This prohibits discrimination at all levels of the Company on the grounds of race, ethnic origin, disability, illness, religion,
marital status, sexual orientation, political opinion, age, nationality, sex, gender identity and expression or for any other
reason.
We also recognise that people are unique in our differences and similarities. In keeping with our slogan ‘We Design
Opportunities for All’, our Diversity and Inclusion Policy is the foundation on which to build protection and equal
opportunities initiatives for especially vulnerable groups in connection with human rights.
These notably include those focused on socio-ethnic inclusion, inclusion of LGBTQI+ people and disability inclusion.
Programmes such as SALTA and INCLUYE, which promote the employability and socio-labour inclusion of people who
face socio-economic or educational barriers or obstacles because of a disability, are examples of this commitment.
The Policy also fosters equal treatment and opportunities for women and men in the workplace.
i More information on the SALTA programme in section S1-4. Actions related to own workforce and on the diversity
target in section S1-5/S1-12. Persons with disabilities and related target of this Report.
Moreover, one of our priorities is to comply with the principles of universal accessibility, especially for our workforce and
customers who may have any kind of disability. That is why we adapt our workplace for people with disabilities or mobility
issues.
To apply our Policy, we develop procedures and implement training initiatives and programmes, driven by the Diversity and
Inclusion department. This department is part of the Culture and Values team and is led by the person designated as Global
Diversity & Inclusion Officer, who coordinates an international team. Among other functions, this team supports the
Diversity Champions, who are responsible for this area in their markets, retail concepts and workplaces. In addition, to
ensure transparency and independence, we have our Ethics Line.
6. Social Information / S1. Own workforce
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
223

S1-1_10;
S1-1_11

S1-1_12

S1-1_18

## Page 224

Our commitment to diversity and inclusion is also driven by the Board of Directors. This body adopts measures aimed at
promoting diversity and avoiding discrimination of any kind, particularly on grounds of gender, when appointing members
of the Board or Senior Management. The People department management defines and implements the strategies and
measures to comply with the general principles set out in the Diversity and Inclusion Policy.
Equal treatment and opportunities with regard to the recruitment, training and promotion of people
The Diversity and Inclusion Policy is also applicable to recruitment and selection, pay and benefits, promotions, transfers,
career development and training, demotions, dismissals and other disciplinary measures.
We therefore promote initiatives to optimise individual and collective performance and spaces for the integral development
of people, in keeping with a practical and adapted training model. Most training actions are led by in-house professionals,
occasionally supplemented by renowned external entities.
The corporate learning platform Tra!n is the core of our educational offering, providing an extensive catalogue of
customised digital content. This tool facilitates the management of and participation in in-person training, covering strategic
topics such as ‘Fashion and product’, ‘Sustainability’, ‘Customer experience’, ‘Operations’, ‘Skills’ and ‘Digital’.
Tra!n is available in all markets where we operate, with the exception of Mainland China, which instead uses Grow, a local
platform that offers training aligned with Tra!n standards and targets. This multinational approach ensures homogeneous
access to development opportunities, adapted to each geography. It also helps us to offer a wide range of customised
digital content, facilitating the registration of online and in-person training courses.
In addition to advancing training, we foster internal promotion and mobility so that our people can evolve.
i More information on training in sections S1-4. Actions related to own workforce and S1-13. Training and skills
development metrics of this Report.
Awareness
Training our people in diversity and inclusion is essential to create an inclusive culture and make spaces free of any kind of
discrimination. In 2024, 98,749 people from all over the world accessed training on diversity and inclusion in this connection.
In 2023 we launched the Diversity and Inclusion Channel, a specific training channel. It covers mandatory training on
diversity, equity and inclusion, including the basics of our Diversity and Inclusion Policy. Furthermore, in 2024 we added
training on disability inclusion at the global level and another on inclusive language in Spanish-speaking countries. In total,
74,064 people, including 7,120 team supervisors or management, that have taken at least one of these courses.
Our catalogue of mandatory training also includes training on the Global Anti-Harassment Policy and specific content on
diversity and inclusion for people in leadership positions. For example, the company’s senior management programme
developed in collaboration with IE Business School, in which 113 executives attended training in 2024, includes a specific
module on inclusive leadership and the promotion of inclusive and equitable work environments, led by the Global Diversity
& Inclusion Officer.
Health, safety and well-being
As part of our unwavering commitment to people’s health, safety and well-being, we apply international standards and
focus on measures to identify and mitigate risks, foster a culture of safety, training and skill-building, along with health and
well-being programmes, among other initiatives.
6. Social Information / S1. Own workforce

Occupational Health and Safety Policy
Approved by the Board of Directors on 10 September 2019 and updated on 13 December 2022
Key contents
/ Commitment to and continuous improvement of workplace well-being, health and safety
/ Effective management of workplace accidents and occupational illnesses
/ Foundation for our occupational health and safety management systems
Scope
/ The entire Group (companies and individuals)
Accountable for
the
implementation
/ People department and its chief officer
Third-party
standards
/ ISO 45001
Consideration
given to
stakeholders
/ Involvement through occupational health and safety training and management plans, with the participation of
stakeholders
In addition to the Occupational Health and Safety Policy, we have an occupational health and safety management system,
certified to ISO 45001 standard. This system optimises the monitoring of safety conditions in the workplace to reduce
incidents and aims to increase workers’ participation in health-promoting activities, including emergency and first aid
training.
Information security and data protection
Data protection and privacy, in particular in respect of our people and customers, is of paramount importance for Inditex.
Accordingly, the following policies reflect best practices in connection with information security:
/ Compliance Policy regarding Personal Data Protection and Privacy: in line with our Code of Conduct, it sets out our
mission to respect privacy and protect personal data, in particular with respect to the risk of data loss as a result of
cybersecurity vulnerabilities.
/ Information Security Policy: it ensures the protection of information, regardless of how it is communicated, shared,
projected or stored. This protection covers both the information inside the Group and the information shared with third
parties.
i More information in section S4-1. Policies related to consumers of this Report.
Grievance mechanisms
Grievance mechanisms are key to help identify potential negative impacts and develop plans for their prevention,
mitigation and remediation, while tackling any associated risks at the same time. The Ethics Line is available to our people
as an effective and accessible communication channel to confidentially communicate their queries and/or doubts
regarding the interpretation of our policies, or breaches and other non-compliances relating to the legal system, our Codes
of Conduct or any other internal regulations.
i More information on how the Ethics Line works in section G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct  of this Report.
Communication of policies
All our policies can be found on the Group’s intranet and corporate website and are available in a wide range of languages
to provide access to any stakeholder, whether internal or external.
Furthermore, to ensure that all our teams are aware of corporate policies and their updates, we make use of training and
monitoring plans, as well as other internal communication initiatives.
i More information in section G1-3. Prevention and detection of corruption and bribery of this Report.

## Page 226

S1-2. Processes for engaging with own workforce
At Inditex we believe that it is essential to listen to and involve our people to correctly manage the actual and potential
impacts on them. That is why we have relationship and dialogue mechanisms that help us to ensure that their perspectives
are heard and incorporated into our decision making. These include:
/ Social dialogue mechanisms
Our commitment to the right of participation is regulated by the Code of Conduct. In its section on adequate working
conditions, we guarantee the right of all workers to join, associate with and/or create the trade union of their choice, as
well as the right to collective bargaining. The Code also encompasses respect for employees’ right to have trade unions
and workers’ representatives represent them and negotiate their working conditions collectively.
In turn, we also have an active role in social dialogue through our relationship with UNI Global Union and the constitution
of the Company’s European Works Council (EWC), a body for assurance and effectiveness of information and
consultation of workers on transnational issues. The section on ‘Agreements with our people’ contains more details on
these mechanisms.
/ Internal communication with our people
INET, our main internal communication channel is a tool that allows us to connect with our people and enhance their
engagement. We also have internal communication spaces for each brand, which connect all our stores, and we are
making progress in the deployment of our internal social network InStories.
In addition, to ascertain our people's perception of their experience at the Company, we developed ‘Your opinion matters
to us’, an anonymous survey that we send to store employees when they leave Inditex. Launched in 2016 and available in
all our markets with own stores, it allows us to learn their opinion on aspects such as the relationship with other members
of their team or managers, their training, salary or how well their working hours suit their availability.
Over the course of 2024, we received around 22,000 responses. In addition, the question ‘Would you recommend Inditex
as a place to work?’ is used to measure our people engagement. In 2024, this question obtained an average of 74%
positive responses (71% in 2023), obtaining 97% positive responses in Spain (93% in 2023).
/ Ethics Committee
The Ethics Committee is the internal body in charge of managing the Ethics Line, our main internal grievance mechanism.
Thanks to its work, we can gather all information related to possible breaches of our Code of Conduct, as well as any
concerns or violations of our people’s rights. This information is crucial to identify potential negative impacts on our
people and other professionals.
i More information on the Ethics Committee and the Ethics Line in section G1-1. Corporate culture and business
conduct of this Report.
/ People department
This team, present in each of the markets where we have own workforce, is the first contact point for our people, ensuring
an ongoing and direct relationship with those who work in the Group.
Accordingly, our strategy for relationships with our people includes constant communication through tools such as the
Agreement with UNI Global Union, the European Works Council (EWC), in-house training and promotion and internal
communications. We also carry out one-off communications such as those linked to cases received via the Ethics Line.
Furthermore, we train the people responsible for these dialogue processes internally to ensure that we have functional
mechanisms in place and that the relationship and dialogue processes with our people and/or the organisations
representing their interests are conducted from a position of respect and protection of human rights.
By using these tools, we can involve our people in managing impacts, factoring their views into the development and
evaluation phases of impact prevention and mitigation strategies.
Responsibility for relationships with our people lies with the Inditex Group’s Chief People Officer, except for the Ethics Line,
which is outside the realm of responsibility of that role.
Agreements with our people
Inditex plays an active part in the social dialogue through its relationship with UNI Global Union (UNI). In 2009, Inditex and
UNI signed a Global Agreement for the respect and promotion of decent work and labour rights, which covers 100% of the
Group’s workforce and was renewed in 2024 to mark the 15th anniversary. UNI is a network of trade unions in the trade and
retail sector which represents more than 20 million workers across 150 countries.
6. Social Information / S1. Own workforce
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
226

S1-2_01;
S1-2_02

S1-2_03;
S1-2_14

S1-2_04

S1-2_05

## Page 227

The Agreement, applicable to the entire Group workforce and whose principles will be applicable to franchises,
subcontractors and suppliers of services, sets out the minimum rights and respect for statutory, contractual or collective
agreement provisions that confer higher rights, in matters such as working hours or the protection of a safe, healthy and
sustainable working environment, and promotion of best practices for occupational health and safety with the appropriate
equipment and training.
This Agreement with UNI refers to a number of issues governed pursuant to the principles established by
the ILO:
/ Freedom of association and the right to collective: bargaining: specific mention is made to the application
of ILO Conventions No. 87 and 98, stating that ‘Inditex recognises the right of trade unions to represent
workers and to regulate through collective bargaining the terms and conditions of their employment’.
/ Freedom to join any trade union: in addition to guaranteeing this freedom, non-discrimination due to
membership is also guaranteed.
/ Wage: when setting the wages of the people working for the Inditex Group, in addition to the provisions of
international labour standards and those arising from national legislation and collective bargaining
agreements, Inditex shall also take into account the definition of the minimum living wage, as endorsed by
the ILO Governing Body in session on 13 March 2024.
/ Working hours: Inditex undertakes to comply with the requirements set by national law and national
collective bargaining agreements (ILO Conventions No. 1 and 47 and Recommendation No. 116) on working
hours.
Furthermore, since 2015 we have an agreement in place with UNI, the US trade union United Food and Commercial
Workers International Union (UFCW) and Zara USA, which led to the approval of a collective agreement for the US stores.
In Spain, a significant agreement on working conditions for store staff was also reached: the State Collective Agreement for
the Improvement and Standardisation of the Remuneration and Social Conditions of the Employees, signed in 2023 by the
leading trade unions and the Group’s brands.
In turn, on 10 July 2024, an agreement was reached with the Federation of Services of the trade union Comisiones Obreras,
applicable to the store employees of the retail concepts, concerning the management of the experience of senior staff in
matters relating to the guarantee of continuity and reduction of working hours due to age. Its most relevant aspects are:
minimum guarantee of continuity after 8 years in the Company; loyalty bonus after 20 and 25 years; and reduction of
working hours from 2025 onwards upon turning 55 for those with at least 10 years of seniority.
Moreover, since 2023, the Group has a Equality Plan in Spain. The key aspect of this Plan is that it encompasses all retail
concepts and central services, thus unifying the provisions under previous plans. This Plan coexists with the equality plans
already in place at all our logistics centres and factories and has the following aim:
/ To guarantee the principle of equal treatment, applying the gender perspective in terms of access, job performance and
professional development at all levels and at all stages of the professional career.
/ To apply the gender perspective to all areas, consolidating an environment that fosters the co-responsible exercise of the
rights to a personal, family and work-life balance and avoiding any type of discrimination or unfavourable treatment
related to pregnancy, maternity and breastfeeding.
/ To continue to guarantee equal pay for equal work.
/ To promote an equality culture within the company through the dissemination of the values of gender equality and non-
discrimination of any person.
/ To consolidate an inclusive, non-sexist and non-discriminatory internal and external communication, disseminating an
image of the company in which there is respect for women and men and for equal opportunities.
/ To prevent and penalise sexual or gender-based harassment.
6. Social Information / S1. Own workforce
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
227

## Page 228

i More information on the measures established in terms of work-life balance after the signing of the Group’s Equality
Plan in Spain in section S1-15. Work-life balance metrics of this Report.
With regard to the assessment of the existing mechanisms for social relations and dialogue, proof of their effectiveness
can be seen in the reaching of agreements with our workforce that are beneficial to both parties. Along these lines, in 2019
we set up the Company’s European Works Council (EWC), a body for assurance and effectiveness of information and
consultation of workers on transnational issues.
To preserve the health of social dialogue at the local level at Inditex subsidiaries, training is provided to our People and store
managers areas concerning trade union rights and cooperation with our workforce’s legal representatives. Thus, the works
councils and management of our subsidiaries meet periodically to inform, consult and listen to the union representatives
and reach agreements to improve people’s working conditions and quality of life.
For example, in 2024, the Mexico subsidiary reached an agreement to sign a collective work contract with the Justo Sierra
trade union, which must be approved at each store, with the aim of regulating relations between the workforce and the
Company. In the United States, the renegotiation of the agreements is yielding benefits such as guaranteed wage increase
based on seniority; extended leave for illness or the death of a family member; union leave with reinstatement for New York
and New Jersey trade unions; and the reimbursement of parking expenses for people who cannot access free parking
facilities or public transport.
Moreover, to improve work-life balance, additional rest measures were approved for full-time and part-time employees in
the United States. Additionally, in France, a new seniority bonus for 25 years with the Company has been agreed with the
representative trade unions.
Communication with our people
We are mindful that, to achieve quality communication with our workforce, we must overcome barriers such as cultural
differences or language. Consequently, among other measures, we have initiatives, including cultural actions with adapted
content and initiatives, and we rely on our People team to maintain personal and direct dialogue.
Our people also have access to INET, our main internal communication channel. Available in website and app format and in
as many as 23 languages. This tool allows us to communicate Group news in real time, simplify and digitalise the day-to-
day processes of our people or strengthen connections between our employees.
Plus, our brand-specific channels —Dear Team for Zara, MD Journal for Massimo Dutti, Oysho News for Oysho,
#tengoalgoquecontarte for Zara Home and Stradi People for Stradivarius— adapt commercial messages to the identity of
each brand and the particularities of each market. This allows team managers to enrich their meetings with updated and
relevant content. We have also customised one of our key internal channels —Dear Team— creating a space exclusively for
sales reps. This approach responds to the need to provide job-specific content that facilitates access to information and
decision-making.
In 2024 we continued to roll out InStories, our internal social network, in four new markets: Switzerland; Hong Kong SAR;
Taiwan, China; and Macao SAR, reaching a total of 37 markets. This tool allows us to meet people in the Group from
anywhere in the world through photos and videos, follow our hashtags and take part in challenges and campaigns.
Communication with our people: vulnerable groups
At Inditex we work to ensure that people at risk or in vulnerable situations have access to equal opportunities and work in a
safe environment free from discrimination. Accordingly, under our Diversity and Inclusion strategy, we see it as important
to actively listen to our people, especially groups potentially vulnerable to impacts or at greater risk, such as migrants,
groups at risk or in a situation of social exclusion, people with disabilities or members of the LGBTQI+ community.
In 2024, we have made a concerted effort to promote the participation of people from these groups in internal and external
communication materials, where they have shared their testimonies in videos, articles, and posts on corporate social
networks. Our Voices of Pride capaigns, which give a voice to four people from the LGBTQI+ community in Spain, Brazil,
the Netherlands and Germany, and Voices of Inclusion, featuring five people with disabilities from our workforce in
Portugal, the United Kingdom, Greece, South Korea and Brazil, are a reflection of this commitment to empowering and
giving a voice to people who may face obstacles to professional development and full social inclusion.
Likewise, our socio-labour inclusion programmes such as SALTA and INCLUYE are designed to receive the perspective of
our people, and incorporate monitoring and support tools, such as mentoring and tutoring, which are provided by both
internal staff and external partners, as well as dialogue sessions.
6. Social Information / S1. Own workforce

## Page 229

In this context, we work with expert organisations to accompany the participants in our initiatives. These organisations also
share their views to help us to improve our programmes. Thus, we collaborate with global and regional organisations that
focus on fighting against discrimination and promoting equality in all areas: racial, gender, sexual orientation, gender
identity, disability, etc. Foundations such as the European Network Against Racism (ENAR) in Europe and The Legal
Defense Fund (LDF) in the United States advocate for the development of campaigns and programmes to raise awareness
about racism and discrimination
i More information on related actions in section S1-4. Actions related to own workforce of this Report.
S1-3. Processes to remediate negative impacts and channels for own
workers to raise concerns
Processes to remediate negative impacts
We identify our potential and actual negative impacts not only to prevent them, but also to mitigate and remedy their effects,
should they occur. Our actions therefore seek to minimise the potential negative impact of our activities.
This is reflected in our relationship with our teams, to whom we apply special protection. We do this by developing human
rights due diligence processes and activating grievance mechanisms that enable us to identify negative impacts affecting
our workforce and act accordingly.
i More information in section GOV-4. Statement on due diligence of this Report.
Main communication and grievance mechanisms
All our teams have access to the Ethics Line, as do all our stakeholders. The procedure for handling the concerns and
reports received through the Ethics Line and ensuring the channel's efficacy is the same as for those received from other
stakeholders and is outlined in the relevant chapter of this Report.
They may also use third-party reporting channels that have been appointed by the relevant authorities, including the
institutions, bodies and agencies of the European Union.
In order to ensure that everyone in our workforce are aware of our Ethics Line, in addition to including information and
facilitating access to it via INET and our website, we carry out training, awareness and communication activities that are key
to building and strengthening our corporate ethical culture. This training, included in the Compliance Training Plan, is
adapted to the risk profile of the different groups of employees that form part of the Inditex Group.
In 2024, we also carried out awareness and communication initiatives for those working in stores and subsidiary offices. An
example of this is the use of specific signage announcing the existence of the Ethics Line in rest areas and common
spaces.

i More information on how the Ethics Line works, including monitoring, control and assurance of its effectiveness, in
section G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct of this Report and on compliance training, awareness and
sensitisation in section G1-3. Prevention and detection of corruption and bribery of this Report.
S1-4. Actions related to own workforce
In terms of prevention and mitigation of our negative impacts, we focus our efforts on guaranteeing equal pay and
preventing the gender gap in our people, minimising the risk of accidents and occupational diseases, ensuring protection
against situations of harassment at work, the demotivation of our workforce due to a lack of training and the problems
arising from the loss of data.
To influence these issues, we carry out various actions, including promoting internal audits focused on diversity and
inclusion, developing our Equality Plan in Spain, implementing measures to prevent accidents and our many training
initiatives.
We also protect human rights and prevent their infringement, and specifically we establish prevention measures to banish
child or forced labour, among others.
6. Social Information / S1. Own workforce

## Page 230

Our actions are designed to manage our impacts, risks and opportunities, both potential and actual. Where the impact has
already occurred, we apply actions from a dual perspective of remedying the negative effect and preventing its recurrence.
This is the case with health, safety and well-being initiatives for our workforce, which seek to act in occupational accidents
and diseases, or with initiatives related to the management and prevention of workplace harassment.
With regard to risk prevention and mitigation, we promote a favourable workplace and strictly comply with labour
regulations, thereby reducing the risk of labour disputes. We also develop strategies to attract and retain talent, and
implement effective communication channels to facilitate early resolution of potential discrepancies. We are also working
on measures for outreach, prevention and promotion of occupational health, safety and well-being.
In addition to preventing and mitigating any negative impacts deriving from our activity, at Inditex we are committed to
maximising the positive impact that we can pass on to our teams.
In order to do so, we carry out initiatives that allow us to have a positive impact on our people. Among them, we would like
to highlight our efforts to guarantee diversity, equality and inclusion in our Company, such as our Salta programme, for the
employment of vulnerable groups; the INCLUYE project, for the inclusion of people with disabilities; and the Woman in Tech
and Technovation Girls initiatives, focused on empowering women and girls and showcasing female talent in tech and
entrepreneurship.
As an opportunity, we identify the attraction and retention of talent as a key factor in driving the development of our
business. We therefore carry out strategic actions, such as our specialised talent programmes Zara Business Graduates
and Zara Boost, and training initiatives such as LEAP, etc.
It is important to note that we follow up each of our actions and their outputs. This means that we can prolong initiatives that
generate a real positive impact and eliminate or improve on those that do not obtain the expected performance.
Through this monitoring, we see continued progress in the relationship with our workforce, in the form of year-on-year
growth in participation, as in the case of actions linked to health, safety and well-being or training initiatives. Our actions also
allow us to contribute to our targets and commitments in relation to our people. A clear example is the effectiveness of our
disability inclusion initiatives, which has enabled us to reach our recruitment target in 2024.
i More information in section S1-5/S1-12. Persons with disabilities and related target of this Report.
We also design our actions in consideration of the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Through our workforce-
related initiatives, we contributed to the following SDGs in 2024:

Diversity and inclusion
Our people are at the heart of our commitment to diversity and inclusion, which is why we have developed a strategy based
on the following four global pillars:
/ Gender equality
      / LGBTQI+ inclusion       / Socio-ethnic inclusion        / Disability inclusion

S1-4_07

## Page 231

/ Key action for gender equality: promoting STEM talent
Related IROs: [S1.I1; S1.I2; S1.I3; S1.I5; S1.I6; S1.R1; S1.O1]
Mindful of the challenge posed by attracting and retaining female talent with technological profiles, since 2020 we have
been actively working to boost the professional careers of women in technological areas so as to promote the value of
diversity and inclusion and have an impact on the Company and society.
Thanks to the internal initiative Women in Tech, our work is grounded on two pillars: the need to influence society,
supporting actions that encourage girls to take an interest in science and technology; and the impact on women in the field
of technology through actions that empower them. Internally, we develop projects that showcase the importance of gender
equality in this field, and our talent pool promotes fairness and diversity within the technology team. Accordingly, in this
year's junior programmes, we recruited a 58% of women, addressing our internal ambition of increasing the proportion of
women in technology and related positions.
Also in the STEM field, in 2024 we have supported the Technovation Girls project for the third consecutive year. This
entrepreneurship and innovation programme invites girls aged between eight  to 18 to learn and develop skills for using
technology to solve problems, with the aim of reducing gender gap in tech. This collaboration was carried out through the
sponsorship of Power to Code, an ambassador organisation for this initiative in Spain.
/ Key action for LGBTQI+ inclusion: I AM PROUD project
Related IROs: [S1.I1; S1.I3; S1.I6; S1.R1; S1.O1]
Through our corporate project I AM PROUD, we nurture inclusive environments where everyone feels welcome and
where prejudice, gender stereotypes and discrimination based on gender identity and sexual orientation are addressed. In
2024, on the occasion of International LGBTQI+ Pride Day, many of our subsidiaries actively participated in various
celebrations, talks and meetings to reflect on the importance of allies in creating more inclusive work environments.
In order to prevent all discrimination against members of the LGBTQI+ community, and in particular transgender and non-
binary people, we continued to strengthen our internal inclusion policies. Therefore, we are adapting our systems so that
our people can express their gender identity within a spectrum of options ranging from binary ('Male' and 'Female') to 'Non-
Binary' and 'Other/Unspecified'. This option is already available in 10 markets. In 2024 the figures showed 0.02% people
who identify as non-binary gender, and 0.50% as unspecified gender/other (0.02% and 0.01% respectively in 2023).
/ Key action for socio-ethnic inclusion: SALTA programme
Related IROs: [S1.I1; S1.I3; S1.I6; S1.R1; S1.O1]
To promote the integration of groups at risk or in a situation of social exclusion and to reduce inequalities, we have
employability programmes, most notably the SALTA programme, focusing on the socio-occupational inclusion of
vulnerable groups. The purpose of SALTA is to generate job opportunities in our stores, logistics centres and factories for
these people, paving the way for their professional development, or at the Company or in any other opportunity that allows
them to re-enter the labour market and continue advancing to make a qualitative leap (‘salto’) in their lives. At Inditex we
know that migrants and refugees are one of the groups most vulnerable to discrimination and racism at work. For this
reason, a large majority of editions in recent years have focused on this group.
In SALTA we work with various NGOs that support us in the process of selection, training and follow-up of the participants.
Set up in France in 2008, this programme was present in 21 markets31 in 2024.
As a result of this initiative, since 2008 more than 1,900 people have joined our teams in stores, logistics platforms and
factories, and more than 4,000 people have taken part in this project as mentors, trainers or tutors.
With regard to the monitoring of socio-ethnic inclusion, it is worth mentioning that in most markets, and especially in
Europe, the privacy and personal data protection regulations do not allow companies to compile data containing ethnic or
racial criteria. However, in markets like the United States and South Africa, the legislation on equality and non-discrimination
places the emphasis on knowing the individual’s ethnic and racial identity so as to ensure equal opportunities and rights.

/ Key actions for disability inclusion: The INCLUYE Programme and Impact Week
Related IROs: [S1.I1; S1.I3; S1.I6; S1.R1; S1.O1]
The INCLUYE programme, created in 2021, drives the socio-occupational integration of people with disabilities. Since 2022,
all Inditex’s direct employability projects worldwide were grouped together and consolidated under the umbrella of this
programme, which is expanding to ensure the sustainable and lasting inclusion of people with disabilities, having a global
impact. Based on supported employment methodology, the programme involves local organisations that work to achieve
the occupational integration of people with disabilities, and accompany them in their integration as a key factor for
successful employability.
Furthermore, with the aim of promoting the full inclusion of people with disabilities in our workforce, in October 2024, for the
fifth consecutive year, we held the Impact Week worldwide, focusing on disability inclusion. The purpose of this initiative is
to raise awareness and mobilise our workforce to continue to promote accessibility projects, customer experience and
inclusion of people with disabilities, thereby helping to break down barriers and foster equal opportunities.
i More information on the related metrics and targets in sections S1-5/S1-12. Persons with disabilities and related target
of this Report.
In addition to these initiatives, we develop other social and labour market integration projects such as for&from, which
generates synergies with non-profit organisations by opening stores staffed by people with different types of disabilities,
with the commercial support of our concepts.
This collaborative model allows us, on the one hand, to generate employment opportunities for people with disabilities, and,
on the other, to ensure revenues for the organisations themselves to continue to fund new projects and strengthen this
associative fabric. In 2024, we opened the first for&from store in Mexico, bringing the total to 16 stores in Spain, Italy, Mexico
and Portugal.
i More information on the for&from initiative in section S3-4. Actions related to communities of this Report.
i More information on actions related to diversity and inclusion in the report Diversity and Inclusion published on the Inditex corporate
website.

## Page 233

Talent, promotion and training
Talent recruitment programmes
Related IROs: [S1.I5; S1.O1]
/ Talent programmes
Collaboration with universities and schools is a key part of our strategy for attracting talent. We have different programmes
that help incorporate talent to our creative, technological and business areas. These notably include:
• Creatives programme: this year we designed The House, an experience focused on giving participants a broader vision
of the sector and sharing with them concepts that are fundamental in their day-to-day lives as creative people. In 2024 we
reached out to more than 25 international fashion schools, complementing the selection process with talks and
workshops involving our sustainability people to share our approach to this strategic aspect in the creation of our
products.
• Business talent programme: our programme for final year students of bachelor's/master's degrees in management or
engineering, offering them their first work experience in areas such as International sales, Purchasing, Distribution,
Management control, Finance or Logistics. To connect more with them, this year we organised a live event in which more
than 1,000 people participated, where we talked about our values and company culture, as well as the Zara Talent
programme.
• Technological talent programme: as part of Zara Boost, we highlight University Colab, an initiative that has allowed
students to complete their final dissertation with Inditex, in areas such as software development, data and cybersecurity.
We also aim to continue fostering and nurturing female STEM talent, which is why we have launched programmes such
as Ambassador.
Training, promotion and talent development programmes
Related IROs: [S1.I5; S1.R1; S1.O1]
/ Promotion and talent development programmes
• Boost the Change
Since 2021, we have focused on developing communities and activities that foster the culture necessary to enact change,
starting with the Group’s stores and ending with the consolidation of these networks for change in all our offices.
Changemakers is the community leading the cultural transformation from the heart of our business, our
stores. Changemakers are curious people committed to sustainability, diversity and inclusion, who dedicate
part of their working day to keeping abreast of sustainability and fashion issues, training their store teams and
rolling out initiatives that make sustainability a reality in their work environment.
We work to identify, analyse and materialise each of the initiatives proposed by Changemakers. To do so, we
have developed the Boost the Change platform, which shows in real time and in all our Company’s languages
what is happening in matters of sustainability, diversity and inclusion, at each store. Thanks to this programme,
in the last year we helped improve waste management in the stores, inclusion in our fitting rooms and internal
procedures for garment care.
At the end of 2024, this project has been implemented at all the stores in the markets in which Zara and
Stradivarius are present. We also extended its rollout to include Zara Home, Massimo Dutti, Bershka, Oysho
and Pull&Bear. Inditex therefore now has more than 3,000 Changemakers and we plan to consolidate the
Changemaker community across all the Group's stores and markets in future exercises.
• InTalent
InTalent, our Marketplace for in-house opportunities, is essential to structure the path of internal promotion for our
workforce. This year we continued our efforts to get to know our people better through their InTalent profile. In 2024 we had
more than 40,000 people with an updated profile and more than 7,500 internal opportunities were posted.
6. Social Information / S1. Own workforce
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information

## Page 234

• LEAP.
This year, LEAP&Co, our in-store internal talent management tool, has been transformed into LEAP, in what was a complete
overhaul of the internal development tool for more than 55,000 employees worldwide. This new version offers a more
intuitive, clear and customisable experience, designed by the users themselves. This year LEAP has added two new
modules: MISSION Module, for an immersion in the employee's future position before the end of their plan, and STORE
VISIT Module, providing 360° training for future store managers. In addition to being used at Zara and Zara Home, LEAP
has now also been introduced at Massimo Dutti. Through 51 markets in Europe, America and Asia, in 2024 LEAP generated
more than 200,000 training hours.
• Zara Campus
Zara Campus is a project designed to respond to training and development needs among new promotions and people in
management positions in stores. This training is conducted in our Campus stores, which are Zara stores used as a practical
scenario for training our managers in three areas (management, sales and operations). Throughout the year, our store
teams receive training, led by tutors trained by our Campus team, who are responsible for conveying this knowledge to their
teams. Also included are group sessions on communication and leadership, designed specifically for positions of
responsibility, promoting team work.
In addition to LEAP and Campus Zara, we have other projects focused on developing the talent of people working in stores,
including Visual Commercial to the Spotlight, Opening Support and Move.
Training programmes
• Sustainability training
In 2024, as part of our space dedicated to sustainability training and innovation, The Sustainable Fashion
School (SFS), we started the third edition of the Foundations of Textile Manufacturing Master’s programme,
devised in collaboration with the University of Leeds. More than 450 people completed the course, adding to
the 1,217 graduates of previous editions, making it the leading training programme for our product teams.
To supplement this theoretical training and as part of The Sustainable Fashion School (SFS), in 2024 we
launched a new learning format, called SFS Bootcamp. These are immersions in our supply chain factories
giving participants hands-on experience of the main processes that our products go through and enabling
them to share technical and sustainability concerns with professionals from the sector. Over the course of
2024 we held 22 bootcamps, where more than 250 people had the chance to experience first-hand some of
the content learned in the training.
• School of Leadership
The purpose of the School of Leadership is to identify and develop the leaders of the future, ensuring that they understand
their key role in affirming our identity and creating opportunities for change. To this end, we have created an integrated
learning space in which we combine various approaches and methodologies to design high-impact initiatives. All our
programmes are structured around the Company’s four pillars of leadership: SELF, TEAM, IMPACT and TRANSFORM.
Thus, we cultivate leaders able to self-manage, drive their teams, influence their environment and promote strategic change
in the organisation.
This year, more than 1,000 leaders were trained through the School of Leadership’s various programmes, impacting on all
groups at the Company, including employees at stores, central offices, and structural and logistics areas.
In addition to these actions, we carried out other initiatives through which we provide training to our employees on specific
topics. These include the training and education of our people to carry out tasks in a safe and healthy manner, training in
sustainability, diversity and inclusion or corporate training in compliance.
Some of these actions’ outputs are:
• Increase the number of training hours: in 2024 we held 3,292,787 training hours (2,782,830 in 2023).

• Increase the number of training hours per person: in 2024, the number of training hours per person was 20.3 (17.2 in
2023).
• Enhance internal promotion: in 2024, 70% of the Group's vacancies were filled internally, which means that more than
9,300 employees were promoted over the course of the year. By gender, 73% of the promotions were for women, 27% for
men, 0.02% for non-binary people and 0.04% for other-unspecified gender. These figures are consistent with the gender
distribution of our workforce. This commitment to generating opportunities for internal development results, in our office
people, in more than 680 promotions and more than 2,000 changes of functions, department, retail format or market.
i More information on other training initiatives in section S1-13. Training and skills development metrics of this Report.
Health, safety and well-being
We continue to advance in our firm commitment to the health, safety and well-being of our people, by implementing
international regulations, guaranteeing compliance with the most exacting standards and putting in place continuous
improvement indicators as part of the ISO 45001:2018 management system.
We endeavour to implement proactive measures to identify and mitigate potential risks, as well as to nurture a culture of
safety, health and well-being that fosters individual and collective responsibility. After listening to our teams and conducting
periodic risk assessments and incident analyses, we undertake to identify areas for improvement and to implement
corrective measures in a timely and effective manner.
Some of the health, safety and well-being actions were:
/ Training and education of employees to perform tasks in a safe and healthy manner
Related IROs: [S1.I4; S1.R1]
Continuous health and safety training remains vital both for reducing accidents and empowering our teams. Accordingly,
we provide training programmes tailored to a variety of tasks and positions, with objectives ranging from the initial training
to the essential training needed to work safely. Occupational health and safety skills and qualifications form a specialised
body of knowledge that ensures that our teams are properly equipped to understand and prevent risks in their working
environment.
These programmes include, among others, first aid training, specific training for handling machinery and equipment, and
periodic refresher sessions on safety and emergency procedures.
In 2024, a total of 190,589 employees took part, accumulating 155,784 training hours (179,881 participants and 148,152 training
hours in 2023).
/ Promoting well-being
Related IROs: [S1.I3; S1.I4; S1.R1]
• Inditex, Healthy Organisation
As part of our ongoing commitment to promoting and caring for the safety, health and well-being of our community, Inditex
has strengthened its position as a Healthy Organisation. This annual recognition underscores our comprehensive
management approach, addressing both physical and psychosocial aspects, allocating significant resources to our teams’
well-being and fostering their active engagement in the community.
In fact, the companies in Spain and the rest of markets that were certified in 2023 have renewed their certification32 as a
Healthy Organisation in 2024, with the ambition for 2025 to include certification in Austria, Brazil, the United States, France,
Hungary, Kazakhstan, the Netherlands and North Macedonia.
• Workplace well-being programmes
With the aim of promoting health and healthy habits among our people, Inditex has the InHealth Portal, available in 28
markets. In 2024 it was rolled out in Serbia, Montenegro, New Zealand and Australia. Next year we plan to deploy it in
another 17 new markets. This platform features news, actions and challenges adapted to the workplace, cultivating a
balance between body, mind and emotions.
6. Social Information / S1. Own workforce

## Page 236

Furthermore, as part of our commitment to caring for the health of our people, we continue to offer the Open Salud
platform, which is available to people working in the stores of all the Group’s brands in Spain. This tool provides medical
consultations with specialists in general medicine, dermatology, traumatology, psychology and nutrition. The tool has 6,320
users and 11,676 consultations, improving growth compared to 2023.
We also focus our commitment to well-being through the creation of Well-being Committees, which are cross-disciplinary
bodies that coordinate various initiatives related to diversity, equality, promoting health, inclusion, work-life balance, mental
health, working hours, food, ergonomics, workspaces, sports, our people mobility and participation in social events and
actions. During 2024, new Well-being Committees were established in markets such as France, Serbia, Switzerland and
United States, bringing the total number of markets to 23. In 2025 we plan to set up these committees in additional markets.
/ Prevention initiatives
Related IROs: [S1.I4; S1.R1]
• Initiatives for the prevention of musculoskeletal injuries
In the prevention of musculoskeletal injuries, we continuously evaluate ergonomics in our workplaces, providing initial
training on the subject to all employees and emphasising aspects such as ergonomically designed work spaces,
appropriate load lifting techniques and the use of assistive equipment as well as safe working environments. In this regard,
during 2024, work continued on improving spaces in distribution centres, implementing machinery that minimises physical
effort, and 8,477 people were trained in work teams.
We also encourage task rotation, regular rest and active breaks and open communication on health and safety matters. For
instance, the SmartPlanner technological solution has been implemented at our distribution centres, a system that plans
the rotation of tasks in an automatic and agile manner, taking into account ergonomic criteria and internal equity, while
meeting the operational needs of the centre.
Furthermore, in 2024 more than 7,983 workers took part in initiatives such as the Back School and Preventive and Recovery
Plans, at both logistics centres and stores.
• Implementation of Emergency and Evacuation Plans and Self-Protection Plans
So as to actively manage at all times the risks that may arise in any workplace, and in keeping with our philosophy of
following the precautionary principle, we have designed, prepared and implemented Emergency and Evacuation Plans
and Self-Protection Plans that establish the organisational and functional criteria in the different facilities. The objective is
to prevent, control and provide an adequate response, from the outset, to potential emergency situations that may cause
harm to people and/or their property.
Through these Plans, we comply with the regulatory requirements applicable to occupational risk prevention and
occupational health and safety, as well as with the internal requirements established by the Group for the workplaces. In
2024, 1,331 Emergency and Evacuation Plans were made.
i More information on other health and safety initiatives and metrics in section S1-14. Health and safety metrics of this
Report.
General considerations on actions related to our people
Actions related to our people are mainly carried out by our own workforce. The financial resources earmarked for these
actions are associated with staff costs and other external operating expenses, which separately do not represent a material
amount. Both are included in Operating Expenses in the Consolidated Income Statement (see Note 6 of the Consolidated
Annual Accounts).

## Page 237

Agreements and collaborations
We consolidate our commitment to diversity and inclusion by collaborating with various entities. These include Arborus,
through the European and international label GEEIS (Gender Equality European and International Standard), and the
European Network Against Racism (ENAR).
We also partner with the Business Network for LGBTI Diversity and Inclusion (REDI) in Spain, which aims to foster an
inclusive and respectful environment in organisations, and Open for Business, an entity that brings together leading global
companies that advocate for inclusion and LGBTQI+ rights to demonstrate that more inclusive societies are better for
business and for companies because they are more innovative, productive and dynamic.
In the field of disability inclusion, Inditex is a member of the ILO Global Business and Disability Network, of which we have
been part of the Steering Committee since January 2025, Moreover, we are members of Disability:In. These alliances, as
well as those we have at the local level, are established with the objective of continuing to work on best practices to ensure
real and sustainable inclusion over time.
i More information on actions related to diversity and inclusion in the report Diversity and Inclusion, published on the Inditex corporate
website.
C. Metrics and targets
S1-5/S1-12. Persons with disabilities and related target
At Inditex, our goal is to help people with disabilities to overcome barriers, fostering an inclusive culture for everyone. With
this in mind, the Group’s disability inclusion strategy is based on three priority areas:
/ Employability, direct recruitment and professional development.
/ Disability awareness and training for our people.
/ Accessibility of workplaces, inclusive shopping experience and transversal projects.
One of our priorities is to comply with the principles of universal accessibility for persons with disabilities, whether in our
workforce or customers. Our workplaces apply the functional and dimensional requirements that allow an independent use
by people with disabilities or people with impaired mobility. Our commercial websites and apps incorporate functionalities
adapted for people with various kinds of disability, as do our corporate sites, with a focus on technological advances in
digital accessibility that allow us to continue improving in this regard.
Therefore, within the framework of our Shaping Today x Tomorrow initiative in 2024, a multidisciplinary working group was
created with a focus on accessibility, involving people from different areas of the business (Commercial, Operations, Digital,
Construction, Labelling, Design, People, etc.) to promote innovation linked to accessibility for all people, with targets set for
the short-, medium- and long-term.
Employability and awareness are two pillars that we build together, as training and awareness are key to the success of the
labour inclusion process. Our efforts in employability of people with disabilities are materialised in the following objective:
• To double the number of people employed with disabilities
Our commitment to the inclusion of people with disabilities in the workplace is founded upon the basic principles of dignity
and independence, availing them of the opportunities and resources to play an active and equal role in society.
That is why we advocate for sustainable recruitment with a long-term strategy to attract and recruit people with disabilities,
ensuring inclusive selection processes for people with different types of disabilities.
6. Social Information / S1. Own workforce
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information

This target is set in the framework of our membership of the ILO Global Business and Disability Network, that seeks to
promote the inclusion of people with disabilities in the workplace.

The actions carried out over the years have allowed us, not only to achieve the goal of doubling the number of people with
disabilities we employed in 2021, but to exceed it.
Thus, at the end of 2024, we directly employed 3,142 people with disabilities (2,041 people with disabilities in 2023),
representing 2% of the annual average workforce.

In order to establish the target, a prior diagnosis was made during 2022 of the employability status of people with disabilities
in each market, as well as regarding the existence or lack of national or local legislation impacting on such employability.
This diagnosis was made through the Diversity Champions in each market, who have since monitored the actions and
follow-up of the targets at a local level. The Diversity and Inclusion team and the international People teams have been
responsible for coordination with the Diversity Champions community across the different brands and markets.
Likewise, for the purpose of consolidating all the data, since 2022 all our direct employability projects for people with
disabilities have been grouped under the umbrella of the INCLUYE programme, involving expert organisations in each
market. In Spain, it is worth highlighting our work with Plena Inclusión, a confederation comprising more than 880
associated entities. At the international level, we develop our programmes under the global umbrella of the ILO Global
Business and Disability Network and more than 70 national and international organisations. These expert organisations
provide support services and programmes for the occupational integration of people with intellectual disabilities, as well as
awareness and training initiatives to promote more inclusive work environments. Their feedback is therefore essential to
improve our processes and the integration of people with different types of disabilities, enabling us to improve and learn
from experience.
We also establish specific plans at our subsidiaries, to  help them achieve their recruitment targets and improve their
inclusion of people with disabilities through training and awareness. This is the case in Germany, where we collaborate
closely with the Federal Employment Agency; in France, with the Accord Handicap company agreement on disability; in
Italy, which has implemented the ALL IN project for the occupational integration of people with intellectual disabilities in
Milan and Verona; in Portugal, with its Eu Incluo project; in Japan, which holds regular meetings with partner associations
and staff with disabilities; or in Romania, which provides internal training to its teams and recruitment with the support of
several NGOs and the Department for Social Assistance.
i More information on the initiatives carried out in section S1-4. Actions related to own workforce of this Report.
Further considerations
It is important to note that the data linked to this target is particularly sensitive. In fact, there are countries where it is not
permitted to ask or record information on disability status in company systems, as well as others where there are regulatory
limitations in this regard.

## Page 239

The collection of disability information has certain limitations, such as those linked to confidentiality and/or the
heterogeneous legislation in the markets where we operate, which do not always have regulations comparable to those in
Spain. In addition, some countries have specific regulations that affect the accessibility of such information. Specifically,
some of the difficulties we encounter when collecting this information, which in some cases mean we have no measured
data available, are as follows:
/ Countries with legal restrictions on requesting information and/or registering information with regard to a person’s
disability.
/ Countries with discrepancies between the Company’s calculation method (number of people with disabilities on 31/01
divided by the average number of people hired in the last 12 months) and the legal calculation methodology in the country
for the number of people with disabilities.
/ Countries where people with disabilities are not allowed to be hired directly during the first year, even if they are employed
within our workforce, and must be recruited by an expert entity during the first year.
As a result of these limitations, the actual number of people with disabilities in the Company may differ from the figure
reported in some markets where certain idiosyncrasies have been taken into account in the calculation, such as: United
Kingdom, Ireland, Nordic countries (Sweden, Norway, Finland, Denmark), Switzerland, Spain, United States, Canada, Australia
and New Zealand.
i More information on the scope, methodology and calculation changes in the Methodological Annex of this Report.
6. Social Information / S1. Own workforce

S1-6. Characteristics of the employees
It is a priority for Inditex to create motivating, stable and safe working environments, with equal opportunities and
development, ensuring the utmost care for the conditions of our people. We strive to adapt our responses to the local
needs in the 56 markets in which our employees are present, and to guarantee quality workplaces.
The employee figures below are calculated on the basis of the average number of our workforce for the last quarter of the
2024 calendar year, which is the most representative calculation for our business.
i More information on how we calculated employee figures in the Methodological Annex of this Report.
Note 6 to the Consolidated Annual Accounts includes information related to the number of employees, and the breakdowns
by gender and category, as well as associated expenses.
i More information in Note 6 - Operating expenses to the Consolidated Annual Accounts.

## Page 242

Distribution of the workforce by type of contract and type of working hours
In 2024, 83% of our workforce is on a permanent contract (compared with 81% in 2023). In terms of working hours, 57% of
Inditex’s employees work part-time and 41% work full time in 2024 (57% part-time and 42% full-time in 2023).
This breakdown by type of working hours is typical of the retail sector, where there are especially busy periods during sales
that require part-time reinforcements. We also often hire people who combine their work at Inditex with other activities, such
as academic development.
The Full-Time Equivalent (FTE) workforce, a meaningful indicator when it comes to analysing employment quality, shows the
workforce after consolidating all working hours to full-time. In 2024, it represented 78% of total jobs (78% in 2023).
The tables below show the different types of contract and working hours for our people by gender and region:

Leaves and turnover rate
In 2024, 138,152 leaves were recorded (144,930 leaves in 2023), which represents a turnover ratio of 88% in 2024 (93% in
2023). The Group’s voluntary turnover rate was 36% (43% in 2023), while non-voluntary turnover rate was 52% (50% in
2023). Turnover is influenced by the nature of the retail sector, with a marked seasonality. The existence of peaks in activity
that require reinforcement of teams with temporary contracts especially impacts non-voluntary rotation, since it includes
leaves due to the end of the contract of this collective.
S1-9. Diversity metrics
Of our achievements in diversity and inclusion best practices, we highlight the GEEIS (Gender Equality European and
International Standard) certification at several of our subsidiaries. The GEEIS recognises those companies and workplaces
that are actively engaged in building a fairer society, based on equality between men and women and on diversity and
inclusion as the pivotal values of their policies. Between 2018 and 2024, the global corporate Group and nine of our
subsidiaries worldwide (Belgium, France, Italy, the Nordic hub, Germany, Ukraine, Bulgaria, Romania and the United States)
were awarded this certification. This is enabling us to consistently and continuously deploy our gender equality policies in
the various markets.

## Page 244

Distribution by gender
Ensuring equal treatment and opportunities at the workplace has always been a part of our core at Inditex. We nurture
diverse and inclusive workplaces in which women, who represent 73% of our workforce, feel empowered in their professional
development and free of any discriminatory practices. By empowering women and men alike, our aim is to strengthen our
growth and the success of the Company and society as a whole.
Cultivating women’s talent has enabled us to keep mantaining a solid commitment in regard to their leadership within the
Group: in 2024, 77% of the Company’s management positions were filled by women (78% in 2023).

S1-8. Collective bargaining coverage and social dialogue
Globally, the percentage of our workforce covered by local collective bargaining agreements is 61% (59% in 2023), while
within the European Economic Area (EEA) the figure is 84% (82% in 2023). Just like in the previous year, in Spain, 100% of
the workforce is covered by collective bargaining agreements.
For our workforce in EEA countries, we have various collective bargaining agreements, depending on the country in which
the employees are located. The percentages of collective bargaining coverage and workplace representation for our
workforce are included below.

Collective Bargaining Coverage

We work to guarantee the right of participation of our workforce worldwide. That is why our people have minimum
guarantees in their working conditions, regardless of whether or not they are covered by collective bargaining agreements.
Proof of this are the commitments reflected in the Code of Conduct and the renewal in October 2024 of our Global
Agreement signed in 2009 for implementation of fundamental labour rights and decent work, with UNI Global Union.
i More information on the Global Agreement with UNI Global Union in S1-2. Processes for engaging with own workforce
of this Report.
Furthermore, our Human Rights Policy reflects the respect for freedom of association and collective bargaining not only of
our own employees but of other professionals linked to Inditex. To achieve this, we require companies linked to non-
employees to respect the rights of association, affiliation and collective bargaining of their workers, without retaliation, and
without offering payments or remuneration to workers with the aim of hindering the exercise of these rights. We also take an
open and collaborative approach with trade unions and workers' representatives. In this way, we contribute to favourable
working conditions for non-employees, always in line with their right to collective bargaining.
As explained above, in 2019, Inditex formally founded the Company’s EWC, a key body for assurance and effectiveness of
information and consultation of workers. In 2024, Inditex and the EWC published a joint statement against gender-based
violence, which was disseminated internally and externally on 25 November, the International Day for the Elimination of
Violence against Women.
i More information in section S1-2. Processes for engaging with own workforce of this Report.
S1-15. Work-life balance metrics
At the Inditex Group we promote work-life balance, advocating especially for co-responsibility, which we consider to be
both a right and a duty. We work to implement work-life balance measures in all the markets.
In Spain, these measures are enshrined in our Equality Plans. These plans include, among other things, the possibility of
splitting up leave periods for hospital stays or care of relatives up to second degree of kinship, flexible working hours for
adaptation periods at nursery or infant schools, or the extension of leave with job guarantee for personal matters, studies,
international adoption or care of dependent family members, in addition to various improvements with respect to ordinary
legal requirements, such as the extension of breastfeeding leave.
6. Social Information / S1. Own workforce
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information

S1-8_07

## Page 246

Internationally, we strive to improve the rights guaranteed by the local legislation of each country, prioritising those markets
in which the legislation is not especially protective.
/ In the United States, 16 weeks' paid leave for birth, fostering or adoption is available to all of our people who meet certain
minimum requirements. The subsidiary also provides care services for children and adults with special needs, both in care
centres and at home. We also have other benefits that help improve people's well-being and quality of life such as
emotional support programmes, financial and health advice, transport subsidies, and tuition fee subsidies or
reimbursement.
/ We promote measures to broaden the rights enshrined in local legislation, such as flexible working hours, efficient
organisation of teams, extending leave for caring for children and/or dependants and even financial assistance to help
cover the cost of childcare or other care. Markets where such measures are applied include Greece, the United States,
the United Kingdom, Italy, Germany, and France, among others.
/ An increasing number of markets are extending family health coverage, either as a social benefit paid for by the subsidiary
itself or by negotiating more favourable health insurance conditions. In addition to Spain, through its flexible remuneration
plan, markets such as Brazil (with its Baby on Board programme), Canada, Mexico, Greece, Romania and Mainland
China already offer this type of benefit.
The Inditex Group also promotes the right to digital disconnection in the workplace, pursuant to Spain’s Data Protection
Act (Organic Law 3/2018, of 5 December, on the Protection of Personal Data and Guarantee of Digital Rights).
Notwithstanding the commitment acquired at the corporate level, negotiations with workers’ representatives have resulted
in other measures on this front, included both in the equality plans of the logistics companies, and in the Group’s new
Equality Plan, applicable in Spain.
Internationally, France has included the right to disconnect as a measure to improve work-life balance. In turn, in 2024,
markets such as Australia and Slovenia introduced digital disconnection laws.
In other markets where there is no specific legislation in this regard, we implement information and awareness initiatives,
along with technical measures applied to our corporate systems, such as e-mail, to encourage these healthy work habits.
Inditex advocates for all our people to have the social protections regulated in the local legislation of each market. In 2024,
99% of our worldwide workforce was entitled to maternity, paternity or child fostering leave (99% of women and 99% of
men). In this period, 6% of those people took such leave (7% of women and 3% of men).
Below is a breakdown of maternity and paternity leave worldwide:

S1-13. Training and skills development metrics
We want working at Inditex to be more than just a job for our people, and we make it easier for them not only to secure
professional goals and opportunities, but also to find inspiration, innovation and creativity. To achieve this, from the talent
management standpoint we focus on:
/ Attracting the best talent.
/ Providing our people with opportunities for professional development and growth, primarily through internal promotion
and mobility.
/ Providing our workforce with opportunities for continuous learning.
/ Creating a safe and motivating working environment that enable us to retain talented professionals and strengthen their
commitment.
6. Social Information / S1. Own workforce
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
246

S1-15_01;
S1-15_02;
S1-15_03

## Page 247

Thus, from 1 February 2024 to 31 January 2025, more than 3.3 million training hours were imparted to more than 2.5 million
participants (more than 2.7 million hours and more than 2.2 million participants in 2023)34.
Accordingly, in 2024 the number of unique people trained35 was 214,952 (214,187 in 2023), broken down by gender as
155,964 women, 55,418 men, 40 non-binary and 3,530 persons of other/unspecified gender (158,324; 55,183; 46 and 634 in
2023).
This training is part of our drive to develop our workforce and our ongoing work to nurture environments that encourage
lifelong learning.
Our aim is to provide training experiences that help our people to respond optimally to the challenges they face daily. We
are convinced that not only do these experiences build the wealth of skills of our workforce, but they are also a key pillar for
the Company’s sustainable success.
To this end, we offer learning experiences that respond to two needs: on the one hand, the business, offering initiatives
aimed at improving individual or group performance and helping our staff to do their job better; and, on the other hand,
promoting opportunities for our people to learn beyond their current role, to be inspired and encouraged to explore new
perspectives.
Our model is based on in-house training and is eminently practical. Training is mostly delivered by in-house trainers or
developed in collaboration with top external entities when the required knowledge or experience is not available in-house.
In addition, as mentioned above, our learning platform Tra!n offers a wide range of digital content tailored to each individual.
i More information on talent, promotion and training in S1-1. Policies related to own workforce and S1-4. Actions related
to own workforce of this Report.

S1-14. Health and safety metrics
In connection with health and safety protection, since 2014 we have been implementing the ISO 45001:2018 management
system, internationally recognised for its ability to control risks and improve occupational health and safety performance.
In 2024, this system, considered to be the highest standard in health and safety, is being implemented in a total of 29
markets and 336 workplaces, covering 86% of our own workforce. In 2024 we also obtained that certification in the markets
of Kazakhstan, Australia and France, and we maintained certification of our refurbishment and construction works in
Europe, carried out by GOA INVEST, S.A.
