Non-employee accidents
At Inditex, we also look after the health and safety of people who are not part of our staff but who work at the Group’s
workplaces under its supervision. That is why we monitor the occupational accidents of non-employees.
In 2024, seven recordable accidents and health events of unpaid staff were recorded.
S1-10/S1-16. Compensation metrics
Adequate wages
All of our workforce receive adequate wages, according to the analysis based on applicable agreements, legal benchmarks
or recognised methodological sources, such as the Anker methodology.
i More information in the Methodological Annex of this Report.
Average remuneration and gender pay gap
At Inditex we are committed to equal pay for women and men. This commitment rests on a remuneration policy
established in accordance with the value that each person contributes from their professional experience, dedication and
responsibility, with no discrimination on the grounds of gender, age, culture, religion, race or any other characteristic.
Under the framework of the Equality Plan, and taking into account the workforce in Spain, in 2024 the remuneration audit
was renewed in the presence of workers’ representatives. The methodology and calculation of this audit was reviewed at
the time of signing the agreement, through external suppliers. We obtained a verification based on the International
Standard on Assurance Engagements (ISAE) 3000 Revised. This verification guarantees the integrity of the Remuneration
Register and Wage Audit carried out by the Company, the findings of which establish that there is no gender-based pay
discrimination in work of equal value.
Inditex remunerates its team in accordance with the Group’s values, guaranteeing non-discrimination for reasons of
gender, age, culture, religion, race or any other circumstance. Our remuneration policy is therefore determined by the value
that each person contributes through their professional experience, dedication and responsibility.
As a Group with a strong international presence, we strive to ensure that our remuneration policy is adapted to the specific
circumstances of each of the 56 markets in which our teams are located, aligning the standard remuneration with the
benchmark practices in each of these markets in their local currency.
Inditex's remuneration comprises a fixed component and a variable component.
/ Experience, dedication and responsibility within the Company are the factors that determine fixed remuneration.
/ The variable part depends on pre-established, quantifiable and measurable indicators, linked both to the Company’s
results and to the fulfilment of the sustainability objectives of the office employees, thus reinforcing the commitment of our
people in this area.
Based on this criterion, variable remuneration depends solely on objective parameters, and not on a discretionary
assessment of the person's individual performance. This aims to eliminate any room for discrimination. In this regard,
variable remuneration is one of the key components of Inditex’s remuneration policy and applies to employees in all
areas of the Company’s activity.
In our stores, the most widely used variable remuneration system is that of the monthly sales commission scheme. This is a
way to reward the engagement of store employees in key issues as sales results, feedback on products and store
coordination and organisation.
Global average remuneration
Average remuneration is defined as the average wages in the Group —considering the total salary: fixed plus variable,
consisting of commission and bonus— translated into euros, using the average exchange rate in 2024. Based on this
calculation, global average remuneration in this period at Inditex amounted to 30,850 euros gross annually (28,726 euros in
2023).
6. Social Information / S1. Own workforce
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
249

## Page 250

With regard to this figure, it should be highlighted that the number of employees in Spain, our home market, only represents
31% of the headcount, as a significant part of the remaining 69% staff are based in markets where wages translated into
euros give rise to lower average remuneration (30% and 70% of workforce in 2023).
By gender, the average remuneration of women in 2024 amounted to an annual gross figure of 29,694 euros, and that of
men came to 33,932 euros (women: 27,831 euros in 2023; men: 31,196 euros in 2023).
These two figures are not representative in terms of equal pay: the pay gap between men and women is due to a higher
presence of women in a significant number of markets where average remuneration is lower on account of the exchange
rate effect. As explained above, the indicator that provides the most transparency in terms of equal pay for men and women
is the pay gap, which in 2024 was 0.7% in favour of women.

i More information on the methodology for calculating the annual total compensation ratio in the Methodological Annex

Gender pay gap
Inditex is committed to equal pay. For quantification purposes, we use the gender pay gap, which is the indicator that most
reliably represents the real difference in pay between women and men.
The gap is calculated on the basis of the median salary in each market considering the total salary: fixed plus variable,
consisting of commission and bonus.
i More information on the calculation methodology in the Methodological Annex of this Report.
The outcome of the analysis carried out in 2024 shows wage parity between men and women in Inditex. In total salary,
women have been paid 0.7% more than men. The global gender pay gap has varied slightly with respect to previous years
—in 2023, women were paid 0.5% more than men— which is explained by staff turnover in the period.

representing 85%, 9% and 6% of our people in 2024.

S1-17. Incidents, complaints and severe human rights impacts
Our Ethics Line is in place for reporting any type of infringement or irregularity concerning human rights linked to our
people, including potential inherently severe violations such as child labour or forced labour, in accordance with the UN
Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, the ILO Declaration on Fundamental Principles and Rights at Work and/
or the OECD Guidelines for Business. Through this grievance mechanism we monitor all possible cases, handling the
appropriate cases and adopting the corresponding decisions.
In 2024, the Ethics Line (which includes both the Global Ethics Line and the various Local Ethics Lines) recorded a total of
791 cases, of which 280 were related to our people. Of the total number of cases reported in 2024, four cases of
discrimination have been confirmed. However, no inherently severe human rights-related incidents have been confirmed
for our employees.
As far as the National Contact Points for OECD Multinational Enterprises are concerned, no reports have been received,
neither related to our people, nor of any other kind.
This year, no fines, penalties or damages were imposed for discrimination or human rights infringements materialising in
2024 in connection with our people.

i More information on the Ethics Line in section G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct and on the reported
indicators in section G1-4. Communications received through the Ethics Line and in the Methodological Annex of this
Report.

S2. Workers in the value chain
A. Strategy
SBM-3. Material impacts, risks and opportunities
Combining dialogue and commitment to our stakeholders and the analysis of our value chain allows us to identify the
material impacts, risks and opportunities (IROs) linked to the people who work in it.
As a result of this analysis, we identified that our IROs focus mainly on the supply chain.

i More information on stakeholder engagement and our materiality analysis can be found in section SBM-3. Material

Scope
We have assessed the possible impacts of our operations on supply chain workers, so as to take into account everyone
who might be impacted, whether positively or negatively.
The findings of this assessment highlight that the IROs in connection with value chain workers focus primarily on our supply
chain.
Furthermore, our due diligence in connection with human rights allows us to identify the groups in our supply chain that
might be most affected, such as women, migrants and people with disabilities, or those who work in the production of raw
materials. Our Workers at the Centre strategy focuses on these vulnerable groups, making their needs visible and including
them in our actions to guarantee their protection.
i More information on the due diligence process in section GOV-4. Statement on due diligence of this Report.
6. Social Information / S2. Workers in the value chain
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
252

S2.SBM-3_01;
S2.SBM-3_02;
S2.SBM-3_03;
S2.SBM-3_08

## Page 253

Occurrence and types of impacts
Among the negative impacts related to supply chain workers, we have found various patterns of occurrence, linked, for
example, to the realities of each region. That is why we believe it is crucial to understand the context of each one. This is a
task in which we are supported by our supplier clusters, through which we are able to learn about the realities of those
working in our supply chain on the ground. In these clusters we have local teams in direct contact with suppliers, workers,
trade unions, NGOs, local and national authorities, international organisations and academic institutions, etc. In 2024, Inditex
had 10 clusters in Spain, Portugal, Morocco, Türkiye, India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, Vietnam, China and Cambodia.
This work enables us to identify individual impacts, such as those linked to occupational accidents and serious
occupational diseases or workplace harassment; as well as potential systemic impacts, linked to the existence of global and
industry-wide characteristics.
This might be the case, for example, of inadequate work environments or situations of child or forced labour, the risk of
which may increase in certain regions due to specific contextual factors. However, at Inditex we take a systemic approach
to minimising the probability that this type of impact may occur. We therefore have mechanisms in place to prevent both
systemic and individual impacts, implementing mitigation and remediation strategies to address the negative effects once
they have occurred.
In this regard, our approach is based on the Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers and its compliance
programme, as well as our Workers at the Centre strategy and its five Priority Impact Areas (PIA): Social dialogue, Living
wages, Health, Respect and Resilience. Through these, we also endeavour to generate a positive impact, designing
initiatives that allow us to enhance employee quality of life and well-being.
In this context, we underscore our Global Framework Agreement with IndustriALL Global Union, which promotes the
participation of workers and respect for collective bargaining, and also provides key information for our strategy.
In addition, we have devised specific Remediation Plans to protect minors and refugees.
i More information on related actions in section S2-4. Actions related to value chain workers of this Report.
As reflected in our Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers and in our Human Rights Policy, we have zero
tolerance towards child labour and any form of forced or involuntary labour and we are committed to eliminating them.
To ensure compliance with it, we conduct regular audits of our supply chain facilities.
i More information in section G1-2. Management of relationships with suppliers of this Report.
We also work actively to identify the stages of the supply chain that are most susceptible to human rights violations, so as
to prevent them. In this regard, we identify greater vulnerability in areas associated with the cultivation of raw materials such
as cotton. To achieve this, we work hand in hand with the ILO, within the framework of our Public-Private Partnership, for the
promotion and respect of the Fundamental Principles and Rights at Work (FPRW) in cotton-growing communities in India,
Pakistan and Uzbekistan.
Risks and opportunities
By assessing impacts and dependencies, we also determine the possible risks and opportunities for our Company. We
classify as material those risks associated with workers in our supply chain, related to various types of incidents, such as
labour disputes or non-compliance, which is not linked to any specific group of workers.
To address this, we have a number of tools that allow us to anticipate and mitigate the risk, such as our policies, compliance
programmes or Ethics Line.
6. Social Information / S2. Workers in the value chain
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information

## Page 254

B. Impact, risk and opportunity management
S2-1. Policies related to value chain workers
At Inditex, we develop policies that set out the conditions we want our suppliers to pass on to the workers in the value chain,
with the aim of:
/ Fostering respect for human rights in the supply chain.
/ Providing those who work in our supply chain with the tools for their empowerment, participation and well-being.
/ Creating social value in the community in a responsible way and driving industry transformation.
These policies also ensure that the third parties with whom we have dealings are aligned with our commitments. Therefore,
the manufacturers of the products marketed by the Group, as well as the rest of the supplier companies, are obliged to
comply not only with the Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers, but also with the Code of Conduct, insofar as
it applies to them.

Policies related to value chain workers
/ Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers i
/ Human Rights Policy i
/ Code of Conduct i
/ Sustainability Policy i
/ Ethics Line Procedure i
/ Sustainability Stakeholder Relations Policy i
i More information on our policies and their updates in section MDR-P. Policies of this Report.
Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers
At Inditex we have a Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers, which defines minimum standards of ethical and
responsible behaviour that must be observed by all of the Group’s manufacturers and suppliers across the supply chain, in
accordance with our corporate ethical culture, firmly grounded in respect for human rights and sustainability.
Compliance with the Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers is a pre-requisite for a supplier or manufacturer to
form part of our supply chain.
i More information on the Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers in section MDR-P. Policies of this Report.
Respect for human rights in the value chain
Our Human Rights Policy establishes the principles of respect and promotion of all universally recognised human rights,
both labour and non-labour, in our own operations and the value chain.
This Policy is binding for all our workforce as well as for anyone related with the Group, including those who are part of our
value chain. For prioritisation purposes, the Policy also identifies the rights connected with the activities of the Group and
the stakeholders.

i More information on the Human Rights Policy in section MDR-P. Policies of this Report.
In addition, through this policy, underpinned by the Sustainability Policy and the Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and
Suppliers, we promote fair working conditions in our value chain. Specifically:
/ We embrace the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).
/ We promote and respect human rights in our value chain, within the framework of the United Nations Guiding Principles
on Business and Human Rights.
6. Social Information / S2. Workers in the value chain
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
254

S2.MDR-P_01-06,
S2-1_10

S2-1_06

S2-1_01;
S2-1_02

## Page 255

/ We develop measures to prevent, mitigate and remediate negative impacts related to our activity, as well as to the
activities of our business partners.
/ We drive sustainability in our value chain alongside our suppliers, contributing to the improvement of supply chain
workers’ living conditions and to the development of their communities.
Furthermore, our Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers expressly prohibits forced or compulsory labour and
child labour, underscoring:
/ Zero tolerance for any form of modern slavery or human trafficking in both its organisation and its supply chain.
/ The establishment of a minimum age of 16 for recruitment in our value chain, even when local regulations allow for a
lower age.
To guarantee these commitments, we develop mechanisms for collaboration and dialogue with our stakeholders through
the supplier clusters and sales and sustainability teams.
Thus, through the new Sustainability Stakeholder Relations Policy and the update of our Sustainability Policy, we are
prioritising stakeholder engagement on sustainability issues, topics and trends. Accordingly, we undertake, among others,
the commitment to listen and actively talk to our stakeholders, as well as to act responsibly, ethically and transparently in
our relations with them.
We also have partnerships that allow us to complement this dialogue, such as our Framework Agreement with IndustriALL
Global Union and alliances with Action, Collaboration, Transformation (ACT), Ethical Trading Initiative and International
Apparel Federation (IAF).
i More information in section S2-2.Processes for engaging with value chain workers about impacts of this Report
Accordingly, we guarantee the application of the principles of our policies, which are grounded on a commitment to respect
and promote human rights, as outlined in the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights.
In this regard, as can be seen from reading them, we construct our policies and Codes of Conduct in line with international
principles and benchmark standards.
i More information in sections MDR-P. Policies and S1-1. Policies related to own workforce of this Report
Policy-related mechanisms and instruments
To ensure the proper protection of human rights and compliance with our standards, we use grievance mechanisms, such
as our Ethics Line, available to our stakeholders.
i More information in section G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct of this Report.
Additionally, through our Global Framework Agreement with IndustriALL Global Union, we foster social dialogue in our
supply chain and work on dispute resolution mechanisms through the internal communication channel implemented for
this purpose.
To address the impacts we have identified, we enact strategies and measures, such as the Workers at the Centre strategy
or the initiatives of our Compliance Programme, which are aimed at support and remediation in the event of supplier non-
compliance.
Accordingly, to identify and manage potential non-compliances linked to the rights of supply chain workers, we conduct
regular and periodic social audits. These ensure that our standards and the Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and
Suppliers are upheld. In section G1-2 of this Report, we provide the ratio of compliance with the different parts of the Code
by the factories in our supply chain.
i More information in section G1-2. Management of relationships with suppliers of this Report.
6. Social Information / S2. Workers in the value chain

## Page 256

Our Ethics Line also allows us to monitor notifications regarding non-compliances, thus enabling us to identify any gaps in
our Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers and Human Rights Policy, and to activate the necessary
mechanisms to manage them.
S2-2. Processes for engaging with value chain workers about impacts
We believe that the relationship with the people who are part of our value chain is fundamental to correctly manage the
actual and potential impacts that may affect them.
To this end, we have different relationship and dialogue mechanisms to listen to their views and take them into account in
our decision making, adapting our strategies to suit their needs.
Through our ten supplier clusters, we are able to generate local spaces for dialogue and collaboration on the ground,
allowing us to learn the perspectives and needs of these groups and inform our strategies.
It is important to highlight the audit processes we regularly conduct at our manufacturers and suppliers. During the course
of these audits, our teams are in direct contact with people working in our supply chain through interviews with them and
their representatives.
i More information on our supplier clusters in section G1-2. Management of relationships with suppliers of this Report.
Another of our main tools in relation to our supply chain workers is our Global Framework Agreement with IndustriALL
Global Union, which we signed in 2007 and renewed in 2019.
This Global Framework Agreement allows us to foster worker participation and respect for the rights of freedom of
association and collective bargaining. It also gives us a crucial direct listening channel to inform our strategies, enabling us
to:
/ Manage incident at any workplace in the supply chain by fostering social dialogue.
/ Facilitate the involvement of our supply chain workers’ legitimate representatives.
We have also established mechanisms for joint access to workplaces as well as for managing disputes at our supply chain
as part of the Global Framework Agreement with IndustriALL.

i More information on our Global Framework Agreement with IndustriALL Global Union in section S2-4. Actions related
to value chain workers of this Report.
In addition to this tool, we also have our Ethics Line for reporting any potential non-compliance with our policies and codes.
i More information on the Ethics Committee in section G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct of this Report
Lastly, our double materiality assessment, which we update every year, allows us to incorporate the perspectives of expert
organisations. This means we can set up a channel for communications with our stakeholders regarding matters that are
important to the people in our supply chain and the potential impacts that may affect them.

i More information on stakeholder engagement and our materiality assessment in sections SBM-2. Interests and views
of stakeholders, SBM-3. Material impacts, risks and opportunities and IROs. Disclosures on the materiality assessment
process of this Report.

## Page 257

As a result of these collaborative efforts, we have agreements with various organisations such as our Public-Private
Partnership with the International Labour Organization and the Global Framework Agreement with IndustriALL Global
Union.
Additionally, our collaborations are embodied in various actions as part of the Workers at the Centre strategy, which we
evaluate by monitoring each project using general and/or specific indicators, depending on the initiatives.
We also gauge the effectiveness of our efforts by conducting audits of our suppliers and manufacturers. Lastly, our clusters
are a key tool in determining the effectiveness of our collaborations because of their ongoing communication with local
Revstakeholders.
The Chief Sustainability Officer oversees the implementation of the sustainability strategy, including stakeholder
engagement, in particular with people working in our supply chain and suppliers.

i More information in section GOV-2. Information provided to governing bodies and sustainability matters addressed of
this Report.
Focus on vulnerable groups
Although we consider all people in the supply chain, we develop a special focus on particularly vulnerable groups.
We have therefore introduced in our Workers at the Centre strategy a specific focus for women, migrants and people with
disabilities, or workers involved in the production of raw materials. In this way we aim to listen to those people who are least
represented and who may be most affected.
S2-3. Processes to remediate negative impacts and channels for value
chain workers to raise concerns
Processes to remediate negative impacts
The identification of potential and actual negative impacts related to our activity allows us to increase our capacity to
prevent, mitigate and remediate their effects. That is why we endeavour to assess the impacts linked to those working in our
supply chain.
To achieve this we implement due diligence processes and we activate grievance mechanisms, which help us detect
potential negative impacts and introduce the necessary measures to prevent, mitigate and remedy their effects.
i More information in section GOV-4. Statement on due diligence of this Report.
Our main remediation tools are:
/ The projects covered by the Workers at the Centre strategy —which focus on managing the impacts identified in our
due diligence process in human rights— allowing us to implement initiatives aligned with our Priority Impact Areas.
i More information on the Workers at the Centre strategy in section S2-4. Actions related to value chain workers of this
Report.
/ Corrective Action Plans (CAPs), part of our compliance programme, through which we develop roadmaps to support
suppliers to correct and prevent any non-compliance with our Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers that is
detected through social audits.
i More information on Corrective Action Plans (CAPs) in section G1-2. Management of relationships with suppliers of this
Report.
/ Specific Remediation Plans for protection against child labour and the safeguarding of refugees, focused on the
special protection of these vulnerable groups.
/ Remediation measures carried out in the framework of our collaboration with IndustriALL Global Union.
6. Social Information / S2. Workers in the value chain
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
257

S2-2_04

S2-2_06

S2-2_07

S2-3_01

## Page 258

Main communication and grievance mechanisms
Value chain workers have access to the Ethics Line, which is available to all our stakeholders. The procedure for managing
communications received from value chain workers through the Ethics Line and ensuring the channel's efficacy is the same
as for those received from other stakeholders and is outlined in the relevant chapter of this Report.
i More information on how the Ethics Line works, including monitoring, control and assurance of its effectiveness, in
section G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct of this Report.
To ensure that supply chain workers are aware of our Ethics Line, in addition to including information and facilitating their
access via the corporate website, the Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers explicitly designates this channel
as a preferred and confidential channel available to them. This code, which must be accepted in order to be able to work
with Inditex, requires manufacturers and suppliers to:
/ Post the Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers, translated into the local language, on their premises.
/ Appoint a management representative who is responsible for the implementation of the Code of Conduct for
Manufacturers and Suppliers.
Compliance with the requirements of the Code, including it is available to workers, is verified through social audits we carry
out regularly at our manufacturers and suppliers.
Furthermore, workers can also use external grievance channels designated by the competent authorities.
Our Framework Agreement with IndustriALL Global Union also constitutes a specific communication and contact
mechanism, reinforcing the other available grievance mechanisms. Its functioning is articulated through the relationship
with local affiliates, the legitimate workers' representatives, who channel their concerns via an escalation process developed
between Inditex and IndustriALL with the aim of promoting social dialogue.
For a better understanding of the main points of the Global Framework Agreement, we provided training for local affiliates in
different countries. This strengthens the knowledge of the people working in our supply chain regarding this mechanism, so
as to foster social dialogue in our supply chain as a way of solving any incident that may be reported to Inditex through our
Global Framework Agreement with IndustriALL.
S2-4. Actions related to value chain workers
We strive to implement practices and strategies that minimise any potential impacts related to our activity that could affect
workers in our supply chain.
Consequently, we are committed to implementing responsible purchasing practices to avoid potential impacts on the
working conditions, wages or benefits of workers.
In addition, we conduct thorough human rights due diligence, supported by an ongoing exercise to identify and prioritise
potential human rights impacts.
i More information on our due diligence regarding human rights in section GOV-4. Statement on due diligence of this
Report.
Through this due diligence exercise, in addition to our materiality assessment, we identify the potential impacts that our
activity may have on our value chain workers.
This assessment enables us to identify the actions needed to prevent, mitigate and remediate these impacts, as well as to
set up strategies to transfer the positive effect to our supply chain.
To manage our supply chain impacts, we take a collaborative approach to global and local implementation, from a systemic
perspective. To this end, we developed a number of tools, including:
/ Workers at the Centre strategy: it aims to strengthen the protection of human rights and incorporates an approach to
transformation and impact into our relationship with the people working in the supply chain.

## Page 259

This strategy is structured into five PIAs (Social dialogue, Living wages, Respect, Health and Resilience) with specific targets
and lines of action that are materialised through partnerships, solutions and initiatives on the ground, developed by our
teams or along with partner organisations. In their design and development we apply the Theory of Change to launch
transformative initiatives on the basis of the expected benefit for people.
/ Programme for Compliance with the Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers: it ensures compliance with
Inditex’s regulations regarding our supply chain, enabling us to trigger action mechanisms in the event of non-
compliances. Specifically, this programme is structured through audits, Corrective Action Plans (CAPs) and training,
among other measures.
i More information on the Compliance Programme in section G1-2. Management of relationships with suppliers of this
Report.
We also have specific Remediation Plans for protection against child labour and the protection of refugees, which
include special measures for prevention, detection and remediation in cases of child, forced or irregular labour, by
providing support and remediation for the affected person.
6. Social Information / S2. Workers in the value chain

Workers at the Centre
The Workers at the Centre 2023-2025 strategy is based on respect for the human rights of the workers in the supply chain. Our goal is
to reach three million people by 2025.
Priority Impact Areas
Elements of the strategy
/ Due Diligence
A continuous process, based on the UN Guiding Principles
on Business and Human Rights, allowing us to identify five
Priority Impact Areas. These areas are synergetic and
interconnected.
/ Transformation
Transformation is a slow process and requires the
commitment and collaboration of various parties. Our
Workers at the Centre strategy connects us to the present
and the challenges it poses, without losing sight of our
ultimate goal, which is to transform the industry and
communities.
/ Equal opportunities as our focus
We make more visible and integrate the needs of the most
vulnerable groups, including women, migrants and refugees.
/ Solutions
Priority Impact Areas develop, support and combine different
types of solutions and interventions to pursue their
objectives: partnerships, collaborations with different
stakeholders, direct interventions with suppliers or
community outreach, among others.
All the approaches complement each other and, at different
paces and scales, contribute to and are part of implementing
the strategy.
Social dialogue
Facilitating mature industrial relations as a
vehicle for a more participative society.
Living wages

Living Wages in the Inditex supply chain.
Respect
Promoting safe and respectful environments,
free from discrimination, abuse or harassment.
Health
Protecting the health and safety of workers in
the supply chain, and improving their well-

transformative capacities for development.

6. Social Information / S2. Workers in the value chain
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
260

## Page 261

Our Workers at the Centre strategy, whose new cycle started in 2023 and will end in 2025, is based on the respect and
promotion of human rights and the creation of social value.
For this reason, the actions carried out under our strategy go beyond mitigation and/or remediation of potential negative
impacts, with initiatives aimed at generating positive change for our supply chain workers and their communities.
In this way, we aim to have a diverse, inclusive and safe value chain for workers, made up of committed suppliers and
manufacturers, while ensuring decent conditions and adequate levels of well-being.
Collaboration and support
To achieve this, at Inditex we believe in the culture of collaboration as a means to leverage our effect and achieve a lasting
impact. On this regard, we implement measures aimed at our value chain workers in partnership with local and international
expert organisations such as ACT (Action, Collaboration, Transformation), Ethical Trading Initiative (ETI), IndustriALL Global
Union, International Accord, the International Labour Organization (ILO), Reimagining Industry to Support Equality (RISE)
and many others.
Our local teams, who are present in our main supplier markets, play a key role in the management and implementation of
our actions.
Likewise, we believe in supporting our suppliers to address supply chain challenges as a shared responsibility. That is why
we provide continuous and specialised training on issues such as gender equality, freedom of association, living wages,
and health and safety.
One example of this is our EVOLVES initiative which, through direct collaboration with suppliers and manufacturers, is
aimed at developing facility management systems to furnish them with efficient tools and operating practices that
guarantee and reinforce respect for the rights of supply chain workers.
Likewise, the close relationship between our purchasing and sustainability teams and our suppliers is crucial. We have local
teams in our main production markets, our clusters. This allows us to pay special attention to the context of these regions.
This ongoing communication and collaboration is crucial in the design and implementation phases of our actions within the
Workers at the Centre strategy.
We work with key players like the international trade union federation IndustriALL, with which we signed a Global
Framework Agreement in 2007. An important tool for promoting worker participation and respect for the rights of freedom
of association and collective bargaining.
Similarly, we work with Shift, a leading organisation in human rights and business, in the framework of the
conceptualisation of the due diligence exercise. The analysis of this exercise, along with other information sources, allows
us to develop the Priority Impact Areas of our strategy.
i More information on the strategy in the report Workers at the Centre, available on Inditex’s corporate website.
Priority Impact Areas of the Workers at the Centre strategy
Social dialogue
Related IROs: [S2.I1; S2.I3; S2.R1; S3.I1; S3.I2; S3.R1]
We see social dialogue as a tool to help us foster more mature industrial relations as a vehicle for a more participatory
society.
To achieve this, we have a range of partnerships and collaborations and we work on two main lines of action: the
participation of workers and dialogue ecosystems.
Worker participation
We have endeavoured to promote communication and dispute resolution mechanisms like the internal communication channel of
the Global Framework agreement; the Interim Dispute Resolution Mechanism in Bangladesh relating to freedom of association
and the payment of wages and benefits for workers under the ACT initiative or the International ACCORD’s Complaints
Mechanism, among others.
6. Social Information / S2. Workers in the value chain

Main initiatives in  2024:
/ Implementation of the Global Framework Agreement with IndustriALL Global Union (global).
/ Training on the Global Framework Agreement (Bangladesh).
/ Training for local affiliates of IndustriALL concerning the mechanisms of the Global Framework Agreement (Cambodia,
Türkiye).
Ecosystems for dialogue
We also aim to empower the representatives of workers and employers through social dialogue platforms at the local or
industry level. For this purpose we work actively on representation mechanisms and raising awareness regarding industrial
relations and other spheres such as occupational health and safety or women’s empowerment.
Main initiatives in 2024:

/ Collaboration through the ACT initiative (Bangladesh, Cambodia, Türkiye).
/ Better Work programme (Bangladesh, Cambodia, Egypt, Indonesia, Pakistan, Vietnam).
/ Programme for the promotion of social dialogue (India).
/ Improving operational grievance mechanisms  (Türkiye).
/ Training on improving employee relations  (Cambodia, China).
Results of the social dialogue PIA in 2024:
/ 670,281 people reached, which brings the figure to 741,076 people reached since the start of this strategy cycle in 2023.
/ 325 suppliers and factories involved.
/ Key markets: Bangladesh, Cambodia, China, Egypt, India, Indonesia, Pakistan, Türkiye, Vietnam.
Contribution to the SDGs

Living wages
Related IROs: [S2.I1; S2.I2; S2.R1; S3.I1; S3.I2; S3.R1]
In our view, paying workers living wages can only be achieved by means of effective collaboration at industry level between
workers' representatives and employers, complemented by responsible purchasing practices.
Necessary conditions for collective bargaining
We work to foster collective bargaining agreements at industry level, underpinned by improvements in responsible
purchasing practices.
Main initiatives in  2024:

/ Collaboration through the ACT initiative (Bangladesh, Cambodia, Türkiye).
/ Implementation of the Global Framework Agreement with IndustriALL Global Union (global).
Responsible purchasing practices
Responsible purchasing practices can help create an environment that is conducive to improving working conditions,
wages and benefits for workers in the supply chain.
That is why we are adhered to the ACT’s five commitments in relation to purchasing practices and we train our sales teams
to make responsible purchasing decisions, providing them with a management system that measures the sustainability
performance of each supplier and factory.
Main initiatives in  2024:

/ Responsible purchasing practices with ACT.
/ Training buyers at the Inditex Sustainable Fashion School.
6. Social Information / S2. Workers in the value chain

## Page 263

Management systems and favourable practices
We opt to take a holistic approach, including creating an ecosystem conducive to favourable conditions —skills
enhancement, increased productivity, effective wage management systems, financial literacy, etc.— and lays a solid
foundation for the payment of living wages to supply chain workers.
Main initiatives in  2024:

/ Wage management project (China).
/ Lean Project (Bangladesh, Cambodia, China, Morocco, Portugal, Vietnam).
/ Look Through Another Window project (Türkiye).
/ RISE Digital project (Pakistan).
/ RISE Financial Health project (Cambodia, Egypt, Vietnam).
Results of the living wages PIA in  2024:
/ 86,268 people reached, which brings the figure to 100,454 people reached since the start of this strategy cycle in 2023.
/ 67 suppliers and factories involved.
/ Key markets: Bangladesh, Cambodia, China, Egypt, Morocco, Pakistan, Portugal,Türkiye, Vietnam.
Contribution to the SDGs

Respect
Related IROs: [S2.I5; S2.R1; S3.I1; S3.I2; S3.R1]
We aim to promote safe and respectful environments, free from discrimination, abuse or harassment.
To achieve this, we endeavour to create respectful work environments free from any kind of violence and harassment,
along with a fair recruitment and employment culture.
Respectful work environments free from any kind of violence and harassment
ILO Convention No. 190 and Recommendation No. 206 are the first international labour standards that provide a common
framework to prevent, remediate and eradicate violence and harassment in the world of work, including gender-based
violence and harassment. We promote, support, collaborate and implement all necessary measures to foster engagement
in these areas.
Main initiatives in 2024:

/ Training on respect and equality (Türkiye).
/ Training on the development of zero tolerance policies towards violence (Türkiye).
/ Working parent support programme  (China).
/ EDUCARE project (Morocco).
/ Harmony project (Türkiye).
/ LGBTQI+ Awareness project (Portugal).
/ Parwaaz project - Disability management in the workplace (Pakistan).
/ RISE Respect project (Bangladesh, India).
/ Together Strong project (Türkiye).
/ Workplace Adaptation project (Türkiye).
Fair recruitment and employment culture
Having fair recruitment and employment practices is key to achieving decent working conditions. At Inditex, we take a zero
tolerance policy towards child labour and any kind of forced labour, issues that are reflected in our Code of Conduct for
Manufacturers and Suppliers.
We also have a supply chain management system in place that includes aspects such as audits, corrective plans,
awareness-raising, etc. They allow us to identify and remediate potential violations, and to adopt the necessary action if any
breaches are detected.
6. Social Information / S2. Workers in the value chain

Health
Related IROs: [S2.I4; S2.R1; S3.I1; S3.I2; S3.R1]
We aim to protect the health and safety of supply chain workers and improve their well-being, through three lines of action:
physical health, workplace safety and well-being.
To achieve this, we work with various organisations such as International Accord for Health and Safety in the Textile and
Garment Industry, Medicus Mundi Sur, RMG Sustainability Council (RSC), St. John’s Medical College and the University of
Oxford.
Physical health
We continue our commitments and initiatives aimed at reinforcing the evaluation, support and improvement of compliance
with our Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers.
Main initiatives in 2024:

/ OHS Corrective Action Plan (global).
/ Project in collaboration with Medicus Mundi (Morocco).
Workplace safety
We work to promote safe and adequate facilities and workplaces for workers, as well as to provide training for both
employees and management to effectively manage workplace safety.
Main initiatives in 2024:

/ Accord-CAP Remediation project (Bangladesh, Pakistan).
/ SAFER project (China, Morocco, Vietnam).
/ Boiler safety project (Morocco).
/ Suraksha: fire & electrical system assessment + thermography project (India).
Well-being
We believe that general well-being should be addressed in the supply chain by taking care of mental, emotional and even
social health, safeguarding a workplace culture of well-being and fostering the dissemination of knowledge. Accordingly, we
encourage open communication on matters relating to health and safety issues and we promote safe and healthy
behaviours.
Main initiatives in 2024:

/ SAKHI Menstrual Rights project (India).
/ Suraksha: Safety Management Systems (SMS) project (India).

Occupational Health and Safety Corrective Action Plans
In 2024, we have strengthened our commitment to this initiative, broadening the scope of suppliers and
factories to be involved and stepping up efforts and resources to improve health and safety conditions in
our supply chain, thereby ensuring greater compliance with our CCMS and the applicable legislation.
In this regard, we design our Corrective Action Plans in collaboration with suppliers or manufacturers,
analysing the necessary improvements in each individual case. Subsequently, a specialised team carries
out ongoing monitoring of their implementation, providing guidance to the supplier or manufacturer,
overseeing progress, and verifying the improvements implemented.
Results of the Health PIA in 2024:
/ 1,313,217 people reached, which brings the figure to 1,694,407 people reached since the start of this strategy cycle in 2023.
/ 1,806 suppliers and factories involved.
/ Key markets: Bangladesh, China, India, Morocco, Pakistan,  Vietnam.
Contribution to the SDGs

Resilience
Related IROs: [S2.I1; S2.R1; S3.I1; S3.I2; S3.R1]
We want to contribute to generating preventive, adaptive and transformative capacities to ensure prosperous livelihoods
and create more resilient suppliers and communities.
Social protection
We are engaged in ensuring adequate and sufficient social security access in the supply chain, by periodically assessing
the compliance performance of factories and suppliers and, if necessary, implementing and monitoring corrective action
plans.
Main initiatives in 2024:

/ Employment Injury Scheme (EIS) pilot programme in Bangladesh (Bangladesh).
/ Happiness for All (India).
Just transition and the future of work
We want to ensure that a human rights-based approach is systematically incorporated into our climate commitments. In
this way, human rights experience and practice are combined with environmental plans and actions, thereby ensuring that
the social impact is taken into account. We also strive to understand how jobs and skills are going to evolve.
Main initiatives in 2024:

/ Rise Foundations project (China, Vietnam).
Prosperous livelihoods
We focus primarily on the livelihoods of people involved in the production of raw materials, including those in the
agricultural sector. To address these challenges, we apply a holistic approach that encompasses traceability, due diligence
and a commitment to respect for and promotion of human and labour rights.
Main initiatives in 2024:

/ Public-private partnership with the International Labour Organization (India, Pakistan, Uzbekistan).
/ Child & Women Friendly Mobile Areas project (Türkiye).
/ Collaboration projects for promoting regenerative agricultural practices (India).

Results of the Resilience PIA in  2024:

Contribution to the SDGs

Further considerations
The financial resources linked to implementing these actions, do not imply a material amount on their own. They are
included under Operating Expenses in the Consolidated Income Statement (see Note 6 of the Consolidated Annual
Accounts).
In this regard, we highlight the role of our local teams, who are present in the main supplier markets, in the management
and implementation of our actions.
We monitor our actions in detail to examine and validate their effectiveness.
To this end, we have common indicators for all the actions, such as the number of people reached, and individual indicators
applicable to each type of initiative depending on its nature, which we obtain through different methods such as surveys of
the supply chain workers.
In addition, in some cases we also undertake monitoring visits to assess the correct implementation of the projects. In 2024,
we conducted 776 of these monitoring visits (821 visits in 2023).
In the process of analysing and monitoring our impacts, we pay special attention to the potential materialisation of their
effects, in order to establish preventive measures and, should they occur, mitigation measures.
In those cases where we identify that the impact has occurred or is occurring, we activate specific action plans that allow
us to:
/ Minimise the negative effect on people in the supply chain.
/ Endeavour to remediate it as much as possible.
/ Apply a dual perspective of prevention to minimise the chances of its recurring in the future.
An example of this is our Remediation Plans, which are activated if a potential incident of child, forced or irregular labour is
detected.
Remediation Plans
/ Child labour remediation plan: we implement a mandatory process that is triggered if a worker under the age established
in our Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers is found. From the moment it is activated, suppliers and factories
are responsible for developing and implementing a series of feasible corrective actions, as well as providing schooling
opportunities for the child and paying the child a living allowance until the minimum working age is reached or,
alternatively, employing an adult family member in place of the child, among other measures aimed at reducing the
likelihood of the child returning to the world of work. In addition, to ensure compliance, our teams continuously monitor
compliance, also in collaboration with external expert organisations.
/ Refugee and migrant remediation plans: they are carried out if our assessments identify migrant or refugee workers in
any of the production facilities with whom we work. In these plans we assess the working and living conditions of these
workers in order to provide support to them and their families if necessary, in collaboration with expert NGOs.
In addition to the aforementioned support visits, within the framework of our Compliance Programme we conduct social
audits regularly and without previous notice of the specific date of the audit to all suppliers and manufacturers in our supply
chain, following both our own methodology and that of the Social & Labor Convergence Program (SLCP).
These audits allow us to verify compliance with our Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers and consist of an
unannounced site visit to verify wage rates, working hours, health and safety conditions, among other requirements.
Depending on the degree of compliance, suppliers and manufacturers receive a social rating.
6. Social Information / S2. Workers in the value chain
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information

## Page 267

Accordingly, we use Corrective Action Plans (CAPs) to support suppliers in their improvement processes and help them to
correct and prevent non-compliances. However, we maintain a zero tolerance policy with those who do not show a
willingness to improve, and so if the final verification audit shows critical non-compliances at the end of the CAP, Inditex’s
business relationship with the factory or supplier will be terminated.
These social audits and Corrective Action Plans allow us to monitor and control potential incidents regarding supply chain
workers’ rights. We report the results of these audits grouped by theme in this Report.
i More information in section G1-2. Management of relationships with suppliers of this Report.
We also work to identify those stages of our supply chain that are most susceptible to potential human rights violations, in
order to multiply our prevention efforts on them. This has led us to identify greater vulnerability in the raw material cultivation
phase. For this reason, we work hand in hand with the International Labour Organization (ILO), within the framework of our
Public-Private Partnership, for the promotion and respect of the Fundamental Principles and Rights at Work (FPRW) in India,
Pakistan and Uzbekistan.
Furthermore, value chain workers also have access to the Ethics Line for reporting any type of infringement or irregularity
concerning human rights. In 2024, no inherently severe incidences human rights violations in our supply chain have been
detected through this channel.
i More information in section G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct of this Report.
The implementation of these actions and our Compliance Programme also play an important role in minimising our
material risks, by reducing our risks relating to supply chain workers.
On the other hand, regarding opportunities, we continue to work to deepen our analysis in order to be able to provide more
insight in future reporting exercises. In 2024 we have not identified any material opportunities in relation to our supply chain.
Agreements and collaborations
At Inditex we understand that our sustainability targets for the supply chain are shared by other stakeholders. Accordingly,
we take and open and collaborative approach with a range of organisations and we actively participate in the industry's
global initiatives as the driving force for transformation. In this regard, we carry out our actions aimed at our value chain
workers in partnership with local and international expert organisations. These include ACT (Action, Collaboration,
Transformation), Ethical Trading Initiative (ETI), IndustriALL Global Union, International Accord, the International Labour
Organization (ILO), Reimagining Industry to Support Equality (RISE), Medicus Mundi and many others.
i More information in the report Partnerships on Inditex’s corporate website.
6. Social Information / S2. Workers in the value chain
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information

C. Metrics and targets
S2-5. Targets related to value chain workers
• Reaching  three million people with the Workers at the Centre strategy
We have set ourselves the target of reaching three million people through our Workers at the Centre strategy between 2023
and 2025.
This target is linked to our Human Rights Policy and its commitment to play an active role in promoting human rights and to
proactively work towards their respect, both in our own operations and in the value chain.
Target: reaching three million people with the Workers at the Centre strategy by
2025
Current period
Target year
2023-2024
2025
People reached since 2023
2.2 million
3 million
For this purpose, as with our Workers at the Centre strategy of which it is a part, we aim to guarantee respect for the human
and labour rights of our supply chain workers and to promote their well-being and that of their communities. To achieve this
we design various projects based on the Theory of Change methodology in each of the PIAs with specific targets set.
i More information in the report Workers at the Centre, available on Inditex’s corporate website.
A key factor in designing our strategy, targets and actions is close continuous collaboration and dialogue with specialist
organisations and leading players in our value chain.
Through constant communication with these organisations, we are also able to track how effective our actions are and
define new areas for improvement to fulfil our strategies and goals.
Moreover, the Global Framework Agreement with IndustriALL provides a direct communication channel with workers in our
supply chain through their representatives, giving us valuable insight into their needs and our performance.
i More information on the scope, methodology and calculation changes in the year in the Methodological Annex of this
Report.

S3. Affected communities
A. Strategy
SBM-3. Material impacts, risks and opportunities
At Inditex we see the community as one of our stakeholders, and our relationship model encompasses those people or
entities that are a part of the environment where we operate, represented by NGOs, governments and public authorities,
academic institutions, media and civil society. Bearing this in mind, we have compiled the following list of material impacts,
risks and opportunities related to communities.
Material Impacts, Risks and Opportunities related to communities
TYPE
(I/R/O)
PHASE OF THE VALUE
CHAIN (1)
AFFECTED POLICIES
S3.I1

investment

/ Extended value chain

/ Code of Conduct
/ Code of Conduct for
Manufacturers and Suppliers

S3.R1

/ Extended value chain
(1) The extended value chain comprises the environment and the communities in which we are present even if they are not directly linked
to our activity.
i More information on stakeholder engagement and our materiality assessment in sections SBM-3. Material impacts,
risks and opportunities and IROs. Disclosures on the materiality assessment process of this Report.
Scope
In our assessment, we take into consideration the community, bearing in mind the various ways in which it might be
affected by our activity. Consequently, we identify those people who might be impacted by our activity, whether positively or
negatively.
Accordingly, we take into consideration the communities linked to our own operations (near our offices, logistics centres,
etc.), as well as to our value chain, including from the extraction of raw materials to the product’s end-of-life management.
We also take into consideration those groups whose specific characteristics make them more vulnerable to potential
negative impacts. This is the case, for example, of women, children, migrants and refugees, people with disabilities, people
at risk of exclusion, indigenous communities and others.
We have also identified a potential vulnerability in connection with the processes of extraction of the raw materials used in
our products. We therefore continue to work on improving traceability at the industry level of the materials used to make the
products. The aim being to minimise and be able to remediate any negative impacts on communities’ rights from our
supply chain activities.
6. Social Information / S3. Affected communities
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
269

S3.SBM-3_01;
S3.SBM-3_02;
S3.SBM-3_03;
S3.SBM-3_07

## Page 270

However, our community investment strategy is not only confined to those communities most closely linked to our activity,
but also encompasses other communities, less directly affected by our operations or those of our value chain, but whom
we also want to support through our programmes.
Occurrence and types of impacts
Negative impacts related to the community may have a range of causes, linked to situations such as potential breaches of
cultural rights or more systemic ones such as the potential adverse impact of our activities on the communities’ right to a
healthy environment.
As part of our commitment, we have implemented mechanisms to prevent both kinds of possible impacts and, should they
occur, address them. In this regard, we respect the rights of communities when implementing our actions and we have
expanded initiatives such as Workers at the Centre with the aim of transferring their effect not only to value chain workers
but to their communities. This is especially significant in communities linked to the production of raw materials.
Furthermore, we implement measures to ensure respect for human rights, including the cultural rights of communities. We
have an internal team that is responsible for ensuring that our products are developed in keeping with these rights.
Moreover, we consider our indirect impacts on communities, such as those relating to the environment. In this connection,
we carry out initiatives to promote respect for people’s right to live in a healthy environment.
At Inditex we are committed to generating a positive impact on the community as a whole. To do so, we design initiatives
that enable us to transfer and generate value for people by creating direct, indirect and induced employment as a result of
our activity, fostering an improvement in living standards and improving communities’ well-being.
In this regard, we have a community investment strategy, whereby we carry out numerous projects with different focuses.
Through this programme we contribute to sustainable development beyond our value chain and industry.
In this sense, we highlight our support to initiatives aimed to safeguard the lives, health and well-being of forced migrants
and refugees, as well as other vulnerable groups facing humanitarian emergency situations, such as natural disasters,
armed conflicts or similar circumstances.
i More information on related actions in section S3-4. Actions related to communities of this Report.
Risks and opportunities
By analysing our impacts and dependencies we can identify those material risks and opportunities for our Company arising
from the community.
This in turn allows us to implement initiatives that help us prevent, mitigate and remediate potential negative effects before
they occur. In this regard, we consider members of the community as legitimate users of the Ethics Line, so that they can
report any infringement or irregularity related to their rights. Through this grievance mechanism we can monitor, manage
cases,make appropriate decisions  and take the necessary measures, thereby helping to prevent the potential risk of
sanctions and compensation due to rights-related breaches or incidents.
The risks and opportunities identified relate to the community as a whole; no specific dependence on any particular group
has been identified.
B. Impact, risk and opportunity management
S3-1. Policies related to communities
Our policies concerning the management of community impacts, risks and opportunities, apply to all affected persons,
regardless of their proximity to our own operations or value chain.
6. Social Information / S3. Affected communities

Policies related to the community
/ Community Investment Policy i
/ Human Rights Policy i
/ Ecosystems and Biodiversity Policy i
/ Code of Conduct i

/ Sustainability Stakeholder Relations Policy i
i More information on our policies and their updates in section MDR-P. Policies of this Report.
In particular, we highlight the role of our Community Investment Policy, which helps us to regulate our strategy’s projects
focused on creating a lasting impact in the community from strategic support for long-term projects.
Community Investment Policy
Approved by the Board of Directors in June 2014 and last updated on 3 May 2023
Key contents
/ Committed improvement of community wellbeing,  maximisation of community value generation and construction of
strategic alliances
/ Alignment across the community initiatives pursued within the Group and collaboration channel for participation in
community initiatives
/ Framework of priority activity areas (education, emergency relief and environment)
Scope
/ The entire Group (companies and individuals)

given to
stakeholders
/ Participation of the Social Advisory Board
Respect for community human rights
At Inditex we are committed to respecting community human rights, a commitment enshrined in our Sustainability Policy,
and, more extensively, in our Human Rights Policy. Likewise, we want our activity to contribute to the development of a fair
society, in balance with the planetary boundaries.
Hence, in our Code of Conduct, pursuant to our relationship with our surrounding environment, we commit to:
/ Promoting the sustainable development of the communities and areas in which we operate, by fostering voluntary
initiatives and projects.
/ Collaborating with communities and organisations related to our Group.
Along these lines, our Human Rights Policy advocates the special protection of communities. It establishes as a principle
the fostering and promotion of human rights in the local, national or international communities in which the Group conducts
its business. In particular, the policy underscores our explicit commitment to upholding the rights of local communities by
respecting their laws, culture and customs; open dialogue with stakeholders and attention to the most vulnerable groups.
Likewise, our Sustainability Policy reflects our aim to nurture the well-being of people and communities, promoting
multiculturalism and recognising the importance of the right to a clean, healthy and sustainable environment.
6. Social Information / S3. Affected communities
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
271

S3-1_02

MDR-P_01;
MDR-P_02;
MDR-P_03;
MDR-P_04;
MDR-P_05

## Page 272

Our policies are based on our commitment to respect and promote all internationally recognised human rights, as set out in
the United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, and to promote human rights in the communities in
which we operate. In this regard, as can be seen from their details, we construct our policies in line with international
principles and benchmark standards.
Importantly also, our commitment to respecting community human rights includes indigenous communities, in line with
our Sustainability Policy. This is also explicitly stated in our Ecosystems and Biodiversity Policy, which sets out Inditex's
commitment to responsible sourcing, production and other practices in the value chain that respect and protect the human
rights of the community, with a particular emphasis on the rights of indigenous peoples.
Moreover, the new Sustainability Stakeholder Relations Policy addresses the incorporation of stakeholders into the
relevant topics and issues, committing to active listening and dialogue.
In order to implement these principles, as well as other commitments emanating from our policies with respect to the
community, we engage in active dialogue with the individuals who make up the community through the direct relations with
the various NGOs with which we work. These include our long-term partners Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF), the Red
Cross, Water.org and others.
We also pursue this relationship through the work of our Social Advisory Board and the Sponsorships and Patronage
Committee, which is directly linked to our community investment strategy.
Policy-related mechanisms and instruments
We are supported by our grievance mechanisms to improve our due diligence process, helping us to identify potential
negative impacts, and allowing us to respond to risks in advance. One such mechanism is our Ethics Line, available to
people in the community for them to convey, among other things, instances of infringements of their rights.
i More information on the Ethics Line in section G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct of this Report.
We monitor cases handled by this channel as a means of tracking infringements of the community’s human rights, should
these arise.
In 2024 no cases of non-compliance with the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, the ILO Declaration on
Fundamental Principles and Rights at Work, or the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises were identified through
the Ethics Line, our main grievance mechanism.
i More information on the cases handled by the Ethics Line in section G1-4.  Communications received through the Ethics
Line of this Report.
S3-2. Processes for engaging with communities
At Inditex, we believe that our relationship with and commitment to people in the community is key to properly manage the
actual and potential impacts on them.
This allows us to tailor our strategies and more accurately design our community initiatives, in particular the actions
pursuant to our community investment strategy, to ensure that our model is optimised to best serve the community’s real
needs.
Moreover, at Inditex we see respect for human rights as a whole, including indigenous communities in this paradigm. Our
relationship with local and international organisations is crucial to our understanding of these communities and the possible
impacts of our activity might have on them.
In this line, we highlight the following specific tools of engagement:
/ Commitments to community-based organizations
Given the complexity of establishing a direct relationship with an entire community, our model is based on collaboration
with community representatives, such as NGOs and other local and international organisations.
6. Social Information / S3. Affected communities
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
272

S3-1_01;
S3-1_03

S3-1_04

S3-1_05

S3-2_01;

S3-2_07

## Page 273

By constantly exchanging information, community and stakeholder views are incorporated into the various initiatives. We
therefore work with numerous specialist organisations and institutions, like the UNHCR, Red Cross, Médicins Sans
Frontières, Water.org or WWF, to carry out these initiatives, from their design to their implementation and subsequent
assessment.
In addition, the beneficiaries of the various community investment projects are involved in the assessment phase. Their
assessment helps us to gauge the effectiveness of the actions and improve their implementation and scope. We also hold
direct contacts with them as part of the specific implementation of projects in communities linked to cotton production.
i More information on our partnerships with NGOs in section S3-4. Actions related to communities of this Report.
/ Social Advisory Board
Our external advisory body on community investment is composed of external and independent persons and institutions,
and its main mission is to institutionalise dialogue with key stakeholders from civil society.
/ Sponsorships and Patronage Committee
As the body responsible for the governance in our Community Investment Policy, it is empowered with approving all
contributions made under the umbrella of the community investment strategy. Thus, it coordinates with the Community
Investment department to assess and approve the necessary initiatives.
/ Projects linked to our Workers at the Centre and raw materials strategies
As part of our Workers at the Centre strategy, we also forge specific partnerships and engagements with organisations that
focus on communities linked to our supply chain, and in particular with those communities related to the production of raw
materials. One such example is our Public-Private Partnership with the  International Labour Organization, in place since
2017, to improve respect for Fundamental Principles and Rights at Work in cotton communities, and another is our work
with the Organic Cotton Accelerator.
We also harness our sustainability teams to carry out our community engagement actions.
i More information on stakeholder engagement in section SBM-2. Interests and views of stakeholders of this Report.
We gauge the effectiveness of our relationship with and commitment to the community by the detailed monitoring of the
projects we develop.
When it comes to initiatives carried out within the framework of our community investment strategy, we rigorously measure
the outputs and impacts achieved, using, among others, the Business for Societal Impact (B4SI) measurement
methodology. In terms of the groups we target, we pay special attention to the protection of the most vulnerable groups,
such as women, young people at risk of exclusion, LGBTQI+ people, migrants and refugees, among others. We also take
into account those communities linked to the production of the raw materials needed for our products, such as cotton.
To learn these groups’ views, and to ensure that our strategy is aligned with their needs, we rely on our partnerships with
specialised community-based organizations.
Lastly, operating responsibility regarding stakeholder engagement, in particular with the community, rests with the Chief
Sustainability Officer.
S3-3. Processes to remediate negative impacts and channels for
affected communities to raise concerns
Processes to remediate negative impacts
By identifying our potential and/or actual negative impacts, we are able to prevent, mitigate and remedy the effects of their
materialisation.
6. Social Information / S3. Affected communities
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
273

S3-2_05;
S3-2_06

S3-2_04

S3-3_10

## Page 274

This approach extends to the community. We pay particular attention to developing our due diligence processes and make
grievance mechanisms available to all legitimate third parties, so as to act in the best possible way should any negative
impact affect them.
Main communication and grievance mechanisms
Members of the community have access to our Ethics Line, which is available to all our stakeholders. The procedure for
handling communications received from the community through this channel and ensuring its efficacy is the same as for
those received from other stakeholders, which is outlined in the relevant chapter of this Report.
i More information on how the Ethics Line works, including monitoring, control and assurance of its effectiveness, in
section G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct of this Report.
To ensure that members of the community know, trust and can access our Ethics Line, in addition to including information
and facilitating access to it via the corporate website, we have designated it as a preferred communication channel in all the
policies that apply to it.
Members of the community can also use the external grievance channels designated by the competent authorities in each
case, including the official EU institutions, bodies or agencies.
Moreover, we analyse any other communications we may receive, for example through the organisations we work with in
community matters, in order to monitor them and take any necessary action.
S3-4. Actions related to communities
At Inditex we see community investment as a chance to contribute to sustainable development and have a positive
impact on the community through voluntary activities that go beyond the boundaries of our value chain and of our industry.
Our approach to corporate community investment
We aim to bring about a lasting impact in the community, which is why we focus our efforts on supporting long-term
strategic projects, subject to an exhaustive monitoring and accountability process, instead of making sporadic contributions
to social or environmental causes.
To achieve this, we invest monetary or in-kind resources or hours —such as our people’s time spent volunteering— in an
array of non-profit initiatives.
In this regard, in 2024 we supported 485 organisations (476 in 2023) in carrying out 953 social and environmental projects
with an investment of over 134 million euros, a 20% increase on the investment made in 2023. These initiatives, focusing on
the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs),  that relate to our activity, have directly benefited more than 4.6 million
people in 2024, i.e. 6% more than in the previous year.
The different circumstances of those people are an aspect to which we pay particular attention when developing our
actions.
Distribution of the people benefited in 2024 on the basis of their situation
Living in developing countries
52%
Low income
36%
Refugees
5%
In situation of vulnerability
5%
Other profiles(1)
2%
(1) ‘Other profiles’ refers to people with a disability, people who are unemployed, victims of a natural disaster or catastrophe or who are
homeless, among other circumstances.
Furthermore, we see gender equality as a transversal priority in our strategy. Accordingly, the initiatives we support promote
respect for diversity and equality between men and women in all spheres (economic, social, cultural, labour, political, etc.).
This approach addresses the root causes of the problem and helps to solve it.
We also take into account the path outlined by the SDGs when it comes to designing our community partnership projects.
We identify the main SDGs to which our initiatives contribute. In 2024, 91% of our contributions were in support of projects
aimed mainly at SDGs 3, 4, 5, 8, 10, 12 and 15:

In line with our community investment strategy, our efforts in this regard focus mainly on three priority activity areas
(education, emergency relief and the environment), which account for 75% of our community investment:
/ Education action
Related IROs: [S3.I1; S3.I2; S3.R1]
We support projects focused on safeguarding the community's right to quality education throughout life and generating
opportunities for decent work grounded on inclusion and equity in the community.
Key education initiatives in 2024:
/ Inditex Chair of Refugees and Forced Migrants in the Comillas Pontifical University.
/ TEMPE-APSA Chair of Disability and Employability at Miguel Hernández University in Elche.
/ Inditex Chair of Spanish Language and Culture at the University of Dhaka.
/ Inditex Chair of Artificial Intelligence in Green Algorithm at the University of A Coruña (UDC).
/ Inditex Chair of Sustainability at the University of A Coruña (UDC).
/ Inditex Chair of Family-Owned business at the Universities of A Coruña (UDC), Santiago de Compostela (USC) and Vigo (UV).
/ Inditex Chair of Inclusion of Persons with Intellectual Disabilities at the University of Barcelona (UB).
/ Programa CEMS: The Global Alliance in Management Education.
/ for&from: social/workplace integration programme for people with disabilities.
/ GO (Generating Opportunities: education and inclusion for a sustainable world) with Entreculturas.
/ Collaboration programme with Tsinghua University.
/ Collaboration programme with the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT).
/ Collaboration programme with the University of A Coruña (UDC): InTalent, InMotion, Master's Degree in Digital Law and Artificial
Intelligence (MUDDIA), Master's Degree in Financial Innovation and Digitisation.
/ Employment and Training Programme in Spain with Caritas.
/ FAD Juventud programme: programme to provide quality employment for women in technology professions in Spain.
/ SALTA: employment integration programme for people at risk or in a situation of social exclusion.
To implement this action, in 2024 we allocated 23 million euros —excluding management costs— which implies 17% of our
contribution to community investment actions (17 million euros in 2023).
/ Emergency relief action
Related IROs: [S3.I1; S3.I2; S3.R1]
We contribute with initiatives to safeguard the lives, health and well-being of forced migrants and refugees, as well as other
vulnerable groups facing humanitarian emergencies such as natural disasters, armed conflicts, etc.
