## Page 276

Key emergency relief initiatives in 2024:
/ Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) programme: medical-humanitarian response programme in various parts of the world.
/ Relief programme for those affected by the floods in Brazil.
/ Relief programme for those affected by the DANA in Spain.
/ UNHCR (United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees) Programme.
/ Assistance to displaced people in South America with Entreculturas.
/ A Flote with the Emalcsa Foundation and the A Coruña City Council: social integration and social benefits programme in Spain.
/ Every Mother Counts Programme: programme for the supply of prenatal and maternity care to pregnant women in Bangladesh and the
United States.
/ Medicus Mundi Programme: programme for the improvement in the well-being of garment workers in Morocco.
To implement this action, in 2024 we allocated 41 million euros —excluding management costs— which implies 31% of our
contribution to community investment actions (46 million euros in 2023).
/ Environmental action
Related IROs: [S3.I1; S3.I2; S3.R1]
We contribute to activities aimed at conserving and restoring ecosystems, fostering regenerative practices, supporting the
circular economy and using resources efficiently.
Key environmental initiatives in 2024:
/ Moda Re- with Caritas: programme to generate employment for vulnerable people through the collection, recycling, and reuse of used
garments in Spain.
/ Water.org programme: programme  to improve access to drinking water and sanitation for vulnerable families through microloans in
countries such as Bangladesh, Cambodia or India.
/ #BRINGYOUROWNBAG (#TRAETUBOLSA) initiative:
• WWF Programme: restoration and protection projects in forest and freshwater ecosystems.
• Water & Climate Fund by Water.org: projects to improve water and sanitation infrastructure.
• Agreement on fire prevention with Galicia Regional Government.
• Sustainable Forestry and Demonstration Forests with FORESTIS - Associação Florestal de Portugal- and the Galician Forestry
Association.
• Conservation International programme: Regenerative Fund for Nature and Mountains to Mangroves .
• Regenerative agriculture programme in India in collaboration with ASA, Laudes Foundation, IDH The Sustainable Trade Initiative and
WWF India.
• Royal Botanic Gardens Kew programme.
• SOS Atlantic Forest Programme.
To implement this action, in 2024 we allocated 35 million euros —excluding management costs— which implies 26% of our
contribution to community investment actions (25 million euros in 2023).
i More information on some of these initiatives in section E4-3. Actions related to biodiversity and ecosystems of this
Report.
/ Other issues addressed
Related IROs: [S3.I1; S3.I2; S3.R1]
In addition to our initiatives linked to education, emergency relief and environment, we also earmark part of our investment
to initiatives linked to social welfare, social and economic development, healthcare, art and culture.
Key social welfare, social and economic development, healthcare, art and culture initiatives in 2024:
/ Fundación ProCNIC: collaboration focused on cardiovascular health research.
/ Real Instituto Elcano: collaboration focused on research.
/ Fundación Carolina: collaboration focused on research.
/ Royal Spanish Academy: collaboration focused on art and culture.
/ Reina Sofía National Museum of Art: collaboration focused on art and culture.
/ Royal Theatre opera house: collaboration focused on art and culture.
To implement this action, in 2024 we allocated 34 million euros —excluding management costs— which implies 25% of the
contribution to community investment actions (23 million euros in 2023).

In this regard, in 2024 we increased the value of our in-kind contributions by 58% compared to 2023, through the donation
of 7.25 million articles from our collections. Furthermore, our employees have dedicated 353,618 hours of their working

It is worth noting that 98% of the total investment in 2024 was channelled to support initiatives that we consider strategic:
community investment and commercial initiatives in the community. This is due to our commitment to ongoing initiatives
that enable us to maximise the effectiveness of our inputs and broaden the impact of key projects.
With regard to the geographic scope, we prioritise regular investment in the areas in which we conduct our activity, in
particular, in our clusters. Accordingly, our brands and subsidiaries operate locally and/or nationally when it comes to
supporting community and environmental projects. This allows us to maximise the positive impact within our communities.

Monitoring the positive outcomes of our actions
We carry out an exhaustive monitoring and accountability process in connection with our community investment projects,
using B4SI methodology, highlighting the achievements and impacts:
/ IMPACTS
To gauge the effectiveness of the projects we support, we measure the impact of our investments on both the
community (direct beneficiaries and community organisations) and the Company (our people and the business).

/ LEVERAGE
Furthermore, it is important to consider the amount of additional investment leveraged: the additional resources that
our investment programmes have leveraged as a result of contributions by third parties—employees, customers, etc—.
In 2024, this leveraged investment amounted to 210.2 million euros, which represents a 95% increase compared to 2023.
This amount was raised mainly through the collaboration with Water.org —through the additional capital released by
microfinance institutions—, the Moda Re- project —through the collection of garments— and donations from our
employees from their pay checks.
i More information in the Methodological Annex of this Report.
Other community actions
Our community engagement strategy prioritises having a positive impact, as previously outlined, but we also take into
account any potential negative impacts on the community from our activity. We do this using a dual approach of
prevention and remediation, first minimising the possibility of the impact occurring, but also having the necessary
mechanisms in place to remedy it if it does occur.
In this regard, we respect the rights of communities when implementing our actions and we extend the scope of
programmes like Workers at the Centre with the aim of transferring their effect not only to supply chain workers but to their
broader communities. This is especially significant in the communities linked to the production of our raw materials.
6. Social Information / S3. Affected communities
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
279
38 In 2024, the assessment was carried out through surveys of 352 employees in 19 countries following their involvement in corporate
volunteering initiatives (225 employees in 18 countries in 2023).

S3-4_01;
S3-4_06

## Page 280

i More information in section S2. Workers in the value chain of this Report.
We take into account impacts indirectly related to the community as well, such as environmental impacts. In this
connection, we carry out a number of programmes and initiatives that contribute to respecting communities' right to a
healthy environment.

i Details of the environmental programmes are provided in chapters E1. Climate change , E2. Pollution, E3. Water
resources, E4. Biodiversity and ecosystems and E5. Resource use and circular economy of this Report.
Further considerations
At Inditex, we are mindful that the challenges arising from our global presence are shared, so we collaborate with all major
stakeholders to leverage our effect and achieve a real and lasting impact. That is why we join forces with NGOs, local and
international organisations, companies and brands, and other stakeholders such as academic institutions, media, and civil
society itself. And we also work with global industry bodies and initiatives with the aim transforming the sector and
addressing shared challenges.
Managing impacts, as well as identifying the measures needed to address them, are tasked centralised in our sustainability
teams, who are responsible for tackling the challenge of accompaniment and collaboration on the ground. They hold direct
contacts with NGOs, local and national authorities, international organisations and academic institutions, among others, to
seek common solutions that prevent and mitigate negative impacts and promote positive ones.
Furthermore, the Ethics Line allows us to track possible infringements of irregularities, including those linked to human
rights, so that we can process the pertinent cases and put into practice the action required.
i More information in section G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct of this Report.
The Ethics Committee is tasked with identifying which action is needed to respond to each impact. In this regard, when a
communication is received, the Ethics Committee acknowledges receipt and assesses whether it falls within the scope of
the Ethics Line. If so, the Ethics Committee conducts an investigation, in collaboration with the relevant areas, and decides
whether to close the case (if there is no breach), or to adopt disciplinary and/or additional action. These measures include
mitigation measures, such as immediate correction of a breach, and measures to remedy and prevent future breaches.
The Ethics Committee is also tasked with analysing and identifying who best should carry out the relevant actions.
In 2024, no negative impacts linked to communities were brought to our attention through the Ethics Line and therefore no
remediation measures were carried out. Likewise, no inherently severe incidences of community-related human rights
violations have been detected through this channel.
As for community-related cases received through the Ethics Line, the procedure for managing them and ensuring the
effectiveness of the Line is common to that of cases received from other stakeholders, and is detailed in the corresponding
chapter of this Report.

i More information in section G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct of this Report.
Furthermore, at Inditex we include communities in our human rights due diligence processes. In addition, we carry out an
annual materiality assessment that allows us to identify our points of impact, both positive and negative, and inform our
strategy. Thus, we seek to prevent our activity from generating any associated negative effects, especially with regard to the
community.

i More information on the materiality assessment in sections SBM-3. Material impacts, risks and opportunities and
IROs. Disclosures on the materiality assessment process of this Report.
6. Social Information / S3. Affected communities

The prevention and mitigation actions detailed above help us to prevent fines or penalties being exacted for human rights
breaches against communities.
In turn, no material opportunities have been identified in relation to communities.

Agreements and collaborations
Our agreements and collaborations play a fundamental role in the relationships with the communities around our activities.
That is why at Inditex we work closely with the various organisations to mitigate potential negative impacts and have a
positive impact on our surroundings. To achieve this we have estable collaboration agreements with a number of
organisations, such as Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF), Entreculturas and UNHCR, as well as academic institutions like
Tsinghua University and the University of Santiago de Compostela.
i More information in the report Partnerships, on Inditex’s corporate website.
C. Metrics and targets
S3-5. Targets related to communities
• To help ten million people through our corporate community investment programme between
2022 and 2025
In 2022 we committed to help ten million people through our corporate community investment programme  between 2022
and 2025. En 2024, we have achieved this target, helping more than 12.3 million people over the last three years.
This target is linked to our Sustainability Policy, and more specifically to our Community Investment Policy, which is
committed to improving community well-being, maximising the value we generate in the community, and building strategic
partnerships to promote sustainable development together.
Target: to help ten million people through our Corporate Community Investment
programme between 2022 and 2025
Current year
Target year
2024
2025
People
12.3 million
10 million
Through this target, we have worked to have a positive and lasting impact on the community by means of our community
investment projects, aligned with the Paris Agreement and the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) established in the
United Nations 2030 Agenda.
In establishing them, we involve the Social Advisory Board, an external advisory body institutionalising dialogue with key
partners from civil society, and our strategic partners in our community investment initiatives, with whom we work to
implement our actions.
We have also relied on the continuous dialogue we maintain with all the organisations we work with that routinely inform our
decisions and roadmaps.
To gauge the achievement of this target and identify possible improvements, we have involved the various actors in our
projects, especially the non-profit organisations with whom we collaborate and the beneficiaries of our actions themselves.
i More information on the scope, methodology and calculation changes in the year in the Methodological Annex of this
Report.

S4. Consumers and end-users
A. Strategy
SBM-3. Material impacts, risks and opportunities
To ensure customer protection and constant improvement in customer satisfaction, it is crucial to understand their needs
and concerns. We have therefore identified the material impacts, risks and opportunities linked to our consumers and end-
users (hereinafter, consumers or customers).

i More information in sections SBM-3. Material impacts, risks and opportunities and IROs. Disclosures on the materiality
assessment process of this Report.
Scope
Our materiality assessment takes into account any person acquiring any product sold by the Inditex Group brands, either in
physical stores or online, as well as potential customers. We pay special attention to those consumers who require accurate
and accessible information on our products and their components, as well as more vulnerable users or those who may be
at a greater risk of being hurt, such as children.
We therefore pay special attention to the design of all the products aimed at those users, guaranteeing that they are
designed in keeping with their age and needs, so as to protect their well-being.
Occurrence and types of impacts
In line with this commitment to our customers, we have mechanisms in place to prevent specific negative impacts, such as
the loss of personal data, damage related to product health and safety, cases of discrimination or effects on decision
making due to a lack of transparency regarding the product. We also have measures to control the potential emergence of
systemic impacts that might arise due to the various circumstances in the geographies where we operate. These
mechanisms also incorporate mitigation and remediation strategies for negative effects once they have occurred, in order
to minimise their impact on customers.
6. Social Information / S4. Consumers and end-users
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
282

S4.SBM-3_01;

S4.SBM-3_04

## Page 283

Additionally, we are fully committed to the respect and promotion of human rights, rejecting any kind of discrimination. Our
Code of Conduct lays down our commitment to responsible marketing and communication, including the obligation to
provide clear, truthful and comprehensive information about our products, as well as to ensuring that our items for sale and
published content are neither offensive, discriminatory or promote an unhealthy image.
Furthermore, we seek to have a positive impact on our customers through an accessible and inclusive range of products.
For example, we apply universal accessibility principles for our customers with disabilities, in both physical and online
stores, with the aim of offering inclusive shopping experiences. In the same way, thanks to our project in conjunction with
EqualWeb, our home pages feature digital accessibility menus with voice, browsing, colour and content settings to ensure
that these users can properly access all the information. In addition, our customer services are available to all our
consumers to resolve any issues that may arise.
i More information on actions related to consumers in section S4-4. Actions related to consumers of this Report.
Risks and opportunities
The impacts and dependencies analysis allows us to identify the main risks and opportunities linked to our consumers,
verifying that our strategy includes prevention and mitigation actions. For this purpose, we have specialised Customer
Services teams who provide customer support, helping to avoid and act on any quality or health and safety issues (among
others). In turn, our health and safety efforts, through our teams and the development of associated standards, contributes
to ensure that the products we offer to consumers are safe, especially those products aimed at particularly vulnerable
consumers, such as children. As a result, not only do we reduce the possible negative impacts on our customers, but we
also minimise the risks related to consumer rights.
We have also identified a key opportunity to attract new customers through the implementation of responsible practices. In
this connection, we have initiatives such as Zara Pre-Owned through which we foster circularity and attract customers who
share these values.
B. Impact, risk and opportunity management
S4-1. Policies related to consumers
Our customers are the cornerstone of our business. That is why we establish firm principles through our policies, forming a
framework to govern our relationship with people, in a bid to have a positive effect on our surroundings, while mitigating any
potential negative impacts.
Policies related to consumers
/ Human Rights Policy i
/ Sustainability Policy i
/ Code of Conduct i
/ Information Security Policy i
/ Compliance Policy regarding Personal Data Protection and Privacy i
/ Ethics Line Procedure i
i More information on our policies and their updates in section MDR-P. Policies of this Report.
Our commitment to our customers is comprehensive and our aim is to offer them products that meet their needs and
expectations, guaranteeing high standards of quality, health and safety.
The Sustainability Policy covers customers’ concerns and expectations in relation to sustainability, applied to all the
products we sell. In this way, we commit ourselves to:

/ Responsible design of products, promoting the use of lower-impact raw materials and textile fibres39.
/ Ethical and responsible manufacture of our products, especially regarding the relationship with our manufacturers and
suppliers.
/ Complying with the most stringent health and safety standards.
Furthermore, our Code of Conduct lays down our commitment to responsible marketing and communication, including the
obligation to provide accurate, clear and comprehensive information about our products and their characteristics, and to
ensure that the products we sell and our published content are not offensive, discriminatory or promote an unhealthy
image.
We also consider it paramount to guarantee information security, particularly in connection with our customers’ data and
privacy. We are, therefore, aware of the potential cybersecurity risks that may emerge as we conduct our operations, and
we work accordingly to involve our entire workforce in preventing and mitigating them, thereby protecting all our
stakeholders.
Our Information Security Policy and Compliance Policy regarding Personal Data Protection and Privacy establish our action
principles in this sphere.

Commitment to respect our customers’ human rights
At Inditex we endeavour to guarantee that the human rights of everyone around us, including our customers, are upheld.
We therefore reject any form of discrimination, always respecting our customers’ right to privacy, which we duly protect, and
making a properly use of their personal data.

## Page 285

The health and safety of our customers are also paramount for the Group, which is why we undertake to make products in
an ethical and responsible way, in keeping with our health and safety standards.
In this regard, our Sustainability Policy, and in more detail in our Human Rights Policy, inspired by the leading international
declarations on human rights, enshrine our commitment to our consumers’ rights.
i More information in section MDR-P. Policies in this Report.
One of our foremost tools for customer engagement and dialogue are our customer service mechanisms, which establish
a direct avenue of communication for any issue or query that may arise, allowing us to detect and act to address potential
breaches. Our customers can also access the official grievance mechanisms in place in each market.
This set of mechanisms, described in more detail in the following sections of this Report, offers a range of methods to
address any query or complaint in connection with their rights or other matters of interest to them.
Accordingly, in 2024 no breaches of the United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, the ILO
Declaration on Fundamental Principles and Rights at Work, or the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises have been
identified through the Ethics Line, our main complaint mechanism.
i More information on the Ethics Line in section G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct of this Report.
S4-2. Processes for engaging with consumers about impacts
We have various mechanisms of relationship and dialogue with our customers, allowing us to listen to their perspectives
and take them into account in making decisions to properly manage the actual and potential impacts on them. In this
connection, we highlight:
/ Specialised customer service teams
Our brands’ specialised customer service teams are among the main channels for engaging with consumers, primarily at
the operating level. These areas handle customer contacts —calls, e-mails, WhatsApp messages or messages on social
media profiles— concerning questions on products, the purchasing process, shipments, incidents or any current issues
affecting the brands, among others.
/ Store teams
Inditex implements a flexible business model based on multi-concept omni-channelling and the integration of retail space:
stores and online. This allows us to generate a differentiated shopping experience, tailored to our customers’ needs at the
right time and place.
To fulfil this commitment to our customers' experience, the work of our physical and online store teams is crucial, as they
form the first line of direct contact with consumers. In particular, our physical store teams communicate daily with the
people who visit our establishments, directly handling their queries or informing them about the other available customer
care and complaints mechanisms as and when they require them.
In this regard, we promote initiatives such as Customer Experience (CX), in which store managers lead the change,
analyse the feedback they receive from customers and develop their teams’ skills to offer the best possible customer care.
At the same time, the continuous improvement of our customer relations from an innovation standpoint led us to launch Big
Store, a project in which our store teams carry out digital tasks relating to the business.
/ Communication channels: websites, app and social media
Our digital channels enable us to interact with our customers in more than 200 markets. Not only do these channels
showcase our collections, but they allow us to convey our principles in the content we generate on our platforms, fulfilling
our commitment to responsible marketing practices.
6. Social Information / S4. Consumers and end-users

## Page 286

We are mindful of the impact of our products and the image they transmit. That is why we want our campaigns, and the
image of the models who appear on them to convey a diverse, multicultural and positive reality that celebrates the product
by presenting it in a detailed way that is faithful to its properties and qualities.
/ Ethics Line
The Ethics Line, as the main grievance mechanism available to our stakeholders, is one of the communication channels for
our customers to convey their concerns and raise questions about the Code of Conduct and other Group internal
regulations, as well as to report to us, including anonymously, any breaches or actions contrary to the regulations or to any
applicable law.
i More information on the Ethics Line in section G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct of this Report.
Our ongoing relationship with our customers takes place before, during and after the purchase process, through the
various channels mentioned above. This interaction is initiated both at the customer's behest, when they need to raise a
query or file a complaint, and by the Company, as it provides regular information on its product range and services.
Through these actions, we seek to understand their perspectives and concerns, which we take into account as we design
our strategy. This applies not only in terms of our products, but also when it comes to preventing and mitigating impacts,
and evaluating the effectiveness of the measures and their subsequent renewal or updating.
Throughout the entire process, it should be noted that special consideration is given to the views and impacts of
consumers who may be particularly vulnerable. For instance, continuous dialogue with different partner organisations
allows us to exchange views and integrate their perspective into the Company’s day-to-day operations.
i More information on some of the organisations we work with in the Diversity and Inclusion report, published on  Inditex corporate
website.
Moreover, at Inditex we measure the effectiveness of our customer services by following up on our interactions in detail.
This allows us to gauge whether the communication is efficient, and determine whether our customer services are aligned
with their needs.
In this effectiveness analysis, we highlight the work of our teams, who implement their own contact monitoring plans for
each of the brands. These plans allow us to examine a range of valuable metrics to assess the efficacy of the service, such
as first call resolution or the number of repeat contacts.
The Customer Experience manager of each of our retail concepts is ultimately responsible for overseeing these services.
i More information on how our customer service resources work in section S4-3. Processes to remediate negative
impacts and channels for consumers to raise concerns of this Report.
S4-3. Processes to remediate negative impacts and channels for
consumers to raise concerns
We identify our potential and actual impacts in order to prevent, mitigate and remediate their effects. This understanding
applies to all our stakeholders, including our customers.
Depending on the nature of the impact, we have three preferred channels to manage issues or concerns raised by our
customers: customer service teams and channels, official grievance mechanisms and the Ethics Line.
Through these three preferred contact avenues, detailed in section S4-2 of this Report, we receive communications from
our customers and identify and implement remediation and mitigation plans that minimise negative effects arising from
impacts. At the same time, we work to prevent them and to avoid their recurrence in the future. Thus, we guarantee the
protection of our customers' rights.
6. Social Information / S4. Consumers and end-users

## Page 287

Response to our customers
With regard to the communications received through the customer service channels, at the operating level, our aim is to
ensure the quality and efficacy of the service offered. Consequently, our customer service agents have access to a specific
training platform that contains all the procedures and manuals. To guarantee the effectiveness of the channel and with the
aim of effecting continuous improvement of the service, we conduct monthly controls of customer interactions with our
brands, to monitor the quality of the customer care provided.
In 2024 the customer service areas of the Group's brands fielded a total of 45,913,444 customer contacts (calls, e-mails,
WhatsApp conversations and messages via social media profiles), concerning questions on products, the purchasing
process, shipments, incidents or current issues affecting the brands, among other matters (46,446,384 contacts in 2023).
In this regard, the progression of online sales has shifted the weight of customer contacts according to the purchase
channel, so that enquiries about order status, delivery times or the online operation itself make up a very significant part of
the total.
Regarding the service level, understood as the percentage of contacts resolved over the total, in 2024 it was 97%  (98% in
2023).
In 2024, the quality of customer services at five Inditex brands —Pull&Bear, Massimo Dutti, Bershka, Oysho and Zara Home
— renewed its certification under the ISO 18295:2 international standard. This certification underpins the customer service
strategy throughout their shopping experience and interaction with each brand, the operations and protocols for looking
after customers through the various contact channels, as well as the standards of service quality and satisfaction of our
customers.
Types of cases handled
In the relationship with the customer, our teams field a range of queries related to our products and the purchase process.
Each brand independently sorts the reasons for customer contacts, according to their specific needs, although these
reasons can be grouped into four broad areas:
/ Pre-purchase: issues prior to the purchase.
/ Post-purchase: related to an order or purchase at a store.
/ Customer service: relating to the contact channels, website and app.
/ Complaints and claims.
In 2024, our brands handled 44.5 million cases through Customer Services in all the markets where we have a commercial
presence (45.4 million in 2023). Of these, the categorised cases are classified as follows:
Type of cases handled
2024
2023
Pre-purchase (product availability, special collections, customisation, purchasing process,
checkout and sustainability, among others)
26%
20%
Post-purchase (order status, shipments, delivery times, changes and returns, gift cards,
among others)
70%
76%
Customer Service (customer account, web/app, channels, social media, among others)
5%
4%
Complaints (includes any customer feedback, as well as formal complaints)
0.02%
0.02%
The complaints figure includes complaint forms and claim procedures in Spain processed through official consumer
complaint and response mechanisms, which in 2024 totalled 6,924 (5,840 cases processed in Spain in 2023). The main
reason for these complaint forms is related to product returns and exchanges, and store and online sales.
With regard to cases received from customers through the Ethics Line, the procedure for handling them and ensuring the
channel's efficacy is the same as for those received from other stakeholders, and is outlined in the relevant chapter of this
Report.
Our customer service channels can be accessed via all our online store websites and apps. Likewise, the physical store
teams pass on information about customer service and other formal complaints mechanisms as and when necessary.
6. Social Information / S4. Consumers and end-users

## Page 288

Likewise, everyone, including consumers, can access the Ethics Line continuously via our corporate website. In addition,
external reporting channels designated by the competent authorities may also be used, including the institutions, bodies or
agencies of the European Union.
i More information in section S4-2. Processes for engaging with consumers about impacts of this Report.
In handling customer complaints and concerns, we maintain the utmost respect for confidentiality, privacy and data
protection. In the specific case of our customer service channels, customers only need to provide contact information
when their query is related to an order or when it requires subsequent follow-up or action by our customer service team.
Other communications may be made anonymously.
To facilitate the implementation of our privacy programme, we carry out various transversal projects, such as removing or
blocking any sensitive information provided by customers as a security measure, and other measures aimed at improving
their shopping experience.
Furthermore, concerns about compliance received via the Ethics Line may be made anonymously.

i More information on the workings of the Ethics Line, including follow-up, control and ensuring its efficacy in section
G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct of this Report.
S4-4. Actions related to consumers
The actions we carry out were designed to manage our impacts, risks and opportunities, whether actual or potential. For
this purpose we continuously carry out initiatives to ensure the protection and care of our consumers and aim to prevent
the occurrence of negative impacts, and, should these arise, remediate their effects. We therefore do everything we can,
and allocate the necessary resources, to properly manage these impacts. We also carry out the necessary follow up
through related metrics.
Against this backdrop, we take a dual approach of prevention and mitigation, responding to the negative impact and at the
same time minimising the chances of its occurring in the future. We underscore our efforts to ensure the protection of our
consumers' data, the accessibility and inclusion of all our customers, regardless of their specific characteristics, and our
special health and safety protection measures, both in our stores and relating to our products.
Accordingly, we have standards, training plans and prevention and control programmes focused on preventing, mitigating
and remedying any impact on our customers’ health and safety. Likewise, our product health and safety teams are
coordinated with all areas of the Company to ensure an adapted response to any notification of an incident from
customers, community organisations or supervisory bodies, carrying out technical assessments and following up. If we find
indications that a product might not be safe, it is withdrawn and our internal procedure is activated to communicate the
situation and recall any units sold.
Our communication channels are the main source of key information to guide our strategies and actions relating to
customers. In particular, we highlight the work of our store and customer service teams who, through their direct and
constant relationship with consumers, pinpoint their priority needs. This ensures that our strategy is adapted to our broader
reality.
Furthermore, the Ethics Line allows us to track possible human rights infringements or irregularities, so that we can process
the pertinent cases and promote the action required. Among the cases reported through the Ethics Line in 2024,  no
inherently severe incidents linked to customers’ human rights have been revealed.
i More information in section G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct of this Report.
6. Social Information / S4. Consumers and end-users
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
288

## Page 289

In addition to responding to negative impacts, at Inditex we endeavour to have a positive impact on our customers. Our
Customer Experience initiative takes a customer-centric approach, aiming for a constant improvement of their experience
with Inditex.
In this vein, we offer our consumers quality and safe products, which they can access in an inclusive way, with a personal
and careful service experience, and having privacy guaranteed.
Hence, when designing our customer actions, we consider the path outlined by the UN Sustainable Development Goals
(SDGs), with a focus on contributing to achieving the goals defined. In 2024 our initiatives have contributed mainly to SDG
12, focusing on sustainable consumption and production patterns.
At Inditex, we are mindful that the challenges posed by having global operations are shared, so we collaborate to leverage
our effect and achieve a real and lasting impact. That is why we share our goals with suppliers, manufacturers, companies
and brands, and other stakeholders such as NGOs, trade unions and local and international organisations. And we also
work with global industry bodies and initiatives with the aim of achieving widespread adoption of sustainable practices
across the sector.
The possibility of attracting new customers by implementing responsible practices that are aligned with their interests and
concerns is one of the opportunities we have identified. However, our actions that contribute to this opportunity are not
designed to attract new customers, but are aligned with our understanding of sustainability as a responsibility and driver of
our Company’s strategy. These actions are detailed throughout the other chapters of this Report, mainly in standards E1, E2,
E3, E4, E5, S2 and G1.
Health and safety actions
Related IROs: [S4.I2; S4.I4; S4.R1; S4.O1]
/ Health and safety initiative
The health and safety of our products is paramount to Inditex. Accordingly, we have standards, training plans and
prevention and control programmes devised to ensure that our products comply with the strictest requirements and
guidelines in all the markets where we operate.
We have product-specific generally applicable and mandatory standards for all the articles we sell40, covering processes
from design to manufacturing. We currently have eight product standards, each with a different scope:
Product standards

Safe to Wear
Garments, children's footwear, accessories, including
trimmings and fabrics used in their manufacture

Garments, fabrics, footwear, accessories and home textiles
Physical Testing Requirements
Garments, footwear, accessories and home textiles
Active to Wear
Garments and fabrics
i+Cosmetics
Cosmetics

Products in contact with food
i+Home Fragrance and Candles
Home fragrances, candles and their accessories.
i+Child Care Furniture
Childcare furniture

## Page 290

As indicated in the chapter on pollution, we work with technology companies, research centres and laboratories of
international reference, as well as implementing our own programmes, to ensure that our products meet the Company’s
own standards.
i More information in sections E2-1. Policies related to pollution and E2-2. Actions related to pollution of this Report.
In particular, to strengthen compliance with our Safe to Wear safety standard, we provide our suppliers with manufacturing
guidelines that include measurement tables with specific safety requirements, such as the position of appliqués and cords
or maximum lengths of free ends.
Additionally, in line with our commitment to offer safe products, our product health and safety teams are fully coordinated
with customer service teams on stores and any other area of the Company where information about incidents and/or
complaints may be received.
At the same time, any notification from our customers, from community organisations or supervisory bodies is forwarded to
our technical experts for evaluation and follow-up. If there are signs that a product may be unsafe for consumers, it is
withdrawn from the market, customers are notified through the relevant channels and all units sold are recalled, according
to our internal procedure.
To verify compliance with our health and safety standards, we use our Picking inspections programme. This programme
allows us to verify that our products comply, among others, with our Safe to Wear (StW) and Physical Testing Requirements
(PTR). Specifically, the programme’s results in 2024 show an initial degree of compliance by our textile articles of 99.7% for
StW and 99.3% for PTR (99.6% and 99.3%, respectively, in 2023).
i More information on the Picking programme can be found in section E2-2. Actions related to pollution of this Report.
Data protection and privacy actions
Related IROs: [S4.I1; S4.R1]
In 2024, we have worked in advocating the value of respect for privacy and ensuring an appropriate level of compliance
with data protection and privacy regulations. Our compliance model in connection with data protection and privacy is
based on:
/ Accompanying the business areas and providing risk-based advice.
/ Continuous improvement of the privacy programme and implementation of tools that strengthen the accountability
system, accrediting compliance with the model.
/ Monitoring by the Group’s global Data Protection Officer (DPO) through our privacy control system.
/ Annual report to the Board of Directors through the Audit and Compliance Committee and to the Company's
Management through the participation in the Information Security Committee, when necessary.
Throughout this year, the Personal Data Protection and Privacy department has continued advising the business and
technology areas in the projects they launched over the course of the year to ensure they are compliant with privacy
requirements from the outset (privacy by design). Specifically, 65 projects involving the processing of customers’ data were
analysed.
In addition, in 2024 we carried out a work to monitor regulations and adaptation projects in subsidiaries, keeping track of
new data protection regulations and modifications thereto so as to ensure an adequate level of compliance. We conducted
an exercise to adapt to data protection and privacy regulations in markets such as Canada, Japan and Türkiye with the aim,
not only of guaranteeing compliance, but adapting our corporate compliance model to local specificities.
With regard to training in connection with data protection and privacy, we continue to work on the promotion of the privacy
culture at the Company through the ‘privacy’ course, which is mandatory for all office teams who process personal data of
different stakeholders. Since its launch in February 2024, 97% of the staff required to take this course have completed it.
6. Social Information / S4. Consumers and end-users

## Page 291

Likewise, based on their greater exposure to privacy risks, we have carried out the following actions focused on other
priority groups:
/ We have collaborated with the Compliance department in specific training for the People department on the privacy
aspects of the Code of Conduct.
/ We have continued to train our Privacy Delegates, our liaisons who help coordinate and manage data protection and
privacy issues in each of the subsidiaries. In this regard, in 2024 we trained Privacy Delegates for 18 markets.
i More information can be found in the section G1-3. Prevention and detection of corruption and bribery of this Report
In order to guarantee the data protection of the groups whose personal data we process (customers, employees,
candidates, etc.), we conduct an analysis of all the trends with an impact on privacy across our sector, as well as the
obligations entailed by the new regulations and the interpretations of the supervisory authorities, judges and courts. This
guarantees seamless compliance with the principles deriving from the data protection and privacy regulations.
In turn, we have worked on cases that could potentially affect customers' personal data in order to determine whether a
security breach had occurred and, if so, whether it was necessary to notify the data protection authorities and/or the
affected persons. Over the course of the year, four cases affecting personal data security occurred where the Company
deemed it necessary to notify the data protection authorities, and one case in which the affected persons were also
informed of their situation. All these were minor incidents and, to date, no additional information has been requested by the
authorities. In any event, the necessary action plans have been put in place internally to avoid a recurrence in the future.
The actions carried out in relation to our costumers are carried out primarily by our people. The financial resources
earmarked for these actions are associated with staff costs and other external operating expenses, which separately do not
represent a material amount. Both are included in Operating Expenses in the Consolidated Income Statement (see Note 6
of the Consolidated Annual Accounts).
Other initiatives
In addition to these actions, we carry out other initiatives linked to accessibility measures for customers with disabilities.
Hence, stores meet architectural accessibility standards to enable people with disabilities to access and move around the
stores and to ensure they have a satisfactory shopping experience.
With regard to digital accessibility, and thus our brands’ websites and apps, as well as our recruitment and professional
development platforms, Inditex Careers and InTalent, include specific functionalities to improve access for users with
disabilities. Our websites are compliant with the General Accessible Design Principles established by the Web Accessibility
Initiative (WAI), a part of the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C). In addition, thanks to the project in conjunction with
EqualWeb, their home pages feature digital accessibility menus with voice, browsing, colour and content settings to ensure
a more inclusive user experience.
Agreements and collaborations
Through collaborations and agreements with specialised organisations such as Open to All, ENAR and REDI, we work to
raise awareness concerning the needs of vulnerable groups and take the necessary measures to ensure that our stores are
spaces where everyone feels welcome. In the United States, in 2022 we also signed the Mitigate Racial Bias in Retail
Charter, which advocates for actions to eliminate racial bias in shopping experiences. Furthermore, in the European Union,
13 of our subsidiaries have signed the Diversity Charter, a public commitment to create inclusive spaces that are free of
discrimination, and to promote diversity and equal opportunities for their staff and customers.
This type of collaboration and partnerships are aimed at improving the customer experience by adopting inclusive policies
that generate more respectful and inclusive environments for everyone entering our stores, regardless of their personal
characteristics.

C. Metrics and targets
S4-5. Targets related to consumers
At this stage we do not have specific targets linked to our customers. However, we are working on the global ambition of
improving our consumers’ experience, minimising negative impacts and maximising the positive ones. We do this through
the commitments enshrined in our policies, as well as the initiatives we carry out during the year. An example of this is our
target of including new circularity services in our markets, in keeping with our Sustainability Policy highlighting the
development of our Zara Pre-Owned platform.

## Page 296

A. Corporate ethical culture and solid compliance architecture
GOV-1. The role of the governing bodies
Strategy and governance
The Board of Directors defines and approves the ethical commitments reflected in the Codes of Conduct and ensures
compliance with them.
The Audit and Compliance Committee is tasked with a series of competencies in connection with risk management and
business conduct, which includes the supervision of the Compliance function. On a quarterly basis throughout the year, and
whenever requested, the Compliance function provides the Audit and Compliance Committee with:
/ the annual and half-yearly reports by the Ethics Committee on its activity, the functioning of the Ethics Line and the
overseeing of the Model of Criminal Risk Prevention;
/ the periodic reports from the Chief Compliance Officer on the functioning of the Global Compliance Model, compliance
risk management, the status of the cases processed by the Ethics Committee and the main compliance projects and
initiatives (including training and awareness-raising), the Annual work plan, the annual budget and the means and
resources available to the Compliance function; and
/ the internal rules that are submitted to the Board of Directors for approval or amendment, subject to a report from the
Board Committee with competence in this area.
In view of these reports, the Audit and Compliance Committee may raise questions, draw conclusions and make proposals
for measures aimed at improving the functioning of the Global Compliance Model. The Committee in turn reports to the
Board of Directors on these matters.
The Compliance function is corporate and coordinates all areas with compliance responsibilities within the Inditex Group. It
is made up of:
/ The Ethics Committee is the internal body responsible for overseeing compliance with the Codes of Conduct and the
Model of Criminal Risk Prevention. It is responsible for the Internal Reporting System and for managing the Ethics Line. It
is made up of six members appointed by the Board of Directors on 15 March 2022 and 3 May 2023, following a report
from the Audit and Compliance Committee. Its Regulations, approved by the Board of Directors, regulate its functioning
and powers. This Committee submits regular reports to the Audit and Compliance Committee on matters within its
competence.
/ The General Counsel's Office - Compliance (hereinafter, the Compliance department): it is tasked with the operating
management of the Global Compliance Model and, in particular, the Model of Criminal Risk Prevention and other models
for the prevention of corruption and other offences. The Chief Compliance Officer, who is also a member of the
aforementioned Ethics Committee, reports periodically to the Audit and Compliance Committee on the activity of the
Compliance function, the functioning of the Global Compliance Model, compliance risk management, annual work plans
and the means necessary to carry them out. The Compliance department is made up of 12 professionals, has the support
of more than 70 Compliance Delegates and an annual budget, at headquarters and subsidiaries.
The independence of the Compliance function is guaranteed by the appointment of the members of the Ethics Committee
and the approval of its Regulations by the Board of Directors and by the direct liaising of the Chief Compliance Officer with
the Audit and Compliance Committee, ensuring that the ultimate control of the Model’s efficiency depends exclusively on
the Board of Directors.
7. Governance information / G1. Business conduct

To ensure that the Board of Directors and its respective Committees, which have competencies relating to business
conduct, have a suitable composition that allows them to properly discharge their duties, the Company has a Diversity of
Board of Directors Membership and Director Selection Policy, approved by the Board of Directors.
Likewise, the Audit and Compliance Committee Regulations establish that the members of the Committee must be
appointed on the basis of their knowledge and experience in, among other areas, financial and non-financial risk
management, as well as their relevant technical expertise in relation to the Company’s sector of activity.
The Nomination Committee understands that, generally speaking, to properly carry out its oversight duties, members of the
Board of Directors must, as a whole, be of recognised solvency, competence, experience and merit: (i) in the Company,
Group and retail sector; (ii) in economics and finance, as well as in accounting, auditing and risk management; (iii) in
regulatory compliance and corporate governance; (iv) in the digital and new technologies sector; (v) in sustainability; (vi) in
different geographical markets; and (vii) in business management, leadership and strategy.

i More information on the Board Skills Matrix in the ‘Investors’ section of ‘Corporate Governance - Board of Directors’ of our corporate
website.
G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct
The ethical culture of the Inditex Group is based on the premise that ‘the how matters’. We believe that value comes from
how we do things and that the journey is more important than the destination. We do not understand success without the
ethical commitments and action principles that drive us.
Our ethical commitments and the action principles that should guide our day-to-day activities are set out in our Codes of
Conduct and developed upon further in other internal rules. To promote knowledge of and compliance with these rules,
they are published internally and/or externally and awareness-raising or training initiatives are carried out and adapted to
the profile of the people who should know them and apply them in their work.
Global Compliance Model
Our corporate ethical culture is integrated and implemented in all our operations through the Global Compliance Model. Its
purpose is to ensure compliance with the law and the ethical commitments undertaken by the Inditex Group, and to protect
the Company and the interests of our stakeholders, preventing or limiting any type of legal liability.
The Model is made up of fundamental corporate and high-level elements, that are developed upon in various internal rules,
among which our Codes of Conduct, described in the section MDR-P. Policies, stand out.
The key elements of the Global Compliance Model:
/ Governance, management and supervisory structure of the Global Compliance Model
/ Codes of Conduct
/ Ethics Line
/ Disciplinary system
/ Compliance training plan
/ Due diligence
/ Compliance Policy
/ Policy for Representatives and Attorneys of Companies within the Inditex Group
/ Internal Audit function
i More information in section MDR-P. Policies of this Report, and in the ‘Ethical Commitment’ section of our corporate
website.
In 2024, we also continued with the adaptation of the Ethics Line to the various transpositions of the Whistleblowing
Directive, drew up a corporate taxonomy of compliance risks, reviewed the methodology for assessing these risks and
continued with the integration of the Model of Criminal Risk Prevention and the existing local models into the Global
Compliance Model.
i More information on the policies and their update in section MDR-P. Policies of this Report.
7. Governance information / G1. Business conduct
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
298

G1-1_01

## Page 299

Model of Criminal Risk Prevention
As part of the Global Compliance Model, Inditex relies on a Model of Criminal Risk Prevention to prevent the commission of
offences under Spain’s Criminal Code, among them corruption, fraud and bribery. The Model was approved by the Board of
Directors in 2012 and revised in depth on 19 July 2016. It is continuously updated, evaluated and improved to ensure it is in
line with the Group’s operations, legal requirements and recommendations and best practices in this area, as well as to
ensure its implementation and effectiveness.

Composition of the Model of Criminal Risk Prevention
/ Policy: it describes the Model and details criminal risks to which the Group is potentially exposed due to its
operations, in accordance with Spanish law and the Internal Regulations containing guidelines for conduct
to prevent these risks.
/ Procedure: it establishes the organisational measures; the roles and responsibilities in the field of control,
verification and reporting of the Model, and the Ethics Committee’s functions in this field.
/ Risk and Control Matrix (hereinafter, the Matrix): this is a catalogue of criminal risks to which the Group is
inherently exposed, including corruption risks, and an inventory of controls defined for mitigating such risks,
allocating the frequency and the department responsible for their execution. It is continuously reviewed and
updated in accordance with applicable law and Group operations. The risks are assessed annually based on
their impact and likelihood of occurrence. Controls are monitored on a regular basis, with the support of
external auditors. With the information obtained in the process of assessing and of overseeing the controls,
priority criminal risks are identified.
The Criminal Risk Prevention Policy and the Matrix were reviewed in 2024. A risk assessment and monitoring of the controls
of the Matrix has been carried out, taking into account the prioritisation of the risks they mitigate, in accordance with the
previous year’s assessment.
Our overseeing of the Model of Criminal Risk Prevention carried out in 2024 had the support of external consultants in
reviewing the evidence of the execution of the Matrix controls, which was provided by the responsible departments during
the monitoring process. We also received external expert advice when reviewing the taxonomy of compliance risks and the
methodology for assessing criminal risks. In both cases, professional services firms independent of the Company’s auditors
were hired.
Since its review in 2016, this Model has been audited by an independent third party on several occasions, with a reasonable
assurance review scope, in order to confirm the effectiveness and proper functioning of the controls contained in the Risk
and Control Matrix, as well as the Model’s adequacy in terms of the requirements set out in sections 2 and 5 of article 31 bis
of the Criminal Code, the UNE-ISO 19600 Compliance Management Systems standard, and in keeping with the guidelines
of COSO in its report ‘Internal Control – Integrated Framework’ (2013). The audit report was unqualified in all cases. The last
of these audits was carried out in the financial year 2021, the report of which was presented to the Audit and Compliance
Committee at its meeting on 15 March 2022.
The main anti-corruption risks to which the Group is inherently exposed due to its activity, in accordance with the applicable
legislation, are identified in the Matrix, and are as follows: prevention of corruption in business, prevention of corruption in
international transactions, prevention of bribery and prevention of influence peddling.
In addition to the fundamental elements of the Global Compliance Model mentioned above, there are specific controls to
mitigate corruption, fraud, money laundering and terrorist financing. These include the Integrity Policies (Donations and
Sponsorships, Gifts & Invitations, and Dealings with Public Servants), the Conflicts of Interest Policy, the Policy on Public
Policy, and the Anti-Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing Policy.
Due diligence processes in connection with compliance
Inditex is committed to controlling and preventing compliance risks of third parties with which it has dealings. For this
purpose, there is a third-party control system, described in the Due Diligence Policy and the implementing internal
regulations thereof, which includes the principles and action criteria that aim to align Inditex’s relations with its business
partners, suppliers and large customers with international standards and best practices in the field of anti-corruption and
anti-bribery, the applicable anti-corruption regulations, and the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing. It
also aims to guarantee compliance with the sanctions and commercial restrictions approved by, at least, the European
Union, the United States, the United Kingdom and the United Nations, as well as compliance with the regulations on the
prevention of forced labour and on due diligence concerning the value and supply chains currently in place in the various
jurisdictions where the Group operates.
7. Governance information / G1. Business conduct
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
299

## Page 300

This process consists of the identification and analysis of all the suppliers, business partners and third parties with which
the Inditex Group does business, as well as, in certain cases, their main shareholders, directors and beneficial owners. This
allows us to identify potential risks related to corruption, fraud, tax evasion, money laundering, international sanctions,
forced labour, reputational risks, or similar risks that may be associated with these third parties. The process, for which the
Compliance department is responsible, is separate from but aligned with any other analysis of a social, environmental,
operational, financial, commercial or any other nature which the Inditex Group may be engaged in with suppliers or other
third parties. This due diligence system is consistent with the human rights system, supervised by the Sustainability
department.
i More information in sections GOV-4. Statement on due diligence and G1-2. Management of relations with suppliers of
this Report.
Principles of the due diligence process:
/ Mandatory for all business partners, large customers, suppliers and third parties with whom we engage in
business relations.
/ Necessary prerequisite in order to initiate business relations with third parties, which are also monitored.
/ Prohibition of any business dealings with third parties regarding which compliance risks have been
detected, when no action plan to mitigate or remedy such risks is under way.
/ Reasonableness and proportionality. Different levels of analysis are applied based on objective criteria
(business turnover, industry or market risk, etc.).
The process is carried out from the outset of the commercial relationship. All suppliers and other third parties that enter
into commercial and/or professional relations with the Group are subject to the scheduled due diligence process, which is
increasingly demanding in accordance with certain factors, including: (i) the third party’s total estimated business with
Inditex; (ii) the market in which the third party is based and carries out its main business; (iii) the sector to which it belongs;
and (iv) its degree of interrelation with the authorities and public servants. All existing suppliers are also periodically
assessed during the commercial relationship, submitting them to the flow that may be applicable to them, in accordance
with the Group’s internal regulations.
Risks identified as relevant will lead to the implementation of an action plan coordinated by the Compliance department,
which may range from a remedial measure to the termination of the business relationship with the third party. The internal
regulations describe the roles and responsibilities of the areas involved in the various procedural review flows, as well as in
the design, execution and monitoring, where appropriate, of any action plans established.
Additionally, Inditex’s internal regulations include restrictions on hiring third parties, only allowing those based in markets
authorised by the Group to be hired (i.e., those who meet legal and business operation criteria), and on making and
receiving payments to and from third parties which, having met the foregoing requirement, are made from a bank account
opened in such markets.
The Group has minimum standards that must be accepted by all its product suppliers: the Inditex Minimum Requirements
(IMRs). Among the IMRs are the Codes of Conduct, the Sustainability Policy, the Human Rights Policy, the Traceability
Requirements and the Green to Wear standard. By implementing the IMRs, Inditex guarantees that all the product suppliers
with whom it works agree to comply with certain social, environmental and product health and safety standards, among
others. Furthermore, since 2022, non-product suppliers in Spain have to accept a Declaration of Compliance with Minimum
Conditions prior to being registered as Group suppliers. The aforementioned requirements are applied continuously and
are a prerequisite for contracting new suppliers and third parties.
Key Internal Regulations
Our ethical commitments and the action principles that should guide our day-to-day activities and our dealings with all our
teams and the supply chain are set out in our Codes of Conduct and developed upon further in other internal rules. To
promote knowledge of and compliance with these rules, they are published internally and/or externally and training or
awareness-raising initiatives are carried out and adapted to the profile of the people who should know them and apply them
in their work.

Below is a summary of the main internal rules related to our corporate ethical culture and business conduct, which are
available on the intranet (INET), and on the corporate website (www.inditex.com).
Compliance Policy
Approved by the Board of Directors on 13 December 2016
Key contents
/ Compliance with applicable legislation, internal regulations and the ethical commitments undertaken voluntarily by
Inditex
Scope
/ The entire Group (companies and individuals)

303

## Page 304

Ethics Line
Grievance mechanism: the Ethics Line
The Ethics Line (including both the Global Ethics Line and the various Local Ethics Lines) is the preferred, strictly
confidential, internal communication channel available to any employee, director and shareholder of any company of the
Group, as well as anyone working under the supervision and management of manufacturers, suppliers, contractors and
subcontractors of the Inditex Group may raise, including anonymously:
/ Questions and/or doubts about the interpretation or application of the Codes of Conduct or any other internal rules of
conduct within the competence of the Ethics Committee.
/ Infringements and other breaches of the applicable legislation, the Codes of Conduct or of any other internal rule of
conduct within the competence of the Ethics Committee, affecting Inditex and committed by employees, manufacturers,
suppliers or third parties with whom the Group has a direct employment, commercial or professional relationship. Any
breaches or irregularities related to corruption, fraud and bribery can also be reported via this channel.
The Ethics Line is therefore available to all our stakeholders, including in particular our teams, workers in the value chain,
members of the community, and customers themselves.
How the Ethics Line works
1. Concerns: The person concerned reports an incident through the channels provided for this purpose on the
intranet (INET) and on the corporate website (www.inditex.com).
2. Management: The Ethics Committee acknowledges receipt of the report and evaluates whether it falls
within the scope of application of the Ethics Line.
3. Investigation: The Ethics Committee carries out the investigation process in collaboration, where
applicable, with other areas.
4. Measures: Upon completion of the investigation and after granting the affected party the right to be heard,
the Ethics Committee will issue its decision on:
/ The closing of proceedings, where no breach exists; or
/ The existence of a breach, its severity and whether it is appropriate to take disciplinary measures and/or
complementary actions.
In the event of a breach, the measures to be taken will be determined in collaboration with the corresponding
department depending on the breach’s severity and other circumstances, and may consist of:
/ The immediate correction of the breach and the adoption of measures to remedy and prevent future
breaches.
/ Disciplinary measures (ranging from warning or admonishment to dismissal).
The Ethics Committee is the internal, collegiate, and independent body responsible for the Group’s Internal Reporting
System and for managing the Ethics Line and must promote the necessary investigations to adequately resolve cases
handled, in accordance with the Policy on Internal Reporting Channels and the Ethics Line Procedure (approved and
reviewed, respectively, by the Board of Directors on 3 May 2023). Its composition, operation and competencies are
regulated in the Regulations of the Ethics Committee, as amended by the Board of Directors on 3 May 2023. The
decisions of the Ethics Committee are binding upon the Group and upon the person to whom they are addressed.
In addition to the Global Ethics Line, Inditex has Local Ethics Lines in 15 markets in order to comply with the requirements
and/or best practices applicable in those markets41.
7. Governance information / G1. Business conduct
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
304
41 Belgium, Bulgaria, Canada, Croatia, Czech Republic, Finland, Greece, Hungary, Luxembourg, Netherlands, Slovakia, Slovenia, Sweden,
Poland, United States and Puerto Rico.

G1-1_02;
G1-1_13

## Page 305

The Ethics Line is governed by the principles and guarantees established in the Global Policy on Internal Reporting
Channels and by the Ethics Line Procedure. These internal regulations incorporate international best practices in
connection with human rights and adapt the Ethics Line to the requirements of the markets in which the Group operates (in
particular, to the requirements derived from the transposition into Member States’ law of Directive (EU) 2019/1937 on the
Whistleblower Protection Directive, which include, among others, personal data protection and the rights of persons using
whistleblowing mechanisms).
These internal rules offer the following guarantees and protection measures to the parties involved in the process:
/ Utmost confidentiality.
/ Presumption of innocence and preservation of the right to honour of the persons affected by the report.
/ Non-retaliation against any reporter or any person helping the informant during the investigation.
/ Appropriate use of personal data processed.
/ The parties’ right to be heard.
i All the information regarding the Ethics Committee and the Ethics Line is available on our intranet and on our corporate website
(www.inditex.com), under the ‘Ethics Line’ tab.
When a concern is submitted via the Ethics Line, the Ethics Committee analyses its content and determines the appropriate
measures, including opening an investigation where appropriate.
In keeping with best practices, the Group has a tool provided by an external supplier to receive and handle concerns from
the Ethics Line. This tool is accessible 24 hours a day, 7 days a week and is available in 22 languages.
Proof that stakeholders know and trust the Ethics Line is the fact that communications are received from the various
groups, and that these communications have increased during the year, for example in the case of our teams and workers
in the value chain. Furthermore, the monitoring and supervision of the cases via the Ethics Line allows us to work to ensure
their effectiveness.

i More information in section G1-4. Comunications received through the Ethics Line of this Report.
As stated in the Global Policy on Internal Reporting Channels, the Ethics Committee, as the body responsible for the
Group's Internal Reporting System, must lead any investigations that may be necessary and propose, where needed, the
appropriate remediation, prevention and awareness measures. It must carry out its duties independently, impartially and
autonomously and respond for the system's management and diligent processing of concerns received via the Ethics Line
and other Internal Reporting Channels within that System. In accordance with the Regulations of the Ethics Committee,
members of the Ethics Committee must refrain from taking part in the examination, deliberation and/or voting stages on
matters in which they incur a conflict of interest.
The Procedure regulates the deadlines for the processing and investigation of concerns received, to ensure that they are
properly handled. Those deadlines are consistent with applicable regulations.
Pursuant to our Code of Conduct and the Ethics Line Procedure, the Company strictly prohibits any form of retaliation or
threat of retaliation against persons who report any matter in good faith, as well as against persons who cooperate in the
investigation.
The main contents of our Global Policy on Internal Reporting Channels and the Ethics Line Procedure are set out below.

i More information on compliance training in section G1-3. Prevention and detection of corruption and bribery of this
Report.
G1-3. Prevention and detection of corruption and bribery
Corruption and bribery are totally at odds with the way we operate. At Inditex we have zero tolerance for corruption of any
kind and are committed to complying with anti-corruption laws in all markets where we operate. We have Codes of
Conduct and a number of policies that set out principles and guidelines to prevent corruption and bribery: Due Diligence
Policy and Integrity Policies (Donations and Sponsorships, Gifts & Invitations, and Dealings with Public Servants), Conflicts
of Interest Policy, and the Policy on Public Policy.
Furthermore, the Global Compliance Model and, in particular, the Model of Criminal Risk Prevention in Spain and other
models for the prevention of corruption and other crimes in other markets, aim to prevent and manage risks related to the
potential commission of crimes, including corruption, bribery and fraud.
The Company’s Ethics Line is a way to report, among other issues, any unlawful conduct relating to corruption or bribery.

i More information on the Ethics Line in section G1-1. Corporate culture and business conduct of this Report.

## Page 307

The Audit and Compliance Committee is responsible for risk management and business conduct, including supervision of
the Compliance function. At its meetings in February, March, June, September and December 2024, the Committee
received from the Compliance function and the Chief Compliance Officer the reports indicated in section GOV-2.
Information provided to governing bodies and sustainability matters addressed.
Communication and awareness-raising
We provide everyone in the Inditex Group with knowledge of and access to the internal rules that apply to them. The
General Counsel’s Office is responsible for carrying out a series of communication and awareness-raising actions:
/ It informs and updates the members of Inditex’s Board of Directors on a quarterly basis throughout the year, regarding the
compliance matters listed in section GOV-2. Information provided to governing bodies and sustainability matters
addressed. Prior to meetings of the Board of Directors, it makes the policies or other internal rules available to the
directors, for their subsequent approval or amendment.
/ It makes the internal policies and rules applicable to employees available on INET, under the ‘Internal Regulations’ tab, and
communicates them to employees with corporate email.
/ It makes the most significant policies available to all our stakeholders on the corporate website (www.inditex.com), under
the tab ‘Ethical Commitment’.

Moreover, the Group's product suppliers have access to the supplier extranet to consult the IMRs applicable to them.
Notable among all these internal regulations are the Codes of Conduct, which set out the guidelines for preventing
corruption.
Compliance training plan
Training our people and suppliers is key to strengthening our corporate ethical culture and implementing our Global
Compliance Model across all our operations.
Our Code of Conduct sets out the action principles that must guide all our actions (respect, integrity and honesty,
transparency and accountability) and reminds all people in the Group that they are required to complete the mandatory
training when called.
Likewise, our Code of Conduct emphasizes management's commitment ‘Tone at the top’ and includes the fundamental
role of team leaders in the application of the Code and in the promotion of the corporate ethical culture. Therefore, they are
asked to act by example; to promote knowledge of and compliance with the Code; to foster a culture of dialogue and
respect in the management of their teams; and to encourage the communication of doubts or breaches, without fear of
retaliation.
