G1-3_05

G1-3_06;
G1.MDR-A_01-12

## Page 308

The promotion of the corporate ethical culture is supported by the communication and awareness-raising actions
described above, and by a Compliance Training Plan (hereinafter, ‘Training Plan’), with training and awareness-raising
measures adapted to the risk profile of the Group’s employees.
Global campaign on the Code of Conduct
   In 2024, the Training Plan included a global campaign on the Code of Conduct (updated in February 2024)
in order to familiarise everyone in the Group with the culture of ethics and compliance. Corporate tools have
been used to ensure regular monitoring and adequate traceability of communication, awareness-raising and
training actions:
/ Communication and awareness-raising:
• The Code is available in 34 languages on INET to everyone in the Group.
• A global news article was launched on INET, which received the most likes during the year.
• An information page on the Code was published in the ‘Ethical and responsible culture’ section of INET,
which received 61,043 visits, and the information space on INET dedicated to Compliance was updated, with
65,466 visits.
• A launch video on the Code was shown and informative posters on the Code and the Ethics Line were
published in all stores and work centres.
• In the ‘Ethical Commitment’ section of our corporate website (www.inditex.com), the Code of Conduct was
published for all our stakeholders and received 31,011 visits.
/ Acceptance:
• To obtain acceptance of the Code of Conduct by everyone in the Group, a pop-up message was activated
on INET with access to the Code of Conduct, so that it could be read and accepted. Thanks to this
campaign, the Code has been accepted by 97% of employees.
/ Training for all our people:
• A mandatory e-learning course on the Code of Conduct was published in Tra!n, with an accessible version
for people with disabilities. This training covers the Ethics Line and its main protections, the prevention of
corruption and bribery, and other topics, and has already been completed by 91% of employees.
/ Specific training for supervisors and teams in the People department:
• In addition, training was given to supervisors, the People department and Compliance delegates from
headquarters, retail concepts and key subsidiaries, due to their fundamental role in promoting the corporate
ethical culture and their greater exposure to certain compliance risks. The training was designed and
adapted to the particularities of these groups, emphasising the sections of the Code of Conduct on our
integrity, covering topics such as bribery and corruption prevention, managing gifts and invitations, handling
conflicts of interest and the Ethics Line. Other departments were also invited to these training sessions to
reinforce the messages on harassment prevention, privacy and our programme Respectful. A total of 2,690
people received this specific training (in-person and/or hybrid).
As regards the governing bodies, during the process of drafting the Code of Conduct, a specific meeting of the Board of
Directors was held to receive feedback and contributions from the directors to assist in drafting Code of Conduct.
In February 2024, the Board of Directors approved the final version of the Code of Conduct and received a presentation on
its contents. The members of the Board of Directors received and accepted the Code of Conduct and undertook to comply
with it.
/Compliance Training Framework Plan
This Compliance Training Framework Plan (hereinafter, ‘Framework Plan’), is coordinated by the Compliance department
and organises the training provided by the main corporate areas exposed to compliance risks, providing support in the
creation or adaptation of mandatory training content and in the review of the target groups for such training.
The goal is to ensure a homogeneous and robust oversight of the training provided to mitigate priority compliance risks and
contribute to building a corporate ethical culture by targeting the courses at those employees most exposed to compliance
risks.
7. Governance information / G1. Business conduct

## Page 309

To manage this Plan, we have an exclusive space for mandatory training in Tra!n. Each person has different courses, as this
training has been specifically tailored to their risk profile, position and responsibility. Furthermore, throughout the year, the
training courses completed were monitored, which consists of periodic reminders being sent to employees with mandatory
training courses pending about the need to complete them before the end of the year. To this end, and to facilitate course
completion, a specific monitoring tool was made available to the People department teams.
Lastly, and always with a risk-based approach, the Compliance department has created two new mandatory courses ‘New
Products’ and ‘Control Claims’, aimed at a priority group of commercial, sustainability and other corporate areas affected by
these procedures. The contents of ‘New Products’ deal with the viability of this type of articles (those that have not been
marketed previously by the concept or those already marketed previously that have some differentiating element that implies
the need to reassess whether they are viable for marketing) and to indicate the steps to follow to analyse the marketing
requirements of a new product in the different markets in which it is to be launched. The ‘Claims’ course reviews the basic
concepts of the Control Claims validation flow, indicating the steps to follow to analyse the viability of a claim (marketing
claim) before using it.
In 2024, the following mandatory training courses were published for employees in corporate areas with higher exposure to
compliance risks:
Labour Law
• Global Anti-Harassment Policy in the workplace
Industrial Property
• Responsible Creativity
Information Security
• Cybersecurity: Basic principles of Cybersecurity
• Cybersecurity: Learn about the PCI-DSS standard
• Cybersecurity: Secure Code V2
• Cybersecurity: phishing
Compliance
• Code of Conduct
• Control Claims
• New Products
• Model 231
Indicators on compliance training for the priority group
With regard to the Training Plan, it should be noted that, in the 2024 financial year, 86% of the priority group of our people
has received training (in-person or online) on the subject of Compliance. This training has been aimed at employees who,
either on account of their position and responsibilities or because of the type of activity they carry out, are exposed to a
greater risk of compliance breaches and, in particular, the commission of offences relating to corruption between
individuals. The compliance training metrics linked to the priority group of employees are presented below:

With regard to the Board of Directors, 100% of its members have received compliance and/or corruption or bribery training.

from them within the framework of our commercial or professional relationship.
G1-4. Communications received through the Ethics Line
Communications to the Ethics Line (including both the Global Ethics Line and the various Local Ethics Lines) are recorded
in a tool through which they can be tracked. Periodically, the Compliance department reports to the Audit and Compliance
Committee on the activity of the Ethics Line and the development of the cases submitted through it. Thus, the concerns
received through the Ethics Line are monitored and controlled and their effectiveness is guaranteed.
During 2024, the Ethics Line recorded a total of 791 concerns (515 concerns in 2023).

G1-4_04;
G1-4_06

## Page 311

Of the total of 791 concerns received by the Ethics Committee 2024, 556 are closed. Of these, 313 were classified as being
beyond the authority of the Ethics Committee and 63 as not requiring any further action or monitoring by the Ethics
Committee. Of the remaining closed cases: (i) 64 were queries, (ii) 75 were cases which, after investigation, were found not
to be non-compliances, and (iii) the remaining 41 were cases of non-compliance requiring appropriate action.
Of the latter, four cases were related to the prevention of corruption and bribery, 34 were linked to diversity and respect in
the workplace and the remaining  three were related to other violations of the Inditex Group’s Codes of Conduct. In three
cases, the non-compliances detected affected employees of suppliers of goods and services, and the necessary measures
to remedy the situation were taken. In all cases where a breach is confirmed, appropriate remedial measures will be
adopted depending on the nature of the breach detected and its severity. These measures consisted, depending on the
case, of disciplinary measures (either termination of employment or warning), training and review of internal procedures.
With regard to the confirmed reports of corruption, no relevant aspects affecting the Company have been observed in any
of them. Disciplinary measures or termination of employment due to confirmed reports of corruption are detailed in the
table above.
At the same time, during 2024 and 2023, the Group has not been aware, either through its Ethics Committee or through
other means, of the processing of legal proceedings concerning corruption or bribery that affect the Company.
Consequently, in 2024, the Inditex Group neither received through the channels available for this purpose any significant
fines or non-monetary penalties for non-compliance with the legislation or regulations applicable to it in matters of
corruption or bribery.
G1-5. Political influence
In 2024 we developed and approved our Policy on Public Policy:
Policy on Public Policy
Approved by the Board of Directors on 10 December 2024
Key contents
/ Commitment to a transparent and responsible development of the preparation of public policies that affect our
business activities, our stakeholders and society at large
/ Development of the principles of action set out in the Group’s Codes of Conduct and the Integrity Policies
/ General principles: responsibility, integrity and honesty, transparency and respect
Scope
/ The entire Group (companies and individuals)
/ People who provide services or collaborate with the Group (including the Board of Directors)
Accountable for
the
implementation
/ Public Policy department and its chief officer
Third-party
standards
/ OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises on Responsible Business Conduct
/ United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)
/ OECD Principles on Public Policy and Responsible Corporate Political Engagement (currently under development)
Like our Policy on Public Policy, Inditex’s Policy on Donations and Sponsorships expressly prohibits making donations to
political parties, either directly or indirectly. This prohibition is included in the Company’s Code of Conduct, which adds that,
as a general rule, no person from Inditex can offer, grant, solicit or accept, either directly or indirectly, gifts or invitations, of
any kind and from any authorities or public officials.
In accordance with these policies, no contributions were made (in cash and/or in kind) to political parties during the year.

## Page 312

With respect to our governing bodies, no members of the Board of Directors have held a comparable position in
government administrations in the two years prior to their appointment. Furthermore, our Conflicts of Interest Policy states
that if the recruitment of any person by the Group, especially persons coming from public authorities, may give rise to a
conflict of interest, the Ethics Committee may propose that suitable measures be agreed to safeguard the Group’s interests,
in accordance with our Codes of Conduct and internal regulations.
Public policy activities
Our public policy activities focus primarily on four main topics: Sustainability, Trade, Digital and Finance.
To implement these activities, the Inditex Group is a member of the European Union Transparency Register, and its
registration number is 518095443081-20.
B. Suppliers relations
G1-2. Management of relationships with suppliers
Our business model is based on an agile and flexible supply chain. This enables us to meet demand and adapt to new
social and environmental standards quickly and efficiently.
Proximity is one of the key criteria of our supply chain, allowing us to respond nimbly to the market. A very significant
portion of the cutting, sewing, dyeing, washing, printing or finishing factories that manufactured our garments in 2024 are
located in Spain or neighbouring countries like Portugal, Morocco and Türkiye.
Overall, as of 2024 we had 6,615 factories which include all tiers of our supply chain in 50 markets and employed more than
three million people (6,710 factories, 49 markets and more than three million people in 2023).
The supply chain at Inditex in 2024
Distribution by geography

A fundamental tool for managing our supply chain are our supplier clusters, spaces to foster dialogue and cooperation with
industry actors. In 2024 we worked with ten clusters in our main production markets: Spain, Portugal, Morocco, Türkiye,
India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, Vietnam, China y Cambodia.
Main policies, standards and principles on which our supply chain management is based
Our Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers (CCMS) is the foundation on which we build our relationships with
suppliers and manufacturers in our supply chain. It includes all the standards they are obliged to meet with regard to labour
rights, product health and safety and environmental issues.
7. Governance information / G1. Business conduct

## Page 313

i More information in section MDR-P. Policies of this Report.
i More information in the Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers available on Inditex’s corporate website.
In addition to our CCMS, at Inditex we implement our own Green to Wear standard, aimed at reducing the environmental
impact of our supply chain, as well as improving the health and safety of our articles and fostering best practices.
i More information in section Own standards and certifications of this Report.
i More information in the document Green to Wear available on Inditex’s corporate website.
Another important tool for supply chain management are our Traceability Requirements for Manufacturers and
Suppliers, which increase the level of detail of the minimum requirements for our suppliers reflected in the CCMS.
In conclusion, any manufacturer or supplier wishing to form part of our supply chain must comply with the Code of Conduct
for Manufacturers and Suppliers, with the Green to Wear standard, if applicable, and with our Traceability Requirements. To
verify this compliance, we conduct regular and periodic controls to ensure that our standards are upheld. The breach of any
of them may entail the termination of the business relationship.
In addition, internally we promote responsible purchasing practices, guiding the decisions of our buyers and product
teams with the aim of improving working conditions in the supply chain and fostering sustainable development in the textile
sector. In this connection, our involvement in the ACT initiative (Action, Collaboration, Transformation) is pivotal.
i More information in section S2. Workers in the value chain of this Report.
i More information in the report Supply chain: management to transform the sector, is available on Inditex’s corporate website.
Traceability of the supply chain
We see traceability as our ability to identify and trace the history, application, location and distribution of products, parts
and materials. This definition is consistent with Recommendation No. 46 of the United Nations Economic Commission
for Europe, which advocates enhancing traceability and transparency of sustainable value chains in the garment and
footwear sector.
Based on our Traceability Requirements, over the course of 2024 we have added new developments to monitoring, such
as:
– Supporting the implementation of the fibres plan, with emphasis on the traceability of raw materials.
– Improvement of our tools to make it easier for suppliers to provide us with the necessary information.
Thus, our suppliers must know their supply chain and work only with manufacturers and intermediaries that comply with our
sustainability standards and that have been previously assessed and approved by Inditex. They must therefore report which
facilities and intermediaries are involved in each production process, from fibre or yarn to the final garment for each order.
They must also prove the use of lower-impact raw materials42, such as organic or recycled cotton, viscose from preferred
sources or European linen.
To ensure that these requirements are met, our traceability ecosystem allows us to evaluate the traceability of our products.
We also provide training to help our suppliers solve specific challenges regarding the use and understanding of traceability
procedures.
7. Governance information / G1. Business conduct
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
313
42 More information on the criteria that fibres or materials must meet to be classified as lower-impact in the Methodological Annex of this
Report.

## Page 314

Traceability assessment
We use various control mechanisms operating in parallel to verify compliance with our traceability requirements:
/ Designation control check: to verify that our suppliers have provided information on their supply chain by the established
deadline.
/ Review of certificates: we check the raw materials’ certificates to approve them.
/ Traceability audits: we verify the information provided by our suppliers on site, through unannounced visits to the
production facilities. This allows us to check the production processes, the productions in progress and the ones
completed. The findings are then compared with the information entered by the supplier. In 2024, 10,387 traceability audits
were carried out (12,100 audits in 2023).
Monitoring, assessment and continuous improvement
Our aim is to build strong, long-standing relationships with our suppliers. Consequently, in 2024, 10,923 supply chain
manufacturer audits were carried out by our own teams and external auditors, specialised by sphere of audit and trained in
accordance with the Inditex methodology. They conducted pre-assessments, environmental preliminary assessments, and
social and environmental audits.
/ Pre-assessment
These audits are carried out before the factory can receive any order from Inditex. These audits check that they comply with
our Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers (CCMS) and the IMR (Inditex Minimum Requirements) applicable to
them. Without this first step, no facility can join our supply chain.
The IMR are requirements set out in several key documents, such as the Human Rights Policy and the Traceability
Requirements for Suppliers and Manufacturers, which establish the social, environmental or product health and safety
standards our manufacturers must meet to become eligible to receive orders.
In 2024, 2,175 pre-assessment audits were carried out (2,111 in 2023).
/ Environmental pre-assessment
Once the new factory passes the pre-assessment audit, a preliminary environmental assessment is conducted remotely by
external auditors. This ensures that facilities subject to our Green to Wear standard comply with our most demanding
environmental requirements. Facilities classified as not approved at this stage cannot receive orders from Inditex.
In 2024, 251 environmental pre-assessments were executed (301 in 2023).
/ Social audits
After the pre-assessment audit, we conduct social audits on a regular basis to all factories in our supply chain.  These
audits serve to verify compliance with our CCMS and consist of a site visit (including a document review, verification of
facilities and interviews with workers and company management) to verify wages, working hours, health and safety
conditions, among other requirements of our Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers.
Social audits may be carried out by internal or external auditors in accordance with the Inditex methodology. To ensure
their quality, we carry out control audits to verify that our methodology is being properly implemented. In 2024, 6,468 social
audits were carried out at the factories in our supply chain (6,892 in 2023)43.
In addition, social audits using our own methodology have also been carried out in more than 60 external logistics centres
this year.
As a result of the social audits, the manufacturers are classified in a social ranking based on their degree of compliance
with the Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers (CCMS). In 2024, the social ranking of the active manufacturers
in our supply chain was as follows:

(SLCP) methodology integrated in our systems.

/ Environmental audits
Compliance with our Green to Wear standard, targeting facilities that mainly carry out wet processes, is regularly verified
through environmental audits. In 2024, 2,029 environmental audits were carried out (1,868 in 2023).
These audits are conducted by external auditors in announced visits during which documentation is reviewed and, where
applicable, waste water samples are analysed unannounced. The results are shared with the rest of the sector within the
framework of our collaboration with Zero Discharge of Hazardous Chemicals (ZDHC).
As a result of these audits, 85% of the manufacturers required to comply with the Green to Wear standard (80% in 2023) are
rated as A or B, the highest environmental performance ratings in our methodology.
7. Governance information / G1. Business conduct
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
315

## Page 316

/ Continuous improvement
We know that continuous improvement is key to sustainable supply chain management. Accordingly, we use Corrective
Action Plans (CAPs), which are roadmaps to support manufacturers in their improvement processes and help them to
correct and prevent non-compliances. These plans may be carried out in collaboration with other organisations, such as
NGOs and trade unions. In the most sensitive non-compliance cases —classified as being ‘subject to CAP’ according to
social or environmental standards— the plan lasts approximately six months.
In 2024, the following CAPs were carried out:
• Social CAP: we carried out 306 CAPs, of which 223 at factories with a ‘Subject to CAP - D’ rating and 83 at production
centres with other ratings (490 CAPs in 2023: 285 in factories with a ‘Subject to CAP - D’ rating, and 205 in sites with other
ratings).
• Environmental CAPs: we launched 353 CAPs. 91 of these plans were carried out in factories with a ‘Subject to CAP - D’
rating, and 262 in sites with other ratings (327 CAPs in 2023: 99 in factories with a ‘Subject to CAP - D’ rating, and 228 in
sites with other ratings).
Despite the importance for Inditex of accompanying the manufacturers of our supply chain in improving their social and
environmental performance, our commitment to compliance with our standards entails a zero tolerance policy with those
who do not show a willingness to improve. Consequently, if the verification audit still reveals critical non-compliance once
the plan has been completed, the factory will not be able to continue working for with our Company.
/ Other continuous improvement tools
In addition to the CAPs, our Company implements various improvement and support projects for manufacturers, such as
those developed as part of the Workers at the Centre strategy. In 2024 we also continued with our improvement plans, as
part of our Supply Chain Environmental Transformation Plan, with a particular emphasis on water, discharges, management
of chemical products and energy.
i More information in sections S2. Workers in the value chain. and E1-3. Actions related to climate change of this Report.
i More information in the report Supply chain: management to transform the sector, available on Inditex’s corporate website.
Mutual respect and trust are the basis for Inditex’s relationships with its suppliers. The Group has devised payment
procedures that indicate the importance of complying with legal payment deadlines for the proper functioning of the value
chain, based on long-term relationships and responsible financial management.
These procedures are mandatory for all the Group’s companies in connection with managing payments with all kinds of
suppliers, including suppliers classified as SMEs. They define the payment periods, as well as the payment means.
i More information in section G1-6. Payment practices of this Report.
G1-6. Payment practices
With regard to payment practices, the most common method of payment to our suppliers is by bank transfer, and among
the measures that the Group offers its suppliers are various financing programmes.
In addition, specifically focusing on the suppliers across our supply chain, we belong to ACT (Action, Collaboration,
Transformation), through which we undertake five specific commitments in connection with responsible purchasing
practices, that include agreeing and respecting fair payment conditions and, therefore, respecting the established payment
periods.
The responsible management of our suppliers is paramount for the Group. We have therefore developed a third-party
payment procedure to underpin our commitment to meet legal and agreed payment deadlines, in order to ensure that our
value chain activities function optimally.
7. Governance information / G1. Business conduct

## Page 318

Additional information. Cybersecurity
During 2024, cyber threats have increased in number and complexity, due to technological advances and the exponential
growth of data and connected devices, leading organisations to take more sophisticated and proactive measures to protect
their assets.
Emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence and machine learning, along with cloud computing, have transformed
both the landscape of cyberattacks and the defence strategies. On the one hand this allows malicious actors to find new
ways to achieve their goals, and on the other it enables companies to more accurately anticipate risks and implement
controls.
In this new context, cybersecurity is no longer seen as a simple protective measure, but as a central pillar for the resilience
of a business and customer trust, and has become a key factor in the competitiveness of organisations.
Governance and strategy
In recognition of the risks inherent to our activity and the need to protect our stakeholders (customers, shareholders,
employees, investors, suppliers, partners, etc.), we are committed to continuous improvement in security management
within a framework designed to guarantee information confidentiality and integrity, as well as the availability of all the
processes that underpin Inditex's retail and distribution channels.
Senior Management’s firm backing in cybersecurity matters allows us to continue making the necessary investments to
minimise the risks associated with cyberthreats. In 2024 we ramped up our investment in cybersecurity by 7% with respect
to the previous year, implying a cumulative increase of 27% over the last three years. This investment has helped
consolidate and strengthen our plan for the continuous improvement of our cybersecurity capabilities and promote new
key initiatives to achieve our strategic goals.
In response to the current context and external threats, and for the purpose of continuing to drive continuous improvement
with respect to unpredictable risks, we have developed our Next-Gen strategic cybersecurity plan, first devised in 2020.
This plan is aimed at ensuring a comprehensive and proactive approach to protection in our Company for the next two
years to enable us to anticipate potential threats, optimise the use of resources and guarantee business continuity in the
event of an incident. At the same time, we have launched a project to review and update our cybersecurity risk
management methodology, with a view to improving the identification, assessment and mitigation of risks that could affect
Inditex, thereby strengthening our strategic decision making.
The Information Security Policy, available on Inditex corporate website, is a key document that, along with the
accompanying regulations and procedures, establishes the core principles to ensure that our people act in keeping with the
information security goals and standards. The Information Security department is the area in charge of achieving this and
the Information Security Committee, comprising members of Senior Management, is the supervisory body that ensures that
best practices in security management, applicable regulations and ethical values are applied throughout the Company, as
provided in the Regulations of the Information Security Committee.
i More information on the Information Security Policy in section S4-1. Policies related to consumers of this Report.
Since 2022, our Chief Information Security Officer Charter has defined the framework of action and competencies of this
function, regulating its place in the Company, levels of organisational autonomy and independence (reporting to the CEO),
responsibilities and reporting lines:
/ The Chief Information Security Officer reports at least twice yearly to the Inditex Board of Directors’ Audit and Compliance
Committee, and, where necessary, the relevant governing bodies of Inditex. The Chief Information Security Officer also
reports at least every quarter to the Information Security Committee regarding the main risks and aspects linked to
cybersecurity at Inditex.
7. Governance information / Cybersecurity
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
318

## Page 319

/ The Cybersecurity Advisory Committee, a consultative and advisory body made up of independent experts, advised us on
a number of topics at the two meetings held in 2024. At these meetings, we analysed:
• the risks arising from the current geopolitical landscape, which pose a constant challenge to information security,
requiring proactive vigilance and that may impact on the Company's information security,
• threats and opportunities related to the use of artificial intelligence, highlighting the importance of adopting this
technology responsibly and being aware of the potential challenges it may present,
• the importance of not overlooking the basics of cybersecurity in favour of more common threats,
• the legal responsibility of the Chief Information Security Officer,
• emerging cybersecurity threats, including new intrusion techniques or vulnerabilities that require defences to be
constantly evolved,
• effective training and awareness strategies to build a solid cybersecurity culture,
• cybersecurity reporting methods that strengthen incident management and improve decision making in
cybersecurity,
• information classification and protection strategies, examining different models and the impact of each in terms of
user experience,
• matching cybersecurity to the particular characteristics of each business sector, highlighting the unique challenges
and specific solutions,
• the various standards and regulations in place and in the pipeline, and the importance of anticipating legal
requirements so as to guarantee their compliance-
Members of the Board of Directors are informed about the discussions of meetings of the Cybersecurity Advisory
Committee by the Audit and Compliance Committee, in accordance with the established procedures. The meeting held on
12 November 2024 was attended by two members of the Board of Directors. These meetings are vital to strengthening the
decision-making process in connection with cybersecurity and to furthering the Company's strategy on this front.
Compliance
Our commitment to cybersecurity includes a robust strategy, Senior Management’s backing and strict compliance with
international best practices and regulations. We continue to closely track cybersecurity regulations, and in 2024 we
conducted an impact analysis of the transposition of the new Directive (EU) 2022/2555 Network and Information Security
(NIS2) by the EU member states that have already implemented it. This directive aims to improve the security of networks
and information systems in European territory. We have also assessed the implications of DORA Regulations (the Digital
Operational Resilience Act) for Inditex, which aims to strengthen operating resilience and cybersecurity in the financial
sector.
In 2024, we proactively assessed our position with respect to the Good Governance Code on Cybersecurity, drawn up by
the National Cybersecurity Forum in collaboration with the Spanish National Securities Market Commission (CNMV). During
this process, we identified complete or partial alignment with the recommendations set out in the Code and devised an
improvement plan to increase our adherence to its guidelines, evidencing our commitment to implementing best practices
in the area of cybersecurity.
As in previous years, we conducted various external assessments, both mandatory and voluntary, to analyse our alignment
with local and international cybersecurity best practices and standards. As a result, we have successfully renewed all major
information security certifications.
/ Payment Card Industry-Data Security Standard 4.0 (PCI-DSS) on the protection of our customers' payment card data.
/ ISO/IEC 27001:2022, which evaluates our Information Security Management System.
/ Korean Information Security Management System (K-ISMS), which evidences our adherence to legal cybersecurity
requirements in South Korea.
/ Multi-Layer Protection Scheme (MLPS), which regulates cybersecurity issues in China.
We also continue to work with the Data Protection and Privacy department to comply with the legal obligations pursuant to
the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).
7. Governance information / Cybersecurity
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
319

## Page 320

In 2024, we also carried out numerous internal reviews, both by third parties and by the Oversight area, to ensure
compliance and proper application of the policies and procedures. We periodically evaluate our level of cybersecurity
maturity, comparing our results with those of companies in other sectors subject to exacting standards, such as firms in the
banking or tech industries.
Furthermore, in 2024 we developed the initiative aimed at obtaining greater visibility, including prior to contracting,
concerning the security standards of our relevant partners, to reduce the risks that might affect them. In addition, we
continue to pay attention to security in our supply chain, having a team dedicated to carrying out reviews and implementing
controls to verify the level of security and guarantees in the service provided by third parties.
Detection and response
The detection and response teams have focused their efforts on designing and implementing new initiatives, and evolving
existing ones, with particular attention to vulnerability management and high-risk assets. In 2024 we improved our process
of classifying vulnerabilities based on their risk level. As a result, we have managed to more efficiently prioritise their
resolution, ensuring a more agile and effective response with respect to the most critical vulnerabilities.
Over the course of 2024, we have strengthened our defence capabilities through specific initiatives focused on optimising
detection and response to the most prevalent threats in the external cyber environment such as Distributed Denial of
Service (DDoS) attacks, Credential Stuffing and vulnerabilities in third-party products.
We have a specialised cyberintelligence team whose main function is the early detection of the risks and threats we face
by means of continuous monitoring. In 2024 we carried out an analysis of the actors that might pose a threat to the
Company and the techniques, tactics and procedures (TTPs) they might use in malicious activities. This analysis enabled us
to identify the methods used by these actors, assess our degree of readiness in respect of these TTPs and propose
improvements to optimise our detection and response capabilities.
Additionally, we are a member of CSIRT.es (Platform of Spanish Cybersecurity and Incident Management Teams) and TF-
CSIRT (Working Group of Security Incident Response Teams). The purpose of these groups is to share information on
major cybersecurity incidents and improve cooperation and coordination to respond swiftly to any situation that may affect
large companies in Europe and neighbouring regions. Membership of these organisations also allows us to strengthen
relationships with global entities and partners in other regions, addressing cybersecurity challenges on a broader scale.
We own a Security Operations Centre (SOC) available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week in charge of the detection, analysis,
notification and resolution of potential security incidents that may affect the Company. In 2024, a total of 60 events of
interest (123 in 2023) were recorded, the most relevant of which we reported to the Information Security Committee. None
of these events had a material impact on our operations or financial statements.
We remain committed to the automation of security operations, improving the speed, scalability, accuracy and efficiency of
detection and response to cyberthreats. Furthermore, recognising the increasing importance of artificial intelligence, we
have worked with other areas of the Company in an AI governance initiative aimed at developing a framework to ensure
that it is used responsibly and transparently, in line with ethical principles and regulations.
Intrusion prevention
The deployment of prevention measures is one of the most important tasks of our activity, and for this purpose with have
more than 700 external researchers participating in our private vulnerability detection programme, the aim of which is to
find weaknesses in our perimeter. Furthermore, we have a new public rewards programme for the online retail environment,
in collaboration with a community of more than one million active researchers. We also continue to conduct crowdsourced
pentesting which gives us a greater diversity of skills and a broader and more varied perspective regarding potential
weaknesses in our systems.
Also in this field, we have had independent external staff carry out new Red Team activities, focusing on simulating attacks
targeting the Company. We thus aim to try to identify our weaknesses and to improve the Company’s security status.
In 2024 our main priorities are the prevention of leaks and theft of sensitive information, the availability of critical services
(sales and distribution) and the control of information integrity, with an emphasis on financial information. This is
complemented by the improvement of the capabilities of our cyber risk insurance programme, which provides various
coverages and services, including own damage (including loss of profit), liabilities and regulatory procedures, as well as
crisis management services.
7. Governance information / Cybersecurity
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
320

## Page 321

In the area of corporate identity and access management, we continue working to improve oversight and internal processes
with respect to management and granting of permissions, as well as privileged identity governance.
With regard to the availability of critical services, we have carried out various exercises to assess and ensure the recovery of
critical systems so as to gauge and reduce the risks associated with the continuity of our systems. The infrastructure
supporting these services is Tier IV certified, a standard distinguishing data centres that offer the highest level of
performance and reliability, guaranteeing a high degree of availability of our infrastructure.
Training and awareness
We continue to roll out a range of projects aimed at evaluating and enhancing the level of security awareness and
knowledge in the Company. We design specific actions in keeping with the level of risk associated with the various user
groups, according to their profile and function. For example, we have provided cybersecurity training to the Company’s
international managers and the Communication department with regard to handling the communication of cybersecurity
incidents. We have training programmes designed especially for the Board of Directors and we also provide specialised
training for software development professionals in compliance with credit card data security requirements.
Furthermore, to address the ever-changing attack techniques, we have expanded our in-house training catalogue by
introducing new training pills that tackle emerging deepfake and artificial intelligence threats. These courses explore how
attackers are using AI to make their tactics more sophisticated, and therefore more convincing and harder to detect. In
addition, special attention has been paid to educating particularly sensitive groups within the Company, furnishing them
with the tools they need to identify and mitigate these new risks.
As in previous years, we have conducted targeted social engineering exercises, with a particular focus on phishing tactics,
aimed at verifying and reinforcing our staff awareness of this type of attacks, which are becoming more and more frequent.
In 2024 we implemented individual training programmes to more effectively boost the knowledge of our employees who
have repeatedly lapsed into this kind of exercises. These programmes are designed to help individual employees enhance
their ability to identify threats and improve their responses to future situations.
These initiatives have been crucial in addressing the specific training and awareness needs of the most at-risk groups.
Thanks to these efforts, in 2024 we provided specialised training to nearly 5,000 users, who had the opportunity to hone
their skills and knowledge in key areas, thus contributing to their personal and professional development.
We also promote security by partnering with public and private organisations:
/ We work with the NGO CyberPeace Institute, which helps vulnerable communities protect themselves and recover from
cyberattacks.
/ We belong to specialised cybersecurity forums such as the Centre for Industrial Cybersecurity (CCI), the Spanish
Association for the Advancement of Information Security (ISMS Forum) and the Executive Action Forum, promoted by the
RSA Conference.
/ We take part in events hosted by the Retail & Hospitality Information Sharing and Analysis Center (RH-ISAC) for
cybersecurity information sharing.
/ We attend renowned international cybersecurity events, such as the Security50 summits.
/ We harness professional research, guidance and advisory services from expert analysts who provide real-time support on
long-term strategic needs and our short-term tactical requisites.
These collaborations enable us to keep up with the latest innovations and trends in cybersecurity, giving us an advanced
perspective on developments in this area.
In summary, at Inditex we continue to devote resources to strengthening cybersecurity, as it is vital to protecting the
Company’s critical assets, complying with regulations, maintaining business continuity, not to mention preserving the
reputation and maintaining the trust of our customers. All this fuels our ambition to stay competitive in an increasingly digital
business environment. Nevertheless, we continue to strive tirelessly to improve our capabilities in this area, as we know
there much remains to be done to tackle the upcoming challenges.
7. Governance information / Cybersecurity
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
321

## Page 322

Additional information. Responsible risk
management
Risk management and control framework
Integrated Risk Management System
Inditex’s Integrated Risk Management System (IRMS) establishes the Group’s risk management and control framework. The
IRMS encompasses the entire Group, at both corporate level and in the various business units and subsidiaries, regardless
of their geographic location. It is incorporated in our strategic planning process, in the definition of strategic goals, as well as
in the Group’s day-to-day operations. The IRMS entails both financial and non-financial risks (including tax, operational,
technological, cybersecurity, legal/regulatory, social, environmental, climate change, political, reputational, corruption-
related and other risks). We consider risk to be any potential event, regardless of its nature, that could adversely affect the
achievement of business objectives.
The IRMS is based on the Enterprise Risk Management Policy and is developed and supplemented by internal regulations
of different levels that govern the management of different risks and apply to different units or areas of the Group. This
system coexists with other functions tasked with monitoring specific risk areas. Our IRMS is based on the COSO ERM,
Enterprise Risk Management44 methodological framework and the relevant ISO standards, adapted to our own needs and
specific characteristics. The IRMS is based on scenario methodologies to assess and quantify the Group’s critical risks.
Enterprise Risk Management Policy
Its purpose is to provide reasonable assurance in regard to the achievement of the strategic goals set by
the Group in response to the various challenges it faces, providing all stakeholders with an adequate level of
assurance to ensure the protection of the value generated. It was last modified on 10 December 2024.
i More information on our policies in section MDR-P. Policies of this Report or on Inditex’s corporate website.
Bodies responsible for preparing and implementing the Risk Management System
In parallel, the IRMS ensures the proper segregation of duties between the various elements that comprise it. The
responsibilities of the areas and bodies involved in the IRMS are based on the three-lines-of-defence model outlined below:
7. Governance information / Responsible risk management

Three lines of defence
1. Business units  (continuous reporting)
Responsibility for managing and reporting the risks to which the Group is exposed in its various areas,
including those related to climate.
2. Risk management / Compliance function  (quarterly reporting)
Tasked with: coordinating and updating the Integrated Risk Management System to maintain maximum
quality standards; coordinating compliance risk management (detailed in section G1-1. Corporate culture and
business conduct).
3. Internal Audit (quarterly reporting)
Independently and objectively supervising the Integrated Risk Management System.
Risk map
The risk map represents the inventory of critical risks for the Group, distributed by risk class and evaluated through a
scenario methodology. There are also maps relating to specific risk categories with a higher level of granularity.

SOCIAL RISKS
Risks arising from socio-economic trends, including evolving societal preferences, social norms and demographics, as well
as the prevalence of diseases and the development of public healthcare systems.
Description and impact
Main mitigations
Human capital
Risk trend à
Risks connected to talent and people management
pertain to the necessity to adapt our organisational
culture to the needs of employees in a new and complex
environment, where the sustainability of human capital
becomes more relevant and which seeks to ensure the
quality of employment, the health and well-being of our
teams, work-life balance, and diversity, among other
factors.
Today’s labour market is becoming increasingly
demanding in terms of corporate social responsibility,
which determines a company’s appeal as a preferred
employment destination. Accordingly, the content and
impact of human capital risks are evaluated every year.
Furthermore, we are more exposed to the potential risk
resulting from the shutdown of critical operational
processes (logistics activity, transport, administrative
services, among others) as a consequence of labour
disputes, strike action, riots or protests that curb or
disrupt corporate productivity.
/ Knowledge transfer and the involvement of all our people in our culture and way of
operating.
/ Employees development, growth opportunities for the most talented people and
retention of our key employees through career development, training and
compensation policies.
/ Recruitment of new employees to ensure a continuous inflow of talent.
/ Measures to develop the Diversity and Inclusion Policy.
/ Development of equality plans, establishing measures to promote the commitment
to and effective application of equality between men and women, preventing
discrimination in the workplace, guaranteeing a healthy working environment and
helping to maintain a work-life balance.
/ Implementation of community programmes and projects.
/ Fostering social dialogue at all levels.
i More information in section S1. Own workforce of this Report.
Infectious and contagious diseases
Risk trend â
This category includes the risks posed by infectious
and contagious diseases. It corresponds to the
potential disruption caused by a local, regional or
global pandemic as a result of infectious diseases
against which there is little or no pre-existing immunity
in the human population. As Covid-19 has taught us,
the impacts of a pandemic can be multiple,
unpredictable and of varying intensity, both in time and
scope. Due to their ramifications, pandemics can
become a systemic risk.
/ Establishment of an information system to ensure better knowledge of the guidelines
issued by public authorities and bodies.
/ Mechanisms aimed at guaranteeing the continuity of our operations.
/ Flexibility of the business model and strengthening of the online channel.
/ Maintenance and updating of measures established by the health authorities:
creating emergency management committees, compiling data on the
epidemiological situation, regular information to our teams, installing protective
screens in workplaces, adapting capacity, etc.
i More information in section S1-4. Actions related to own workforce of this Report.
Brand perception

Risks which have a direct influence on the perception
of stakeholders (customers, employees, shareholders,
and suppliers) and society in general regarding our
Group.

including Communications and Institutional Relations.
/ Establishment of the necessary procedures and protocols by the Communications
and Institutional Relations.
/ Management of the relationship with regulatory bodies by the General Counsel’s
Office – Compliance. Investor and Analyst Relations Management by the
Shareholders Relations Department.
/ Code of Conduct.
/ Code of Conduct for Manufacturers and Suppliers.
/ Policy on Official Internet and Social Networks Accounts and Profiles.

FINANCIAL RISKS
Threats originating in the macroeconomy, in global value chains and in industry- or company-specific events that may
prevent the proposed objectives from being achieved.
Description and impact
Main mitigations
Competition
Risk trend à
The competitive environment may result in risks from difficulties in
adapting to market conditions in which we operate, either in the
procurement processes or in the distribution and sale of our
products. These risks are inherent to the fashion retailing business
and consist of our Group's potential inability to follow and respond
to changes in the target market or to adapt to new situations in its
sourcing or distribution countries. These risks derive from the
possible difficulties involved in recognising and taking on board the
ongoing changes in fashion trends, and in manufacturing, supplying
and selling new articles that meet customer expectations.
/ Business model through management that seeks to improve the
efficiency and effectiveness of markets, business lines and stores,
rationalising and diversifying the commercial network.
/ Internationalisation and diversification strategy.
/ The Group's multi-brand format based on omni-channelling, through the
full integration of channels and new technologies as alternatives for
communication and sales.
/ Feasibility analysis of each new market, business line or store, plus
subsequent follow-up.
i More information in sections 3. Inditex at a glance and SBM-1. Strategy, business
model and value chain of this Report.
Competition may also emerge in the infringement of industrial
and intellectual property.
/ Existence of an Industrial Property (IP) department to supervise the
use of industrial and intellectual property rights and to protect the
Group's IP assets, as well as of dedicated teams integrated in the
commercial areas.
/ Industrial and intellectual property training.
/ Industrial and Intellectual Property Product Control Policy.
/ Code of Conduct.
à
Counterparty
Risk trend á
The Group is exposed to counterparty risk from our suppliers of
goods and services, especially those that are more strategic for
the continuity of our operations, as well as from our customers
and business partners, which could impact the normal
performance of some of our operations. The Group is also
exposed to the risk that financial counterparties fail to comply
with their obligations in relation to investing our liquidity, credit
policies or other funding and guarantee vehicles, as well as the
derivative instruments arranged to hedge financial risks.
/ Analysis and monitoring of the financial solvency of the most important
third parties for the Group, including legal, technological, operational,
reputational and regulatory compliance aspects, among others. Design,
implementation and monitoring of the proper remediation measures
arising from collaboration between the Third-party Risk Management
Area and the responsible business units.
/ The Group Financial Investment Policy, whose aim is to ensure the safety,
integrity and liquidity of the Company’s financial assets.
/ Financial Risk Management Policy, which determines the caps on
counterparty exposure.
/ Flexibility and diversification of the value chain ensure the resilience and
continuity of our operations in the event of potential disruptions owing to
the behaviour of third parties.
/ Creation of the Third-Party Risk Management area.
i More information in section G1-2. Management of relationships with suppliers of
this Report.

Market crisis
Risk trend à
The euro is the Group’s functional currency. Our international
transactions require the use of numerous currencies, giving rise
to foreign currency exchange risk. Currency exposure manifests
itself in terms of net investment, translation and transaction risks.
We have investments overseas whose assets are exposed to the
foreign currency exchange risk. Consolidating the annual
accounts of our companies in euros, we are exposed to foreign
exchange translation risk resulting from all our entities located
outside the Eurozone.
We are also exposed to the risk arising from the volatility in
currencies other than the euro of payment and collection flows in
relation to the acquisition and provision of goods and services in
both Group and non-Group transactions.
/ Financial Risk Management Policy.
i More information in Note 26. Financial instruments and risk management policy to
the Consolidated Annual Accounts.
Supply and distribution shipping is critical to our business. There
is a risk of paralysis or delay in the movement and customs
clearance of goods as a result of changes in the political/social
situation and stability in markets where goods are produced and
sold, regulatory changes, trade frictions—whether tariff or non-
tariff related, and saturation of logistics infrastructures, among
others.
/ In-house teams specialised in market customs regulations, which
are in permanent contact with customs agencies.
/ Diversification of shipping points and establishment of alternative
routes.
/ Continuous tracking of the product until it arrives at the store.
/ Monitoring of relevant regulations and regulatory changes.

as a result of economic contraction or other macroeconomic
headwinds generated by external factors.
/ Flexible business model based on multi-brand omni-channelling.
/ Territorial diversification through an internationalisation strategy.
i More information in sections 3. Inditex at a glance and SBM-1. Strategy,
business model and value chain of this Report.
Economic variables
Risk trend à
The Group is exposed to the risk of inflation affecting costs linked
to the acquisition of the goods and services necessary to conduct
our business. Notable is the impact of the increase in the price of
the multiple raw materials, (textile and non-textile) consumed
directly and indirectly in the Group’s operations, and in the

particular in connection with the transportation of supplies and
distribution.

/ Flexibility of the manufacturing and procurement model, allowing
production to be adapted to market demand and to possible
changes in the supply market environment.
/ Permanent contact with stores and online teams by our team of
designers, through the Product Management department, helping
them to learn about customer preferences.
/ Selective price adjustments to safeguard our margins.
/ Vertical integration of operations that enables to shorten production
and delivery lead times and to reduce inventory volumes, while
keeping sufficient manoeuvring room to introduce new products
over the course of the season.
/ Monitoring of raw material markets and strategic promotion of
circularity and recycling of raw materials.

GEOPOLITICAL RISKS
Geopolitical risks arise from a deterioration in the political situation, a society's crime levels, changes in the ideology,
leadership and regulation of its authorities, politically motivated conflicts at home or between nation states that threaten
operations or expected prospects.
Description and impact
Main mitigations
Business environment and political instability
Risk trend á
Potential instability in the territories where our supply chain is
located, as well as where products are marketed, poses a
significant risk. Socio-political instability arising from social
uprisings or other causes of political violence, as well as their
potential spread to other countries, may affect our ability to operate
in affected territories, with the ensuing impact on value chains,
sales and expansion, or damage to our facilities.
These circumstances may result in frictions that hinder the normal
movement of goods due to political instability, infrastructure
saturation, or constraints, especially on key routes, which generate
bottlenecks due to supply-demand imbalances that limit access to
transport and/or erode our margins.
/ Value chain with multiple geographic origins providing the flexibility and
adaptation to demand, ensuring alternatives to the eventual need to
switch between different manufacturing markets in case of severe and/or
continuous disruptions.
/ Points of sale diversification.
/ Country risk monitoring and proximity to the local market.
/ Continuous analysis of the evolution of the conflict and its complex
implications, implementing plans to mitigate its impact, especially in
relation to its workforce in the affected markets.
i More information in sections 3. Inditex at a glance, SBM-1. Strategy, business
model and value chain and G1-2. Management of relationships with suppliers of
this Report.
Government and business policies and regulatory framework
Risk trend á
As a result of our extensive direct and indirect geographic
presence, we are exposed to multiple and heterogeneous
legislation in the countries where we operate. Regulatory
changes, which are increasingly frequent and more intense,
especially in our sector, as well as the possibility that local
authorities might adopt different or even divergent interpretations
in various jurisdictions, expose the Group to potential negative
effects of a financial, compliance and/or reputational nature.
This includes risks relating to labour, commercial and consumer
law, industrial and intellectual property law, and personal data
protection and privacy regulations, with a particular emphasis on
tax and customs regulations, as well as risks pertaining to other
legislation.
Expectations of significant regulatory or policy changes that may
create uncertainty for the normal conduct of the business model
and require an effort in financial and operational planning to
ensure proper adaptation.
/ Systematic tracking of the impacts and risks of the emerging regulations
that affect the business model and the proposal of operable solutions.
/ Business model based on a firm commitment to good governance,
transparency and respect, aimed at promoting social and environmental
sustainability, and conveying a corporate ethical culture in the
performance of all our activities.
/ Continuous Compliance training.
/ Existence of an independent body that supervises compliance with the
internal standards of conduct (Ethics Committee) and a mechanism to
report irregularities and non-compliances (Ethics Line).
/ Implementation of the model for the organisation, prevention, detection,
control and management of legal and reputational risks, deriving from
potential breaches.
/ Policy on Donations and Sponsorships, Policy on Gifts and Invitations,
and Policy on Dealings with Public Servants.
/ Tax Policy.
/ Anti-Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing Policy.
/ Continuous assessment of the corporate governance system in order to
verify its degree of compliance with and adaptation to new legislation,
recommendations, standards and best practices in this respect, and to
systematically reinforce good corporate governance practices.
i More information in sections Additional information. Tax responsibility and
transparency and A. Corporate ethical culture and solid compliance architecture
of this Report.

Governance risk includes non-compliance by the Company and, in particular, by its Board of Directors and Senior

and with the commitments that we voluntarily undertake. It also includes risks arising from the tactical and strategic
decisions of the Group's management that may result in the non-achievement of business, functional area or Group
objectives, as well as risks of corruption or damage to the Company's reputation.

ENVIRONMENTAL RISKS
Risks associated with natural disasters, climate change and the interactions resulting from human exploitation of the
environment.
Description and impact
Main mitigations
Climate change
Risk trend à
Our performance is exposed to the potential
impacts of climate change in its various
manifestations of physical risk, whether chronic or
strong, as well as to the risks resulting from the
transition to a low-carbon economy. With regard to
physical risks, eight climate phenomena are taken
into consideration: heatwave, freeze, flash flooding,
river flooding, coastal flooding, ‘extratropical’ and
tropical storms and water stress in river basins
where our own or third-party facilities are located.
/ Decarbonisation is one of the main axes of our Sustainability roadmap, in line with the
goals of the Paris Agreement. We have set very ambitious decarbonisation targets for
our business and for our industry as a whole.
/ Section IRO-1/ SBM-3  Description of the processes to identify and assess material
climate-related impacts, risks and opportunities addresses this risk under the Task
Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) framework, which the Group
joined in June 2020.
i More information in sections E1-6. GHG emissions and IRO-1/SBM-3. Material climate-related
impacts, risks and opportunities of this Report.
Environmental degradation and Scarcity of natural
resources
Risk trend à
There is a risk of producing adverse effects on the
environment through the release of undesirable or
hazardous substances (whether biological or
chemical) throughout our value chain. There is also
a risk that our activities may result in negative
externalities such as the loss of biodiversity,
deforestation, soil degradation, or scarcity of raw
materials, especially those that meet our
sustainability requirements, among others. There is
a need to ensure sufficient and reliable supply of
lower-impact raw materials, known as preferred raw
materials, to meet our sustainability commitments.
/ The Sustainability Policy sets out, among others, the environmental commitments,
which are applied transversally across all its business areas and throughout the supply
chain.
/ Environmental policies: Ecosystems and Biodiversity Policy, Water Policy and Energy
Policy.
/ Commitment to clean energy, lower-impact raw materials and the implementation of
circularity services such as Zara Pre-Owned in key markets.
/ Implementation of waste management systems with strict operating requirements to
allow the repurposing of waste generated at our facilities through its reuse or recycling.
/ Forwarding for reuse and/or recycling of the packaging materials arriving at our
facilities.
/ Research and development of new materials and production processes with a lower
impact through the corporate platform Sustainability Innovation Hub.
/ Collaboration projects with third parties in the field of circularity and innovation (MIT
Spain, Ellen MacArthur Foundation, Global Fashion Agenda, Euratex, etcetera).
/ Application of the Green to Wear standard in the supply chain.
i More information in section 5. Environmental information of this Report.
Extreme weather events
Risk trend à
As a result of natural disasters such as floods, fires,
earthquakes, etc., key business operations and
shipping processes could be halted. These events
could potentially affect our critical infrastructure
/ Management of these risks, including mitigation measures and resilience plans, is
discussed in the section on industrial accident risks. (Technological risks).
/ Section IRO-1 / SBM-3. Material climate-related impacts, risks and opportunities
contains a sub-section concerning the physical climate impact on the Group's value
chain.
7. Governance information / Responsible risk management
Inditex Group Annual Report 2024 / Consolidated Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information
330

## Page 331

TECHNOLOGY RISKS
This includes targeted cyberattacks, collapse of critical infrastructure, industrial accidents with direct or indirect impacts, as
well as the inability to adapt to technological advances.
Description and impact
Main mitigations
Critical infrastructure
Risk trend à
We are more exposed to the
risk arising from various
contingencies (incidents,
sabotage or accidents) that
lead to a halt or operational
inefficiency of IT services or
processes needed to
perform the business activity.
/ Ensuring the availability of the systems by deploying technical contingency plans which, together with
associated technical recovery procedures and their relevant recovery testing, would reduce the
consequences of an incident, breakdown or stoppage.
/ Tier IV certified main data centre, that guarantees maximum reliability and high availability.
/ Availability of certified data centres guaranteeing high availability as well as synchronous data storage in
redundant locations or duplication of equipment and lines.
/ Information Security Procedure: Incident Response Plan, which includes the management of incidents
involving personal data from the standpoint of compliance with personal data protection and privacy
regulations.
i More information in section Additional information. Cybersecurity of this Report.
Cybersecurity
Risk trend à
This refers to exposures that
could compromise the
confidentiality, integrity and/
or availability of our
information, regardless of
whether it is located in our
own systems or those of third
parties; and third parties’
information located in our
systems.
We are aware that
cybersecurity risks evolve
exponentially, unpredictably
and, in some cases, in a very
sophisticated way. Thus,
although information security
is a priority, there is the
possibility of an undetectable
attack which might affect
operations or information
managed by us.
/ Existence of a Chief Information Security Officer (CISO), reporting to the Chief Executive Officer and to an
Information Security Committee.
/ Existence of a Cybersecurity Advisory Committee, an advisory and consultative body composed of
independent experts tasked with strengthening the Group's decision-making in connection with
cybersecurity.
/ Availability of a reference framework (Information Security Policy) that establishes the core principles to
ensure that all employees conduct themselves in keeping with the information security goals and standards.
/ Update of the cybersecurity strategic plan aiming to strengthen the integral protection of our digital assets,
reduce the risks associated with cyberthreats and ensure our operating resilience.
/ Permanent control of the Information Security Management System, ensuring the confidentiality and integrity
of the information, and the availability of the systems and applications that support the sales channels.
/ Existence of a cyber risk insurance programme, which provides various coverages and services, including
own damage (including loss of profit), liabilities and regulatory procedures, as well as crisis management
services.
/ Updating the inventory and information technology and security risk map to establish the necessary mitigation
measures and ensure continuous improvement by the responsible areas.
/ Renewal of certifications—both mandatory and voluntary—to analyse our alignment with best practices and
standards in cybersecurity: Payment Card Industry-Data Security Standard 4.0 (PCI-DSS), ISO/IEC
27001:2022, Korean Information Security Management System (K-ISMS)(1) and Multi-Layer Protection Scheme
(MLPS)(2).
/ Continuous review mechanisms for the prevention, detection and response to cyberattacks. Along with the
existence of the ‘Information Security Incident Response Procedure’ which devises the governance structure,
oversight and guidelines for the detection, assessment, management and elimination of cybersecurity
incidents.
/ Awareness and skill-building through employee cybersecurity training.
i More information in section Additional information. Cybersecurity of this Report.
(1) K-ISMS is South Korea's information security management system. This standard is managed by the Korean Internet and Security Agency (KISA). It was prepared to
evaluate whether enterprises and organisations operate and manage their information security system consistently and securely such that they protect key information
assets from various threats..
(2) MLPS is the multi-layer protection scheme. It is a regulatory classification scheme intended to protect the security of information systems located in China. China’s
Cybersecurity Law requires the network and system components to be protected against disruptions, damage, unauthorised access using a graded scale to prevent
data leakage, manipulation and espionage.
